A **choropleth map** shades administrative regions by a quantitative attribute. The classification scheme critically affects the **visual story** told. KTU examiners frequently test these.

#### (a) Equal Interval Classification
Divides the data range into $k$ equal-width classes.

$$
\text{Class Width} = \frac{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}{k}
$$

**Boundary** $b_i$ of class $i$:

$$
b_i = X_{\min} + (i - 1) \cdot \frac{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}{k}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, k+1
$$

**Pros:** Simple, intuitive. **Cons:** Skewed by outliers.

#### (b) Quantile Classification
Each class contains approximately the same number of observations.

$$
\text{Items per class} = \frac{N}{k}
$$

**Pros:** Equal visual weight to each class. **Cons:** Arbitrary class boundaries may split natural clusters.

#### (c) Natural Breaks (Jenks-Caspall)
Minimizes within-class variance and maximizes between-class variance.

$$
\text{GVF} = \frac{\text{SDAM} - \text{SDCM}}{\text{SDAM}}
$$

Where:
- $\text{GVF}$ = **Goodness of Variance Fit** ($0 \le \text{GVF} \le 1$)
- $\text{SDAM}$ = Sum of Squared Deviations from the Array Mean
- $\text{SDCM}$ = Sum of Squared Deviations from Class Means

**Pros:** Best statistical fit to data structure. **Cons:** Subjective (specific to this dataset).

#### (d) Standard Deviation Classification
Class boundaries are placed at the mean plus/minus standard deviation increments.

$$
b_i = \bar{X} + z_i \cdot \sigma
$$

Where $z_i \in \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$ (for a 5-class scheme) and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation.

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N}(X_j - \bar{X})^2}, \quad \bar{X} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} X_j
$$

**Pros:** Highlights extreme values and outliers. **Cons:** Assumes near-normal distribution.

#### (e) Geometric Interval
Class widths follow a geometric progression. Useful for **highly skewed data**.

$$
b_i = b_{i-1} \cdot r, \quad \text{where } r = \left(\frac{X_{\max}}{X_{\min}}\right)^{1/k}
$$

### 2.4 Color Theory for Data Visualization

Color is encoded using multiple color models. The most common in KTU context:

#### RGB (Additive — Screens)
$$
C = w_R \cdot R + w_G \cdot G + w_B \cdot B, \quad 0 \le R,G,B \le 255
$$

#### HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value)
- **Hue** $H \in [0^\circ, 360^\circ)$: color type
- **Saturation** $S \in [0, 1]$: colorfulness
- **Value** $V \in [0, 1]$: brightness

#### ColorBrewer Schemes (Cynthia Brewer, 2003) — KTU Recommended

| Data Type | Scheme Type | Example Palette |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequential** | Ordered, low → high | Light blue → Dark blue |
| **Diverging** | Midpoint, e.g., zero | Red ← White → Blue |
| **Qualitative** | Categorical, no order | Set of distinct hues |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Rule:** Use **sequential** schemes for ordered quantitative data, **diverging** for data with a meaningful midpoint, and **qualitative** for nominal categories. **Never use rainbow/jet palettes** for quantitative data (proven to be perceptually misleading).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Class width (Equal Interval) | $w = (X_{\max} - X_{\min}) / k$ | Map classes, $\mathbb{R}^+$ |
| Mean | $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{N}\sum X_i$ | Central tendency |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2}$ | Spread |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ | Squared spread |
| Goodness of Variance Fit (GVF) | $\text{GVF} = (\text{SDAM} - \text{SDCM}) / \text{SDAM}$ | $0 \le \text{GVF} \le 1$ |
| Quantile Boundary | $b_i = Q_{(i/k) \cdot 100}$ | Percentile cut |
| Normalized Score (Z-score) | $z = (X - \bar{X}) / \sigma$ | Standardization |
| Luminance (Grayscale) | $L = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | ITU-R BT.601 |
| Weber's Law (Perception) | $\Delta I / I = k$ | Just-Noticeable Difference |
| RGB to Grayscale (Rec. 709) | $L = 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B$ | Modern standard |
| Logarithmic Scale | $y = \log_b(X)$ | Skewed data |
| Map Scale (Representative Fraction) | $\text{RF} = \text{Map Distance} / \text{Ground Distance}$ | Dimensionless |
| Dot Density | $D = N / A$ | Persons per km$^2$ |
| Cartogram Scaling Factor | $s_i = A_i \cdot (T_i / \bar{T})$ | Area-proportional |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of Visual Data Analytics

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Urban Planning** | Population density dashboards, zoning maps |
| **Disaster Management** | Real-time flood/earthquake risk maps |
| **Public Health** | Disease spread choropleths, hotspot analysis |
| **Transportation** | OD flow maps, traffic heat maps |
| **Environmental Science** | Air quality, deforestation tracking |
| **Business Intelligence** | Sales dashboards, market analysis |
| **Defense / Intelligence** | Terrain visualization, situational awareness |
| **Agriculture** | NDVI visualization, precision farming maps |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Number of Classes (Sturges' Rule)

**Problem Statement:** Given a dataset of $N$ observations, derive the recommended number of histogram/choropleth classes.

**Step 1 — Information Theoretic Foundation:**
The number of bits required to encode $N$ observations is bounded by $\log_2 N$. Each class effectively encodes $\log_2 k$ bits.

**Step 2 — Binomial Approximation:**
The probability of falling into class $i$ is $p_i = n_i / N$. The expected count in the $i$-th class, when classes are equiprobable ($p_i = 1/k$), is $N/k$.

**Step 3 — Sturges' Formula Derivation:**
Herbert Sturges (1926) proposed that the optimal number of bins for a near-normal dataset is:

$$
k = 1 + \log_2 N
$$

**Step 4 — Application Test:**

Let $N = 64$ observations. Then:

$$
k = 1 + \log_2 64 = 1 + 6 = 7 \text{ classes}
$$

**Step 5 — Rice Rule (Alternative):**

$$
k_{\text{Rice}} = 2 \cdot N^{1/3}
$$

For $N = 64$:

$$
k_{\text{Rice}} = 2 \cdot 64^{1/3} = 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \text{ classes}
$$

**Step 6 — Doane's Rule (For Skewed Data):**

$$
k_{\text{Doane}} = 1 + \log_2 N + \log_2\left(1 + \frac{\vert \hat{g} \vert}{\sigma_g}\right)
$$

Where $\hat{g}$ is the estimated skewness and $\sigma_g = \sqrt{6(N-2) / ((N+1)(N+3))}$.

**Step 7 — KTU Practical Recommendation:**

$$
k_{\text{optimal}} \in [4, 7] \text{ for cartographic maps}
$$

> Perceptual evidence (Mersey, 1990) shows that map readers' accuracy peaks at **5–6 classes** and degrades sharply beyond **7**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Jenks Natural Breaks (GVF Maximization)

**Problem:** Partition a sorted dataset $X_1 \le X_2 \le \ldots \le X_N$ into $k$ classes to maximize the GVF.

**Step 1 — Define Sum of Squared Deviations from Array Mean (SDAM):**

$$
\text{SDAM} = \sum_{j=1}^{N}(X_j - \bar{X})^2
$$

This is the **total variance** of the dataset, a constant.

**Step 2 — Define Sum of Squared Deviations from Class Means (SDCM):**

$$
\text{SDCM} = \sum_{c=1}^{k}\sum_{j \in C_c}(X_j - \bar{X}_c)^2
$$

Where $C_c$ is the set of indices in class $c$ and $\bar{X}_c$ is the class mean.

**Step 3 — Define Goodness of Variance Fit:**

$$
\text{GVF} = \frac{\text{SDAM} - \text{SDCM}}{\text{SDAM}}
$$

**Step 4 — Optimization Objective:**

$$
\underset{\text{boundaries}}{\text{maximize}} \quad \text{GVF} = 1 - \frac{\text{SDCM}}{\text{SDAM}}
$$

Since SDAM is constant, **minimizing SDCM** is equivalent.

**Step 5 — Class Mean Calculation:**

$$
\bar{X}_c = \frac{1}{n_c}\sum_{j \in C_c} X_j
$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example:**

Let dataset $X = [10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, 70]$ (13 values), $k = 3$.

Compute overall mean:

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{10+15+20+25+30+35+40+45+50+55+60+65+70}{13} = \frac{520}{13} = 40
$$

Compute SDAM:

$$
\text{SDAM} = \sum_{j=1}^{13}(X_j - 40)^2 = 6500
$$

Try class boundaries at $X = 30, 50$ (i.e., classes: $[10\text{–}30]$, $[35\text{–}50]$, $[55\text{–}70]$):

- Class 1 mean: $\bar{X}_1 = 20$, members: $\{10, 15, 20, 25, 30\}$
- Class 2 mean: $\bar{X}_2 = 42.5$, members: $\{35, 40, 45, 50\}$
- Class 3 mean: $\bar{X}_3 = 62.5$, members: $\{55, 60, 65, 70\}$

Compute SDCM:

$$
\text{SDCM}_1 = (10-20)^2 + (15-20)^2 + (20-20)^2 + (25-20)^2 + (30-20)^2 = 250
$$

$$
\text{SDCM}_2 = (35-42.5)^2 + (40-42.5)^2 + (45-42.5)^2 + (50-42.5)^2 = 62.5
$$

$$
\text{SDCM}_3 = (55-62.5)^2 + (60-62.5)^2 + (65-62.5)^2 + (70-62.5)^2 = 62.5
$$

$$
\text{SDCM}_{\text{total}} = 250 + 62.5 + 62.5 = 375
$$

Compute GVF:

$$
\text{GVF} = \frac{6500 - 375}{6500} = \frac{6125}{6500} \approx 0.9423
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** A GVF of $0.9423$ means the classification explains $94.23\%$ of the data's variance — an excellent fit. Higher GVF $\Rightarrow$ better classification.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Color Luminance from RGB

**Problem:** Convert an RGB color triplet to perceived luminance for grayscale conversion or colorblind-safe design.

**Step 1 — Apply Rec. 709 Coefficients (HDTV standard):**

$$
L = 0.2126 \cdot R + 0.7152 \cdot G + 0.0722 \cdot B
$$

**Step 2 — Normalize RGB to [0, 1]:**

$$
R' = R/255, \quad G' = G/255, \quad B' = B/255
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**

Let $(R, G, B) = (50, 150, 250)$.

Normalize:

$$
R' = 50/255 \approx 0.196, \quad G' \approx 0.588, \quad B' \approx 0.980
$$

Compute luminance:

$$
L = 0.2126(0.196) + 0.7152(0.588) + 0.0722(0.980)
$$

$$
L \approx 0.0417 + 0.4206 + 0.0708 \approx 0.5331
$$

**Step 4 — Grayscale Conversion:** Set $R = G = B = 0.5331 \cdot 255 \approx 136$ for an equivalent grayscale.

**Step 5 — Why This Matters:** Pure blue ($B = 255$) appears *much darker* than pure green ($G = 255$) to the human eye. The luminance formula corrects for this perceptual bias — critical for colorblind-safe and grayscale-printable maps.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Visualization Types

| Visualization Type | Data Type | Variables Encoded | Best For | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Choropleth Map** | Quantitative, by region | Color value | Spatial density | Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP) |
| **Heat Map** | Density / intensity | Color gradient | Clustering, hotspot | Can exaggerate patterns |
| **Dot Map** | Discrete events | Position + count | Event distribution | Visual clutter |
| **Flow Map (OD)** | Origin-Destination | Line position + thickness | Migration, trade | Complex for many flows |
| **Cartogram** | Quantitative | Distorted area | Emphasizing magnitude | Distorts geography |
| **Bar Chart** | Categorical/Quantitative | Length | Comparison | Limited dimensions |
| **Scatter Plot** | Bivariate Quantitative | Position (x, y) | Correlation | Hides 3rd dimension |
| **Bubble Chart** | Trivariate Quantitative | Position + Size | Three variables | Size judgment error |
| **Histogram** | Univariate | Area (bin count) | Distribution | Sensitive to bin width |
| **Box Plot** | Statistical | Position + Length | Spread & outliers | Requires explanation |
| **Parallel Coordinates** | Multivariate | Position on parallel axes | High-dimensional | Steep learning curve |
| **Scatterplot Matrix** | Multivariate | Pairwise position | Correlations | $O(n^2)$ complexity |
| **Treemap** | Hierarchical Quantitative | Area + Color | Proportions | Poor for comparison |
| **Sankey Diagram** | Flow Quantitative | Width of flow | Energy / money flow | Cluttered with many nodes |
| **3D Surface Plot** | Continuous | Position + Height | Terrain, surfaces | Perspective distortion |
| **Space-Time Cube** | Spatiotemporal | Position (x,y,t) | Movement patterns | Steep learning curve |
| **Small Multiples** | Any (faceted) | Repetition | Comparison across categories | Space-intensive |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Visual Data Analytics — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Spatiotemporal Data] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Data Cleaning and Normalization]
    C --> D[Analytical Engine]
    D --> D1[Statistical Methods]
    D --> D2[Spatial Analysis]
    D --> D3[Machine Learning]
    D1 --> E[Visual Mapping Layer]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Bertin Visual Variables]
    F --> F1[Position]
    F --> F2[Size]
    F --> F3[Color Hue and Value]
    F --> F4[Shape and Texture]
    E --> G[Rendering Engine]
    G --> H[Interactive Dashboard]
    H --> H1[Linked Brushing]
    H --> H2[Filtering and Zooming]
    H --> H3[Real time Panning]
    H --> I[User Cognition and Decision]
    I --> J[Feedback Loop to Data]
    J --> B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Bertin's Seven Visual Variables (Hierarchical View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDV[Visual Data Variables] --> Q[Quantitative Encoding]
    VDV --> QU[Qualitative Encoding]
    VDV --> OR[Ordinal Encoding]
    Q --> P1[Position - Most Accurate]
    Q --> P2[Size - Length or Area]
    Q --> P3[Value - Lightness Gradient]
    QU --> P4[Color Hue - Distinct Categories]
    QU --> P5[Shape - Symbolic Forms]
    QU --> P6[Orientation - Angle]
    OR --> P7[Texture - Pattern Density]
    P1 --> APP1[Best for Point and Line Maps]
    P2 --> APP2[Best for Proportional Symbols]
    P3 --> APP3[Best for Choropleth Maps]
    P4 --> APP4[Best for Land Use Maps]
    P5 --> APP5[Best for Point Features]
    P6 --> APP6[Best for Wind Rose Plots]
    P7 --> APP7[Best for Lithology Maps]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Data Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Step 1 - Examine Data Distribution] --> S1{Is Data Approximately Normal}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{Is Outlier Handling Critical}
    S1 -- No --> S3{Is Data Highly Skewed}
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Use Standard Deviation Classification]
    S2 -- No --> S5[Use Natural Breaks Jenks]
    S3 -- Yes --> S6{Do You Need Smooth Progression}
    S3 -- No --> S7[Use Equal Interval]
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Use Geometric Interval]
    S6 -- No --> S9[Use Quantile]
    S4 --> S10[Verify with Histogram]
    S5 --> S10
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Step 2 - Choose Color Scheme]
    S11 --> S12{Ordered Data}
    S11 --> S13{Diverging Data With Midpoint}
    S11 --> S14{Categorical Data}
    S12 --> S15[Sequential ColorBrewer]
    S13 --> S16[Diverging ColorBrewer]
    S14 --> S17[Qualitative ColorBrewer]
    S15 --> S18[Final Map Output]
    S16 --> S18
    S17 --> S18
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Interactive Dashboard Architecture (Linked Views)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Map View 1 - Choropleth] <--> LC[Linking Controller]
    M2[Map View 2 - Heatmap] <--> LC
    C1[Chart View 1 - Bar Chart] <--> LC
    C2[Chart View 2 - Time Series] <--> LC
    T1[Table View] <--> LC
    LC --> F[Shared Filter and Brush State]
    F --> U[User Interaction Layer]
    U --> M1
    U --> M2
    U --> C1
    U --> C2
    U --> T1
    LC --> Q[Data Query Engine]
    Q --> DB[(Spatiotemporal Database)]
    DB --> Q
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Visual Analytics Workflow (Process Loop)

```mermaid
graph TB
    S1[1. Define Analytical Question] --> S2[2. Acquire and Preprocess Data]
    S2 --> S3[3. Choose Visual Encoding Strategy]
    S3 --> S4[4. Generate Initial Visualization]
    S4 --> S5[5. User Explores and Interacts]
    S5 --> S6{Pattern Detected}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Refine Visual Encoding]
    S7 --> S4
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[6. Formulate Hypothesis]
    S8 --> S9[7. Test With Statistical Model]
    S9 --> S10{Hypothesis Confirmed}
    S10 -- No --> S11[Revise Question]
    S11 --> S1
    S10 -- Yes --> S12[8. Communicate Insight]
    S12 --> S13[Dashboard or Report Output]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Define **Visual Data Analytics**. List any **four advantages** of using visual approaches over tabular data analysis.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Data Analytics (VDA)** is the science of reasoning about data using interactive visual interfaces, integrating automated analysis with human cognitive capabilities to enable effective exploration, pattern discovery, and decision-making.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Rapid Pattern Recognition** — Human visual cortex processes images **~60,000× faster** than text. Pre-attentive features (color, size, position) are detected in $< 200$ ms.
2. **Intuitive Exploration** — Non-experts can interact with maps/charts without statistical training.
3. **Multi-Dimensional Insight** — Several attributes (e.g., population, income, density, time) can be simultaneously encoded.
4. **Hypothesis Generation** — Visual anomalies often prompt new analytical questions that pure statistical analysis misses.

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** List and briefly explain **Bertin's seven visual variables**. State which one is the **most accurate** for representing quantitative data.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Variable | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Position** | Spatial location on a plane |
| 2 | **Size** | Magnitude of an area or length |
| 3 | **Shape** | Geometric form of a mark |
| 4 | **Value** | Lightness/darkness gradation |
| 5 | **Color (Hue)** | Wavelength of the symbol |
| 6 | **Orientation** | Rotation angle |
| 7 | **Texture** | Spacing and pattern density |

**Most Accurate for Quantitative Data:** **Position** is the most accurate and perceptually precise variable for encoding quantitative data because the human visual system is most sensitive to spatial location.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2023, End Semester Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain in detail the **different data classification methods** used in choropleth mapping. Compare their **advantages and limitations** with suitable examples.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Discuss the **ColorBrewer color schemes** for thematic mapping. Explain how the choice of color scheme depends on the **type of data** (sequential, diverging, qualitative).

**Model Solution for (a):**

Data classification is the process of **grouping continuous numerical data into discrete classes** to enable effective visualization on a choropleth map. The choice of classification fundamentally affects the map's interpretation.

**1. Equal Interval Classification**
The data range is divided into $k$ equal-width intervals:

$$
\text{Class Width} = \frac{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}{k}
$$

- **Advantages:** Simple, easily understood; suitable for uniformly distributed data.
- **Limitations:** Highly sensitive to outliers; if one district has a population of 10 million and others between 10,000–50,000, most classes will be empty.
- **Example:** Population data: $[0–100]$, $[100–200]$, $[200–300]$ (with $X_{\max}=300$).

**2. Quantile Classification**
Each class contains an approximately equal number of observations.

- **Advantages:** No empty classes; visually balanced; good for ranking.
- **Limitations:** Arbitrary class boundaries that may split natural clusters; classes can have very different numerical ranges.
- **Example:** 10 districts with populations, each class contains 2–3 districts regardless of value gaps.

**3. Natural Breaks (Jenks-Caspall)**
Maximizes the Goodness of Variance Fit (GVF) by minimizing within-class variance:

$$
\text{GVF} = \frac{\text{SDAM} - \text{SDCM}}{\text{SDAM}}
$$

- **Advantages:** Best statistical fit; reveals natural clusters in data.
- **Limitations:** Computationally intensive; classes are dataset-specific (cannot be reused for new data).
- **Example:** Population density: identifies natural urban-rural divide.

**4. Standard Deviation Classification**
Class boundaries are placed at the mean $\pm k \cdot \sigma$.

- **Advantages:** Highlights statistical outliers; useful for risk and anomaly mapping.
- **Limitations:** Assumes near-normal distribution; meaningless for highly skewed data.
- **Example:** NDVI anomaly detection in precision agriculture.

**5. Geometric Interval**
Class widths follow a geometric progression; suitable for exponential data:

$$
b_i = b_{i-1} \cdot r
$$

- **Advantages:** Excellent for highly skewed data; smooth progression of class widths.
- **Limitations:** Less intuitive; requires careful base choice.
- **Example:** Income distribution, city populations (Pareto-distributed).

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Method | Best Use Case | Sensitivity to Outliers | Computationally Efficient |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equal Interval | Uniform data | High | Yes |
| Quantile | Ranking, balanced display | Medium | Yes |
| Natural Breaks | Clustered data | Low | No |
| Standard Deviation | Normal distributions | Low (intentional) | Yes |
| Geometric Interval | Skewed data | Low | Yes |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**ColorBrewer** (Cynthia Brewer, 2003, colorbrewer2.org) is the de facto standard for selecting cartographic color schemes. It provides **perceptually balanced** palettes designed to be colorblind-safe, print-friendly, and projection-tolerant.

**Scheme Types and Data Mapping:**

**A. Sequential Schemes**
- **Data Type:** Ordered, low → high, no critical midpoint (e.g., population density, elevation, temperature).
- **Visual Logic:** Single-hue gradient from light to dark (e.g., light yellow → dark red).
- **Luminance Monotonicity:** Luminance increases (or decreases) monotonically with data rank.
- **Examples:** "Blues", "Greens", "YlOrRd", "Viridis" (matplotlib default, perceptually uniform).

**B. Diverging Schemes**
- **Data Type:** Ordered data with a meaningful midpoint (e.g., temperature anomaly $\pm 0$ °C, population change $\pm 0\%$, elevation above/below sea level).
- **Visual Logic:** Two-hue gradient that diverges symmetrically from a neutral midpoint (white/light gray).
- **Examples:** "RdBu" (red ↔ white ↔ blue), "BrBG" (brown ↔ white ↔ green), "PiYG".

**C. Qualitative Schemes**
- **Data Type:** Categorical, nominal, no inherent order (e.g., land use types, religion, vegetation classes).
- **Visual Logic:** Set of perceptually distinct hues with **equal luminance** to avoid implying rank.
- **Examples:** "Set1", "Set2", "Accent", "Paired", "Pastel1".

**Critical Rules (KTU High-Yield):**
- Use **viridis** or **plasma** for perceptually uniform sequential mapping (matplotlib/plotly default).
- **Never use the rainbow (jet) palette** for quantitative data — it is perceptually non-uniform and obscures mid-range values.
- For **colorblind safety**: avoid red-green combinations; use **Color Universal Design (CUD)** palettes like viridis or Cividis.
- **Number of classes** must be limited to **5–7** for legibility.

**Real-world Application:** Kerala State Disaster Management Authority (KSDMA) uses ColorBrewer's **diverging "RdYlGn"** (red-yellow-green) for flood-risk zoning, **sequential "YlOrRd"** for rainfall intensity, and **qualitative "Set2"** for district-level categorical land-use classification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the **concept and types of multivariate visualization** in GIS. Discuss **scatterplot matrices, parallel coordinates, and small multiples** with their applications.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Describe the **principles of effective dashboard design** for visual data analytics. List the **"do's and don'ts"** for cartographic and statistical visualization.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Multivariate Visualization** refers to techniques that encode **three or more data variables** simultaneously in a single view. In GIS and visual analytics, this is essential because real-world phenomena are inherently multi-attribute (e.g., population, income, education, age, and location).

**1. Scatterplot Matrix (SPLOM)**
- **Concept:** A grid of $n \times n$ scatterplots for $n$ variables; each cell shows a pairwise plot.
- **Use Case:** Identifying correlations, clusters, and outliers among many variables simultaneously.
- **Example:** 5 environmental variables (NO$_2$, PM$_{2.5}$, O$_3$, SO$_2$, CO) → $5 \times 5 = 25$ scatterplots in one figure.
- **Limitation:** $O(n^2)$ complexity; impractical beyond 8–10 variables.

**2. Parallel Coordinates Plot**
- **Concept:** Each variable is assigned a parallel vertical axis; each observation is a line crossing all axes at its data value.
- **Use Case:** Detecting clusters, anomalies, and trends in high-dimensional data.
- **Example:** Comparing 10 socio-economic indicators across 50 districts; clustered lines indicate similar profiles.
- **Limitation:** Line crossings can cause visual clutter; ordering of axes affects interpretation.

**3. Small Multiples (Trellis/Lattice Plots)**
- **Concept:** The same chart type is repeated for different subsets of data, arranged in a grid.
- **Use Case:** Comparing temporal patterns (e.g., monthly rainfall for 5 years across 4 states = 20 mini-maps).
- **Example:** John Tukey's classic "Mortality by State and Year" displays.
- **Limitation:** Space-intensive; ineffective with too many panels.

**4. Glyphs and Star Plots**
- **Concept:** Each observation is a small symbol whose individual features (rays, sectors) represent variables.
- **Use Case:** Multi-attribute comparison at point locations.
- **Example:** Climate station data with 8 monthly temperatures encoded as 8 spokes.

**5. Brushing and Linking**
- **Concept:** Selection (brush) in one view is highlighted across all linked views.
- **Use Case:** Interactive dashboards exploring multivariate spatiotemporal data.

**Application: Kerala District Profile Dashboard**
A KTU-style dashboard might show:
- **Map view:** Choropleth of 14 districts.
- **Scatterplot:** Literacy vs. HDI.
- **Parallel coordinates:** 10 socio-economic indicators.
- **Small multiples:** Monthly rainfall trend (12 panels).
- All **linked** via shared filter on district name.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Principles of Effective Dashboard Design:**

A **dashboard** is an integrated, interactive visual interface that consolidates multiple visualizations on a single screen for at-a-glance monitoring and exploration.

**Design Principles (based on Tufte, Few, and Stephen Few's Dashboard Design):**

1. **Information Density** — Maximize data-ink ratio; minimize chart junk (per Tufte).
2. **Hierarchy** — Most important metric at top-left (Western reading order).
3. **Alignment** — Align all charts to a common grid; avoid arbitrary placement.
4. **Consistency** — Use the same color, font, and scale across all panels.
5. **Progressive Disclosure** — Show summary first; allow drill-down for detail.
6. **Real-time Updates** — For monitoring dashboards (e.g., flood alerts).

**Do's (Best Practices):**

| Do | Rationale |
|---|---|
| ✅ Use perceptually uniform color scales (viridis) | Avoids perceptual bias |
| ✅ Limit dashboard to **5–9 visual elements** | Respects Miller's $7 \pm 2$ |
| ✅ Use legends, titles, and units | Clarity |
| ✅ Enable cross-filtering / linked brushing | Interactivity |
| ✅ Optimize for colorblind users (8% of males) | Accessibility |
| ✅ Test on target device (mobile/4K) | Responsive design |
| ✅ Provide data source citation | Trust and reproducibility |

**Don'ts (Common Pitfalls):**

| Don't | Why |
|---|---|
| ❌ Use 3D pie charts | Distort perception of proportions |
| ❌ Use more than 7 colors in one chart | Clutter |
| ❌ Use dual Y-axes | Misleading |
| ❌ Truncate Y-axis on bar charts | Exaggerates differences |
| ❌ Use the rainbow/jet palette | Perceptually non-uniform |
| ❌ Embed data inside image formats | Loses interactivity |
| ❌ Clutter dashboard with non-data ink (chart junk) | Reduces clarity |

**Reference Tools (KTU Approved):**
- **ArcGIS Dashboards** (Esri) — Geospatial focus.
- **Tableau** — BI focus.
- **Power BI** — Enterprise BI.
- **Plotly Dash / Streamlit** — Python-based custom dashboards.
- **Leaflet / Mapbox GL** — Web-based interactive maps.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**
>
> Common mistakes students make in VDA exam questions:
>
> 1. **Confusing "choropleth" with "heatmap"** — choropleths shade predefined regions; heatmaps show density kernels over continuous space.
> 2. **Recommending the rainbow palette** — this is considered a fundamental error in modern cartography; use sequential or diverging schemes.
> 3. **Forgetting the GVF formula** in Jenks Natural Breaks questions.
> 4. **Confusing Bertin's variables with MacEachren's extensions** — Bertin's 7 are static; modern additions include animation, transparency.
> 5. **Not justifying the number of classes** — always cite Miller's Law ($7 \pm 2$) and the empirical 5–7 class rule.
> 6. **Writing about visualization without referencing perception theory** — KTU marks are awarded for *why* a design works, not just *what* to do.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Visual Data Analytics**

- **Definition:** VDA = Automated Analysis + Interactive Visualization + Cognitive Reasoning.
- **Bertin's 7 Variables:** Position, Size, Shape, Value, Hue, Orientation, Texture. **Position is most accurate.**
- **Extended Variables:** Animation, Transparency, Crispness, Resolution, Time.
- **Sturges' Rule:** $k = 1 + \log_2 N$ — for bin count.
- **Miller's Law:** Working memory holds $7 \pm 2$ chunks.
- **Pre-attentive time:** $\le 200$ ms.
- **Classifications:** Equal Interval, Quantile, Natural Breaks (GVF), Standard Deviation, Geometric Interval.
- **Jenks GVF:** $\text{GVF} = (\text{SDAM} - \text{SDCM}) / \text{SDAM}$ — higher is better.
- **Optimal map classes:** 4–7, ideally 5–6.
- **ColorBrewer:** Sequential (low→high), Diverging (midpoint), Qualitative (nominal).
- **Avoid:** Rainbow palette, 3D pie charts, dual Y-axes, chart junk.
- **Use:** Viridis, Cividis, perceptually uniform scales.
- **Luminance (Rec. 709):** $L = 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B$.
- **Multivariate Tools:** Scatterplot matrix, parallel coordinates, small multiples, glyphs.
- **Dashboard Principles:** Hierarchy, alignment, consistency, progressive disclosure, $7 \pm 2$ elements.
- **Tools:** Tableau, ArcGIS Dashboards, Power BI, Plotly, Streamlit, Leaflet.
- **Kerala Use Cases:** KSDMA flood maps, KSPCB pollution dashboards, Local Self Government planning dashboards.

---

**End of Module 2 — Visual Data Analytics Notes**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — PECET414 Cartography and GIS*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Location Based Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET414 - CARTOGRAPHY AND GIS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Location Based Services ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Location-Based Services (LBS)** are *information services* accessible through mobile devices (smartphones, tablets, wearables, or in-vehicle navigation units) that utilize the **real-time geographic position** of the user to deliver context-aware, spatially relevant content, applications, and functionalities. According to the OGC (Open Geospatial Consortium), an LBS is formally defined as: > A service whose return or execution is dependent on the location of the user (or the user's device), expressed in a standardized geospatial format (e.g., latitude/longitude, UTM). The **four mandatory pillars** of any LBS deployment are: 1. **Where (Location Component):** Precise position of the user/device. 2. **Who (User Component):** Identity, profile, and preferences of the user. 3. **When (Time Component):** Temporal context (real-time, historical, predictive). 4. **What (Service Component):** The actual information/action delivered (map, route, alert). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of Module 2 is not just on "what is LBS" but on understanding its **positioning technologies, architecture, components, and geospatial data integration** with cartographic principles. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are a tourist in a new city carrying a **personal guide** who: - Always knows exactly where you are standing (positioning), - Knows your personal preferences (e.g., "vegetarian, budget under ₹500") (user profile), - Knows the current time (e.g., "it's 1:30 PM, lunch time") (temporal context), - And can instantly suggest the best route, restaurant, or attraction near you (service delivery). That **personal guide is essentially an LBS application** such as Google Maps, Zomato, Ola, or Swiggy. The guide's "knowledge" comes from a combination of satellite signals, cellular networks, and digital maps stored on cloud servers. ### 1.3 Standardized Terminology in LBS | Term | Expansion | Standard / Context | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **GNSS** | Global Navigation Satellite System | Generic term (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, IRNSS, BeiDou) | | **A-GPS** | Assisted Global Positioning System | Speeds up GPS fix using cellular data | | **POI** | Point of Interest | A named feature on a map (e.g., ATM, hospital) | | **GIS** | Geographic Information System | Backend of LBS data management | | **API** | Application Programming Interface | Interface for third-party LBS apps | | **CEP** | Circular Error Probable | 50% accuracy radius (often **≤ 5 m** in smartphones) | | **RTK** | Real-Time Kinematic | Centimeter-level accuracy (≤ 2 cm + 1 ppm) | | **DGPS** | Differential GPS | Meter-level accuracy (≤ 1–3 m) | | **NMEA** | National Marine Electronics Association | Standard GPS data sentence format | | **WGS 84** | World Geodetic System 1984 | Standard datum for GPS coordinates | > [!NOTE] > **Concept Distinction:** *Cartography* is the art/science of **map-making**, while *LBS* is the **delivery mechanism** of location context to end-users. Cartography provides the visual layer (basemaps, symbols, projections) that LBS applications render on the screen. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Architecture of an LBS The LBS architecture is generally a **four-tier client-server model**: - **Tier 1 – Mobile Client (User End):** The smartphone, tablet, or wearable running the LBS app. Contains the positioning hardware (GPS chip, Wi-Fi scanner, Bluetooth radio). - **Tier 2 – Communication Network:** GSM/3G/4G/5G cellular networks, Wi-Fi hotspots, or satellite links that carry requests and responses. - **Tier 3 – Positioning & Service Provider (Middleware):** Receives the location query, queries the GIS database, performs geospatial analysis (e.g., nearest neighbor search), and returns the result. - **Tier 4 – Geospatial Database & Content Provider:** Stores spatial data (roads, POIs, addresses) in formats such as shapefiles, GeoJSON, or PostGIS-enabled databases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this architecture matters for KTU:** A 14-mark question in the ESE often asks to "explain the architecture of LBS with a neat block diagram." You must draw all four tiers and label the data flow arrows. ### 2.2 Positioning Technologies (The "Where" Component) The **positioning method** determines the accuracy, availability, and cost of the LBS. There are three broad categories: **A. Satellite-Based Positioning (GNSS / GPS)** - Uses trilateration from at least **4 satellites** (3 for position + 1 for time synchronization). - Accuracy: **5–10 m** standalone, **1–3 m** with SBAS/DGPS, **1–2 cm** with RTK. - Limitation: Does not work indoors or in dense urban canyons (signal blockage). **B. Network-Based Positioning (Cellular / Wi-Fi)** - **Cell ID (CID):** Uses the identity of the serving cell tower; accuracy **100 m – 5 km** (rural to urban). - **E-OTD / OTDOA:** Measures time difference of arrival between multiple cell towers; accuracy **50–150 m**. - **Wi-Fi Fingerprinting:** Matches the visible Wi-Fi access point MAC addresses against a database (e.g., Google's, Skyhook's); indoor accuracy **5–30 m**. - **Bluetooth Beacons (BLE):** Short-range (< 50 m) indoor positioning using RSSI; accuracy **1–3 m**, widely used in malls and airports. **C. Hybrid Positioning (Sensor Fusion)** - Combines GNSS + Wi-Fi + Cell ID + Inertial Sensors (accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer). - Uses a **Kalman filter** or **Particle filter** to fuse noisy data streams. - Provides seamless indoor-outdoor tracking. ### 2.3 Mathematical Foundation: Coordinate Systems & Distance LBS operates on a **3D spherical Earth model**. The two primary distance formulations are: - **Euclidean Distance** (valid for small areas on a plane): $$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$ - **Great-Circle Distance (Haversine Formula):** Valid for any two points on the Earth's surface (radius **R ≈ 6,371 km**). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the formulas below. They are the most frequently tested equations in LBS problems. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units | Typical Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Haversine Distance | $d = 2R \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right)$ | $R = 6371$ km, $\phi$ = latitude, $\lambda$ = longitude (radians) | Real-world distance between two GPS coordinates | | 2 | Spherical Law of Cosines | $\cos(d/R) = \sin\phi_1\sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos(\Delta\lambda)$ | Same as above | Alternative to Haversine | | 3 | Bearing (Forward Azimuth) | $\theta = \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(\sin\Delta\lambda\cos\phi_2,\;\cos\phi_1\sin\phi_2 - \sin\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos\Delta\lambda\right)$ | $\theta$ in radians, then × $180/\pi$ for degrees | Compass direction from point 1 to point 2 | | 4 | GPS Pseudorange | $\rho = c \cdot (t_r - t_s) + c \cdot \Delta t$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\Delta t$ = clock error | Trilateration raw measurement | | 5 | DOP (Dilution of Precision) | $\text{DOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^\top H)^{-1}}$ | $H$ = geometry matrix | Accuracy degradation factor (HDOP, VDOP, PDOP, GDOP) | | 6 | CEP (50% probability) | $r_{\text{CEP}} \approx 0.588 \cdot \text{HDOP} \cdot \sigma_{URE}$ | $\sigma_{URE}$ = user range error (~2.4 m for GPS) | 50% of fixes fall within this radius | | 7 | R95 (95% probability) | $r_{R95} \approx 2.08 \cdot \text{HDOP} \cdot \sigma_{URE}$ | Same | 95% of fixes fall within this radius | | 8 | RSSI Path Loss (Log-distance) | $P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$ | $n$ = path-loss exponent (2–4), $d_0$ = 1 m | Indoor Wi-Fi/BLE ranging | | 9 | UTM Grid Distance Approx. | $d \approx \sqrt{(\Delta E)^2 + (\Delta N)^2}$ (small areas) | $\Delta E, \Delta N$ in meters | Flat-earth approximation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note for Tables:** In KTU answer scripts, always state the units explicitly. A "distance" without a unit can cost you a mark. For example, write "$d = 3.42$ km" not just "$d = 3.42$". ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of LBS - **Transportation & Logistics:** Fleet management (e.g., DHL, FedEx), ride-hailing (Ola, Uber), route optimization. - **Disaster Management:** Real-time evacuation routing, locating victims via cell-tower triangulation during earthquakes. - **Public Health:** Geo-fencing COVID quarantine zones, locating the nearest hospital/ambulance. - **Smart Cities:** Real-time traffic signal optimization, smart parking, noise/pollution monitoring. - **Surveying & Civil Engineering:** LBS-integrated GIS for asset management, road inventory, and utility mapping. - **Agriculture:** Precision farming using GNSS-guided tractors and drones. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Haversine Formula for Great-Circle Distance The Haversine formula is the most rigorously tested derivation in KTU ESE for LBS problems. Below is a complete, step-by-step derivation starting from first principles. **Given:** - Two points A and B on the Earth's surface. - $\phi_1, \lambda_1$ = latitude and longitude of A. - $\phi_2, \lambda_2$ = latitude and longitude of B. - $R$ = mean radius of the Earth = **6,371 km**. **Goal:** Derive an expression for the great-circle distance $d$ between A and B. **Step 1 — Express the angular difference** The change in latitude $\Delta\phi$ and longitude $\Delta\lambda$ are: $$\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1$$ $$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_2 - \lambda_1$$ **Step 2 — Use the spherical law of cosines** For a sphere of radius $R$, the chord length $d$ between two points and the central angle $\Delta\sigma$ are related by $d = 2R \sin(\Delta\sigma/2)$, and the spherical law of cosines gives: $$\cos(\Delta\sigma) = \sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)$$ **Step 3 — Convert to a half-angle identity** The Haversine function is defined as $\operatorname{hav}(\theta) = \sin^2(\theta/2) = (1 - \cos\theta)/2$. Applying this identity to $\cos(\Delta\sigma)$: $$\operatorname{hav}(\Delta\sigma) = \frac{1 - \cos(\Delta\sigma)}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the law of cosines expression** Substituting Step 2 into Step 3: $$ \begin{aligned} \operatorname{hav}(\Delta\sigma) &= \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 - \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)\right] \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\left[\sin^2\phi_1 + \cos^2\phi_1\right] + \frac{1}{2}\left[\sin^2\phi_2 + \cos^2\phi_2\right] - \frac{1}{2}\left[\sin\phi_1 \sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)\right] \end{aligned} $$ Using the identity $2\sin A \sin B = \cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)$ and $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$: **Step 5 — Simplify using angle sum identities** After algebraic expansion and grouping (the key insight is to express $1 - \cos(\Delta\sigma)$ in terms of half-angles $\Delta\phi$ and $\Delta\lambda$): $$ \begin{aligned} \operatorname{hav}(\Delta\sigma) &= \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \cdot \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Solve for the distance $d$** Since $\Delta\sigma = d/R$, we have $\operatorname{hav}(d/R) = \sin^2(d/2R)$. Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} \sin^2\!\left(\frac{d}{2R}\right) &= \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) \\ \frac{d}{2R} &= \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right) \\ d &= 2R \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)}\right) \end{aligned} $$ This is the **Haversine formula**. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example: Numerical Evaluation **Problem:** Two mobile users are at locations: - User A: $\phi_1 = 10.0261°$ N, $\lambda_1 = 76.3125°$ E (Kochi) - User B: $\phi_2 = 11.2588°$ N, $\lambda_2 = 75.7804°$ E (Kozhikode) Calculate the great-circle distance between them. **Step 1 — Convert degrees to radians** $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_1 &= 10.0261 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17500 \text{ rad} \\ \phi_2 &= 11.2588 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.19651 \text{ rad} \\ \lambda_1 &= 76.3125 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33200 \text{ rad} \\ \lambda_2 &= 75.7804 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.32268 \text{ rad} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute differences** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta\phi &= 0.19651 - 0.17500 = 0.02151 \text{ rad} \\ \Delta\lambda &= 1.32268 - 1.33200 = -0.00932 \text{ rad} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute trigonometric terms** $$ \begin{aligned} \sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) &= \sin(0.010755) = 0.010755 \\ \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) &= 0.00011567 \\ \cos\phi_1 &= \cos(0.17500) = 0.98468 \\ \cos\phi_2 &= \cos(0.19651) = 0.98077 \\ \sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) &= \sin(-0.00466) = -0.00466 \\ \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) &= 0.00002171 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the inner square root** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 0.00011567 + (0.98468)(0.98077)(0.00002171) \\ &= 0.00011567 + 0.00002097 \\ &= 0.00013664 \\ \sqrt{A} &= 0.011690 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the arcsin and final distance** $$ \begin{aligned} \arcsin(0.011690) &= 0.011690 \text{ rad (for small angles } \arcsin x \approx x\text{)} \\ d &= 2 \times 6371 \times 0.011690 \\ d &= 148.92 \text{ km} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** The great-circle distance between Kochi and Kozhikode is approximately **148.92 km**, which closely matches the real-world rail/road distance of ~150 km. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Positioning Methods | Method | Accuracy | Indoor? | Power Consumption | Cost | Best Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **GPS (Standalone)** | 5–10 m | No | High | Free | Outdoor navigation | | **A-GPS** | 5–10 m (faster TTFF) | Limited | Medium | Free | Smartphones in urban areas | | **DGPS / SBAS (GAGAN)** | 1–3 m | No | High | Free (subscription in some cases) | Surveying, precision agriculture | | **RTK-GPS** | 1–2 cm | No | Very High | ₹₹₹ | Cadastral surveying, machine control | | **Cell ID** | 100 m – 5 km | Yes | Low | Free | Emergency 911/112 calls | | **Wi-Fi Fingerprinting** | 5–30 m | Yes | Medium | Free | Indoor malls, airports | | **Bluetooth BLE Beacons** | 1–3 m | Yes | Very Low | ₹₹ (deployment cost) | Retail, museums, asset tracking | | **Hybrid (GNSS + Wi-Fi + IMU)** | 2–5 m | Yes | Medium | Free | Pedestrian dead reckoning (PDR) | | **Inertial (IMU only)** | Drift ~1% of distance | Yes | Low | ₹₹ | Short-term tracking, step counting | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 LBS Architecture: Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB A[User Mobile Device
Smartphone Tablet Wearable] --> B[Positioning Module
GPS Chip Wi-Fi Bluetooth IMU] A --> C[Application Layer
Map UI LBS App] C --> D[Communication Network
4G 5G Wi-Fi Satellite] B --> D D --> E[LBS Middleware Server
Service Provider] E --> F[Geospatial Database
PostGIS Shapefile GeoJSON] E --> G[Spatial Analysis Engine
Nearest Neighbor Routing] F --> E G --> H[Response Package
JSON XML Map Tile] H --> D D --> C C --> I[User Interface Output
Map Marker Route Alert] ``` ### 4.2 LBS Service Request Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step1: User Opens App
LBS Request Initiated] --> S2[Step2: Location Captured
GPS Coordinates Lat Long] S2 --> S3[Step3: Query Formed
Find Nearest Hospital] S3 --> S4[Step4: Sent via Network
4G 5G Wi-Fi] S4 --> S5[Step5: Middleware Processes
Spatial Query on GIS DB] S5 --> S6{Step6: Result Found?} S6 -- Yes --> S7[Step7: Return List of POIs
Distance Bearing ETA] S6 -- No --> S8[Step7: Return No Result
Suggest Broader Search] S7 --> S9[Step8: Render on Map
Markers Route Overlay] S8 --> S9 S9 --> S10[Step9: User Acts
Navigate Call Book] ``` ### 4.3 Positioning Method Selection Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB Start[User Requests Location] --> Q1{Is User
Outdoor?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Required
Accuracy?} Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is User
Indoor?} Q2 -- High cm level --> RTK[Use RTK GPS
1 to 2 cm] Q2 -- Medium m level --> SBAS[Use DGPS SBAS
1 to 3 m] Q2 -- Low 5 to 10 m --> GPS[Use Standard GPS
5 to 10 m] Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Indoor
Range?} Q4 -- Less than 50 m --> BLE[Use BLE Beacons
1 to 3 m] Q4 -- More than 50 m --> WIFI[Use WiFi Fingerprinting
5 to 30 m] Q4 -- Unknown --> CELL[Use Cell ID Fallback
100 m to 5 km] RTK --> End[Location Returned] SBAS --> End GPS --> End BLE --> End WIFI --> End CELL --> End ``` ### 4.4 LBS Data Flow Through Tiered Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TierA[Tier 1 Mobile Client] T1A[User Device] T1B[Positioning Hardware] end subgraph TierB[Tier 2 Network Layer] T2A[Cellular 4G 5G] T2B[Satellite Link] end subgraph TierC[Tier 3 Service Middleware] T3A[Web Server] T3B[Auth Security] T3C[API Gateway] end subgraph TierD[Tier 4 Data Layer] T4A[Spatial Database] T4B[POI Database] T4C[Map Tile Server] end T1A --> T2A T1B --> T2B T2A --> T3A T3A --> T3B T3B --> T3C T3C --> T4A T3C --> T4B T3C --> T4C T4A --> T3C T4B --> T3C T4C --> T3C T3C --> T2A T2A --> T1A ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Location Based Services (LBS). List any four applications of LBS.** **Model Answer:** *Definition:* Location Based Services (LBS) are information services that deliver spatially relevant content to a mobile user based on the automatic geographic positioning of their device, integrated with GIS data and real-time communication networks. *Four Applications of LBS:* 1. **Navigation and Routing:** Google Maps, Ola, Uber. 2. **Emergency Response:** Locating a 112 emergency caller via cell-tower triangulation. 3. **Proximity Marketing:** Targeted advertisements when a user enters a geofenced area (mall, airport). 4. **Fleet Management:** Real-time tracking of logistics vehicles (e.g., DHL, Amazon). 5. **Geo-tagging in Social Media:** Instagram, Facebook location tags. 6. **Public Health:** Contact tracing and quarantine geofencing. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between GPS and A-GPS.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | GPS (Global Positioning System) | A-GPS (Assisted GPS) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Full form** | Global Positioning System | Assisted Global Positioning System | | **Time to First Fix (TTFF)** | 30–120 seconds (cold start) | 5–15 seconds (warm start) | | **Data source for satellite orbit info** | Downloaded from satellites (slow) | Pre-loaded from cellular network (fast) | | **Indoor performance** | Poor (signal blocked) | Better (cellular network aids lock) | | **Network dependency** | Independent | Requires cellular/Wi-Fi connectivity | | **Power consumption** | Higher (continuous satellite tracking) | Lower (shortened satellite lock time) | | **Receiver complexity** | High | Reduced (server does heavy computation) | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In 14-mark LBS questions, students frequently lose marks for: (1) forgetting to convert degrees to radians, (2) missing the unit of distance, (3) drawing the architecture diagram without labeling the data flow arrows, and (4) confusing Cell ID accuracy (in km) with GPS accuracy (in m). Read the question carefully and address every sub-part. --- **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four-tier architecture of Location Based Services with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the middleware. **(b) [7 Marks)** Two LBS-enabled devices are located at the following WGS 84 coordinates: - Device P1: $10.0°$ N, $76.0°$ E - Device P2: $10.1°$ N, $76.1°$ E Calculate the great-circle distance between them using the Haversine formula. Take the Earth's radius $R = 6371$ km. --- **Model Solution for Q-A (a):** The LBS architecture is divided into four interconnected tiers: **Tier 1 — Mobile Client (Device Tier):** The user's smartphone, tablet, or wearable. It contains the GPS chip, Wi-Fi/Bluetooth radio, and the LBS application. It captures the user's location and renders the final result on the screen. **Tier 2 — Communication Network (Network Tier):** This includes cellular networks (4G/5G), Wi-Fi hotspots, and satellite links. It carries the location query from the device to the service provider and returns the result. **Tier 3 — Middleware / Service Provider (Processing Tier):** This is the *brain* of the LBS. Its roles are: - Authenticates the user and authorizes the request. - Receives the location coordinates from the device. - Translates the user's request (e.g., "find nearest ATM") into a spatial query. - Calls the geospatial database to perform a **nearest-neighbor search**. - Aggregates the result and sends it back to the device. **Tier 4 — Geospatial Database / Content Provider (Data Tier):** Stores the spatial data in formats like shapefiles, PostGIS, or GeoJSON. Contains the basemap, POIs, road networks, and address geocoding tables. **Block Diagram:** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.4 of these notes. --- **Model Solution for Q-A (b):** **Step 1 — Convert degrees to radians.** $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_1 &= 10.0° = 10.0 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17453 \text{ rad} \\ \phi_2 &= 10.1° = 10.1 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17628 \text{ rad} \\ \lambda_1 &= 76.0° = 76.0 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.32645 \text{ rad} \\ \lambda_2 &= 76.1° = 76.1 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.32819 \text{ rad} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute angular differences.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta\phi &= 0.17628 - 0.17453 = 0.00175 \text{ rad} \\ \Delta\lambda &= 1.32819 - 1.32645 = 0.00175 \text{ rad} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the half-angle sines.** $$ \begin{aligned} \sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) &= \sin(0.000873) \approx 0.000873 \\ \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) &\approx 7.62 \times 10^{-7} \\ \cos\phi_1 &= \cos(0.17453) = 0.98481 \\ \cos\phi_2 &= \cos(0.17628) = 0.98459 \\ \sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) &= \sin(0.000873) \approx 0.000873 \\ \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) &\approx 7.62 \times 10^{-7} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the inner square root.** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 7.62 \times 10^{-7} + (0.98481)(0.98459)(7.62 \times 10^{-7}) \\ &= 7.62 \times 10^{-7} \times (1 + 0.9695) \\ &= 7.62 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.9695 \\ &= 1.501 \times 10^{-6} \\ \sqrt{A} &\approx 0.001225 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the final distance.** $$ \begin{aligned} d &= 2 \times 6371 \times 0.001225 \\ d &= 12742 \times 0.001225 \\ d &= 15.61 \text{ km} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $d \approx 15.61$ km. --- **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various positioning techniques used in LBS. Compare satellite-based, network-based, and hybrid positioning in terms of accuracy, indoor performance, and power consumption. **(b) [7 Marks]** What is the Dilution of Precision (DOP)? With a neat diagram, explain the effect of satellite geometry on GDOP, PDOP, HDOP, and VDOP. --- **Model Solution for Q-B (a):** Positioning techniques in LBS are broadly classified into three families: **(i) Satellite-Based Positioning (GNSS):** Uses signals from at least 4 satellites (trilateration: 3 for X, Y, Z position + 1 for time). Example systems: GPS (USA), GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (EU), IRNSS/NavIC (India), BeiDou (China). Standalone accuracy: **5–10 m**; with SBAS/DGPS: **1–3 m**; with RTK: **1–2 cm**. **(ii) Network-Based Positioning:** - **Cell-ID (CID):** Identity of the serving cell tower gives a coarse location (100 m to 5 km). - **E-OTD / OTDOA:** Time-difference of arrival measurements; accuracy 50–150 m. - **Wi-Fi Fingerprinting:** Matching observed Wi-Fi MAC addresses against a database; accuracy 5–30 m indoors. **(iii) Hybrid Positioning (Sensor Fusion):** Combines GNSS, Wi-Fi, Cell-ID, and Inertial Measurement Units (IMU) using a Kalman filter. Provides seamless indoor-outdoor positioning and is the default approach in modern smartphones (Apple, Samsung, Qualcomm chipsets). **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Satellite (GNSS) | Network (Wi-Fi/Cell) | Hybrid (Fused) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Accuracy** | 5–10 m outdoor; poor indoors | 5–30 m indoor; 100 m–5 km outdoor | 2–5 m continuous | | **Indoor Performance** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | | **Power Consumption** | High | Low | Medium (optimized) | | **Best Use** | Outdoor navigation | Indoor/urban canyon | Ubiquitous tracking | --- **Model Solution for Q-B (b):** **Definition:** The **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** is a dimensionless number that quantifies how the geometric arrangement of satellites affects the accuracy of a GNSS fix. A low DOP implies a favorable satellite geometry (wide spread), while a high DOP implies poor geometry (satellites clustered together). **Mathematical Form:** DOP is derived from the satellite-to-receiver geometry matrix $H$: $$\text{DOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}\left((H^\top H)^{-1}\right)}$$ **Hierarchy of DOPs:** - **GDOP (Geometric DOP):** Includes 3D position + time. Ideal < 3, Poor > 6. - **PDOP (Position DOP):** 3D position only. Ideal < 3. - **HDOP (Horizontal DOP):** Latitude and longitude only. Ideal < 2. - **VDOP (Vertical DOP):** Height only. Ideal < 3. **Effect of Satellite Geometry:** When satellites are **well-spread** (one overhead, four near the horizon), the intersection volume of their ranging spheres is small → low DOP → high accuracy. When satellites are **clustered** in one part of the sky, the intersection volume is large → high DOP → low accuracy. *See diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes for the geometry selection logic.* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **LBS = Location + Service.** Never define it as just "GPS" — GPS is only the positioning input; the *service* is the GIS-enabled output. - **Four Pillars:** Where (location), Who (user), When (time), What (service). - **Four Tiers of Architecture:** Device → Network → Middleware → Database. - **Positioning Methods:** GNSS (satellite), Cell-ID / Wi-Fi / BLE (network), Hybrid (fused). - **Accuracy Hierarchy (lowest to highest):** Cell-ID (km) < Wi-Fi (5–30 m) < GPS (5–10 m) < DGPS (1–3 m) < RTK (1–2 cm). - **CEP:** 50% probability radius; **R95:** 95% probability radius; $R95 \approx 3.5 \times CEP$. - **Haversine Formula:** Always convert degrees to **radians** before substituting. State $R = 6371$ km explicitly. - **DOP:** Always aim for GDOP < 4 in professional surveys. - **Datum:** LBS uses **WGS 84**; Indian surveying uses **WGS 84 / UTM** or **Everest 1830** for legacy data. - **Privacy:** LBS raises GDPR / India DPDP Act concerns — location data is classified as **sensitive personal data**. - **Indoor positioning:** Use **BLE beacons** (1–3 m accuracy) for malls; **Wi-Fi fingerprinting** for airports. - **Key Standards:** NMEA-0183 (GPS data format), OGC LBS API, ISO 19132 (LBS reference model). - **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic primarily addresses CO2 (Demonstrate the integration of geospatial data with LBS) and CO3 (Apply positioning technologies for spatial analysis). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** "For 3-mark questions, give 1 definition + 2–3 points. For 7-mark sub-parts, include a labeled diagram. For 14-mark questions, structure as: (i) Theory with diagram [4 marks], (ii) Comparison/tabulation [3 marks], (iii) Numerical/formula derivation [7 marks]." Always end with a concluding sentence tying the answer to a real-world application.

topics.title: Multimedia Cartography and Georelief
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET414 - CARTOGRAPHY AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multimedia Cartography and Georelief

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Multimedia Cartography

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
Multimedia Cartography is the discipline of designing, producing, and disseminating cartographic products (maps, charts, atlases, and spatial visualizations) that integrate multiple forms of media—including text, static graphics (images/maps), audio, video, animation, and interactive digital interfaces—within a unified geospatial framework to enhance spatial understanding, communication, and decision-making.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is the convergence of traditional cartographic principles with Information and Communication Technology (ICT) tools to deliver **interactive, sensory-rich, and user-centric** geographic information products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Multimedia cartography is NOT just "a digital map." It is a *system* that combines **at least three media types** (e.g., text + image + audio) into a single, navigable cartographic experience — often delivered via web GIS, mobile GIS, or virtual reality (VR) platforms.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Movie Theatre" Intuition:**
Imagine a traditional printed map as a **silent black-and-white photograph** — it conveys information, but only through one sense (sight) and one static moment.
Now imagine a **smart theatre** showing a movie:
- The **screen (video/animation)** shows a 3D rotating terrain.
- The **surround sound (audio)** plays the roar of a waterfall as you zoom into a feature.
- The **subtitles (text)** describe the location's history.
- The **interactive remote (hyperlinks/clicks)** lets *you* choose what to explore next.

Multimedia Cartography is exactly this — a map that **sees, speaks, animates, and responds**. The map ceases to be a passive document and becomes an *active experience*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Components of Multimedia Cartography (Mnemonic — "TIVA"):**
> - **T**ext — Labels, legends, descriptive narratives
> - **I**mages — Orthophotos, satellite imagery, scanned historical maps
> - **V**ideo & Animation — Fly-throughs, time-lapse, 3D terrain rotation
> - **A**udio — Narration, sound cues, ambient sound

### 1.2 Georelief

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
**Georelief** (also called *terrain representation* or *relief portrayal*) refers to the cartographic depiction of the three-dimensional configuration of the Earth's surface — its elevation, slope, aspect, and landform morphology — on a two-dimensional map medium using mathematical, graphical, or symbolic techniques.

The term combines *geo* (Earth) + *relief* (variation in elevation), and is governed by the principle that **a map must convey the *third dimension* (height) on a *two-dimensional* surface** without sacrificing geometric accuracy.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Braille Book" of the Earth:**
If a sighted person reads a book with their eyes, a blind person reads with their fingertips — feeling raised dots that represent letters.
Similarly, a map reader cannot *touch* a mountain, but the cartographer must make the mountain "felt" through **visual texture**: dense contour lines, darker shades, hatched patterns, and shadows. Each technique is a different "language" for the fingers-of-the-eye to sense elevation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Georelief Matters (KTU Engineering Context):**
> - Civil Engineering: Route alignment, cut-and-fill estimation
> - Environmental Engineering: Flood modeling, watershed analysis
> - Urban Planning: Viewshed, skyline analysis
> - Defense & Navigation: Tactical terrain assessment

The two principal families of relief representation are:
1. **Qualitative methods** — Hachures, hill shading, landform symbols
2. **Quantitative methods** — Contours, hypsometric tints, spot heights, digital elevation models
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Multimedia Cartography — Operational Concept Breakdown

The architecture of a multimedia cartographic product rests on **five interlocking pillars**:

**(1) Data Layer (The Skeleton)**
- Vector data (points, lines, polygons)
- Raster data (satellite imagery, scanned maps)
- Attribute data (linked to features)
- Real-time feeds (GPS, IoT sensors)

**(2) Processing Layer (The Brain)**
- Geoprocessing tools (buffer, overlay, network analysis)
- Coordinate transformations (Datum: WGS-1984, Projection: UTM, etc.)
- 3D rendering engines (OpenGL-based)
- Animation timeline management

**(3) Presentation Layer (The Face)**
- Visual hierarchy (title, legend, scale, north arrow)
- Color theory (sequential, diverging, qualitative palettes)
- Typography and symbology
- Responsive design for multiple devices (desktop, tablet, mobile)

**(4) Interaction Layer (The Hands)**
- Pan, zoom, rotate, tilt (2D/3D navigation)
- Click-to-identify (hyperlinked attributes)
- Time-slider for temporal data
- Voice / gesture / touch input (modern interfaces)

**(5) Distribution Layer (The Voice)**
- Web servers (WMS, WFS, WCS — OGC standards)
- Cloud platforms (e.g., Google Earth Engine, ArcGIS Online)
- Mobile apps (offline tiles for fieldwork)
- AR/VR headsets (immersive cartography)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Distinction — Multimedia vs. Multisensory Cartography:**
> *Multimedia* emphasizes the **medium** (text, image, audio, video).
> *Multisensory* emphasizes the **human perception** (sight, sound, touch).
> In modern KTU parlance, the two terms are used interchangeably, but multimedia is the more accepted academic term.

---

### 2.2 Georelief — Methods of Relief Representation

The cartographic representation of relief has evolved over centuries. KTU 2024 syllabus requires familiarity with the following techniques:

#### (a) Hachures (Historical Qualitative Method)
- Short lines drawn in the direction of **steepest slope**.
- Density and thickness indicate **steepness**; length indicates **relative height**.
- Originated in the 18th century (Swiss school — Johann Heinrich Weiss).
- **Limitation:** Time-consuming; only qualitative; replaced by modern methods.

#### (b) Hill Shading / Shaded Relief (Qualitative-Pictorial Method)
- Simulates the **shadow cast by terrain** under a hypothetical light source.
- Conventionally, light source is placed at the **north-west (315° azimuth, 45° altitude)**.
- Produces a *photographic* impression of terrain.
- **Modern variant:** Multi-directional hillshade (combines several light angles to remove bias).

**Mathematical Foundation of Hillshading (Lambertian Reflectance Model):**

$$
\text{Hillshade} = 255 \times \left[ ( \cos(\text{Zenith}) \times \cos(\text{Slope}) ) + ( \sin(\text{Zenith}) \times \sin(\text{Slope}) \times \cos(\text{Azimuth} - \text{Aspect}) ) \right]
$$

Where:
- **Zenith angle** = $90° - \text{Altitude angle}$ of the light source.
- **Slope** = Maximum rate of elevation change (in degrees).
- **Azimuth** = Compass direction of the light source.
- **Aspect** = Compass direction the slope faces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "NW Light, SE Shadow":**
> Cartographers *always* place the imaginary sun in the **north-west** so that south-east slopes appear in shadow — the most intuitive perception for northern-hemisphere map readers.

#### (c) Hypsometric Tints (Quantitative-Chromatic Method)
- Color-coded **elevation zones** layered on a map.
- Standard color ramp (Cartographic Convention):
  - **Green** (lowlands, 0–500 m)
  - **Yellow/Tan** (midlands, 500–1500 m)
  - **Brown** (highlands, >1500 m)
  - **White** (peaks, snowline)
- Two approaches: **(i) Layer tints** (discrete bands) and **(ii) Continuous tints** (gradual blending).

#### (d) Contour Lines (Quantitative Method — The Gold Standard)
- An **isoline** connecting points of **equal elevation** above a reference datum (usually Mean Sea Level).
- **Contour Interval (CI):** The vertical distance between consecutive contours.
- **Index Contour:** Every 5th contour, drawn thicker and labeled with elevation.
- **Supplementary Contour:** Dashed lines for minor terrain features.

**Key Rules of Contours (KTU Frequently Asked):**
1. Contours **never cross** (except at a vertical cliff → they coincide).
2. Closely spaced contours ⇒ **steep slope**.
3. Widely spaced contours ⇒ **gentle slope**.
4. Evenly spaced contours ⇒ **uniform slope**.
5. Closed concentric contours ⇒ **hill (higher inside)** or **depression (with hachure marks)**.
6. V-shaped contours pointing **upstream** across a valley.

#### (e) Spot Heights & Bench Marks
- **Spot height (▲):** Precisely surveyed elevation at a specific point.
- **Trigonometrical station (△):** Surveyed hilltop with known elevation.
- **Bench mark (BM):** Permanently marked elevation reference (e.g., on a building).

#### (f) Digital Elevation Model (DEM) — The Modern Paradigm
A **Digital Elevation Model (DEM)** is a raster representation of the Earth's surface where each cell (pixel) stores an elevation value.

**Three primary data structures of DEMs:**

| Structure | Acronym | Description | Storage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Raster (Grid)** | DEM | Regular grid of elevation values | Matrix $z_{ij}$ |
| **Triangulated Irregular Network** | TIN | Irregular network of triangular facets | Nodes + topology |
| **Point Cloud** | LIDAR | Dense 3D points from laser scanning | $(x, y, z)$ tuples |

**TIN storage (analytical form):**
A TIN is built on the **Delaunay Triangulation** principle, where for each triangle, the elevation at any point is given by:

$$
z(x, y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y
$$

The coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2$ are solved for each triangle using the three vertex elevations.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Slope** $= \dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta d} \times 100\%$ | $\Delta h$ = rise, $\Delta d$ = horizontal run | Civil: road gradient |
| 2 | **Gradient (degrees)** $\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta d}\right)$ | $\theta$ in degrees | Terrain analysis |
| 3 | **Scale** $S = \dfrac{\text{Map Distance}}{\text{Ground Distance}}$ | $S$ dimensionless ratio | All map work |
| 4 | **Contour Interval (CI)** $CI = \dfrac{\text{Total Relief Range}}{\text{Number of Contours}}$ | Vertical spacing | Topo map reading |
| 5 | **Hillshade (Lambertian)** $H = 255 \cdot [ \cos Z \cos \alpha + \sin Z \sin \alpha \cos(\beta - A) ]$ | $Z$ = zenith, $\alpha$ = slope, $\beta$ = sun azimuth, $A$ = aspect | Visual relief |
| 6 | **Viewshed (line-of-sight)** $LOS = z_{\text{observer}} + \dfrac{(z_{\text{target}} - z_{\text{obs}})}{d} \cdot r$ | $r$ = distance along line | Telecom tower placement |
| 7 | **Volume by DEM (prismoidal)** $V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \cdot \bar{z}_i$ | $A_i$ = cell area, $\bar{z}_i$ = mean elevation | Reservoir capacity |
| 8 | **Multimedia bandwidth requirement** $B = R \times F \times N$ | $R$ = resolution, $F$ = frame rate, $N$ = channels | Web map streaming |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pipe-Symbol Rule:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is replaced by `\vert` or `\mid` in tables to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Multimedia Cartography & Georelief |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Highway alignment using DEM-based cut-and-fill optimization |
| **Urban Planning** | 3D city models (CityGML) with building shadows via hillshade |
| **Disaster Management** | Animated flood-inundation maps with audio alerts (multimedia) |
| **Defense** | Tactical decision aids using video fly-throughs of contested terrain |
| **Tourism** | Interactive museum kiosks with VR terrain walkthroughs |
| **Agriculture** | Contour-based terracing for soil conservation |
| **Mining** | Volume estimation of open-pit mines from TIN-derived DEMs |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Hillshade from First Principles (Lambertian Model)

**Goal:** To derive the brightness value of a pixel on a hillshaded DEM given terrain slope, aspect, and sun geometry.

**Step 1 — Define the Vector Normal to the Terrain Surface**

Given a DEM with elevation $z = f(x, y)$, the gradient vector at any cell is:

$$
\nabla z = \left( \frac{\partial z}{\partial x},\; \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} \right)
$$

The **unit surface normal** $\vec{n}$ is then:

$$
\vec{n} = \frac{ \left( -\frac{\partial z}{\partial x},\; -\frac{\partial z}{\partial y},\; 1 \right) }{ \sqrt{ \left( \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} \right)^2 + 1 } }
$$

The negative signs account for the fact that the normal points *outward* from the surface (upward), opposite to the gradient of descent.

**Step 2 — Define the Unit Sun Vector**

Let the sun have an **altitude angle** $\alpha$ (above horizon) and **azimuth angle** $\beta$ (from north, clockwise). The unit sun vector $\vec{s}$ is:

$$
\vec{s} = \left( \sin\beta \cos\alpha,\; \cos\beta \cos\alpha,\; \sin\alpha \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Lambertian Cosine Law**

A **Lambertian surface** reflects light equally in all directions of the hemisphere, with intensity proportional to the cosine of the angle $\theta$ between the surface normal and the light source.

$$
\text{Reflectance} \propto \cos\theta = \vec{n} \cdot \vec{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the Slope and Aspect**

Let the terrain have **slope** $S$ (degrees) and **aspect** $A$ (degrees from north). The surface normal can be re-expressed as:

$$
\vec{n} = \left( \sin A \sin S,\; \cos A \sin S,\; \cos S \right)
$$

Substituting into the dot product:

$$
\cos\theta = (\sin A \sin S)(\sin\beta \cos\alpha) + (\cos A \sin S)(\cos\beta \cos\alpha) + (\cos S)(\sin\alpha)
$$

**Step 5 — Regrouping Terms**

$$
\cos\theta = \cos\alpha \cos S \left[ \frac{\sin A \sin\beta + \cos A \cos\beta}{1} \right] + \sin\alpha \cos S
$$

Wait — this needs careful regrouping. Let's re-expand directly:

$$
\cos\theta = \sin S \cos\alpha (\sin A \sin\beta + \cos A \cos\beta) + \cos S \sin\alpha
$$

Applying the cosine-of-difference identity $\cos(\beta - A) = \cos\beta\cos A + \sin\beta \sin A$:

$$
\cos\theta = \cos\alpha \sin S \cos(\beta - A) + \sin\alpha \cos S
$$

**Step 6 — Converting to Zenith Angle**

The **zenith angle** $Z = 90° - \alpha$. Therefore $\cos\alpha = \sin Z$ and $\sin\alpha = \cos Z$:

$$
\cos\theta = \sin Z \sin S \cos(\beta - A) + \cos Z \cos S
$$

**Step 7 — Final Hillshade Equation**

Scaling to 8-bit grayscale (0 = dark shadow, 255 = brightest):

$$
\boxed{H = 255 \times \left[ \cos Z \cos S + \sin Z \sin S \cos(\beta - A) \right]}
$$

This is the operational equation used in **ArcGIS, QGIS, and GRASS GIS** hillshade algorithms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check — Flat Terrain:** When $S = 0$, the equation reduces to $H = 255 \cos Z$. For overhead sun ($Z = 0$), $H = 255$ (full brightness). For sun at horizon ($Z = 90°$), $H = 0$ (full darkness). ✓
> **Sanity Check — Sun Behind Slope:** When $\beta - A = 180°$, $\cos(\beta - A) = -1$, and the second term becomes negative — the slope is in shadow. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Volume of a Reservoir from a DEM

**Goal:** To compute the storage capacity of a proposed reservoir using a DEM of the valley.

**Step 1 — Identify Catchment Boundary**
Using GIS overlay, delineate the watershed boundary at the proposed dam site.

**Step 2 — Generate Elevation-Area Curve**
For each contour interval (or DEM cell elevation class) $z_i$, count the number of cells above the spillway elevation. Multiply by cell area $A_c$:

$$
A_i = N_i \times A_c
$$

**Step 3 — Prismoidal Volume Between Successive Contours**
The volume between elevation $z_i$ and $z_{i+1}$ is:

$$
V_i = \frac{(A_i + A_{i+1})}{2} \times (z_{i+1} - z_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Total Storage**
Sum the incremental volumes from the lowest contour to the spillway level:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(A_i + A_{i+1})}{2} \times \Delta z_i
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**

A 3-cell DEM:

| Cell | Elevation (m) | Area (m²) |
|---|---|---|
| $z_1$ | 100 | 50,000 |
| $z_2$ | 110 | 30,000 |
| $z_3$ | 120 | 15,000 |

Volume between $z_1$ and $z_2$:

$$
V_1 = \frac{(50{,}000 + 30{,}000)}{2} \times 10 = 400{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

Volume between $z_2$ and $z_3$:

$$
V_2 = \frac{(30{,}000 + 15{,}000)}{2} \times 10 = 225{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

Total volume:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 400{,}000 + 225{,}000 = 625{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The prismoidal (trapezoidal) formula above is the **expected method**. A more accurate (but rarely required) method is the **Simpson's 1/3 rule**, but the trapezoidal rule is the KTU board-exam standard.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Relief Representation Methods

| Method | Type | Quantitative? | Modern? | KTU Weightage | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hachures | Qualitative pictorial | No | No | Low | Historical maps |
| Hill Shading | Qualitative-quantitative | Semi | Yes | High | Visual relief on web maps |
| Hypsometric Tints | Quantitative chromatic | Yes | Yes | High | Atlas maps, school maps |
| Contours | Quantitative | Yes (exact) | Yes | **Very High** | Engineering topo maps |
| Spot Heights | Quantitative point | Yes (exact) | Yes | Medium | Specific landmarks |
| DEM (Raster) | Quantitative digital | Yes | Yes | **Very High** | Modern GIS analysis |
| TIN | Quantitative digital | Yes | Yes | High | Variable-density terrain |
| 3D Perspective | Qualitative-quantitative | Semi | Yes | Medium | Fly-throughs, multimedia |
| LIDAR Point Cloud | Quantitative | Yes | Yes | High | High-resolution terrain |
| Augmented Reality Relief | Qualitative-quantitative | Semi | **Cutting edge** | Emerging | Future multimedia cartography |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Modern questions increasingly test the **integration** of two or more methods (e.g., "Explain how hillshade and hypsometric tints are combined in a modern web map"). Students must learn *complementarity*, not just isolated definitions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
