## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Define multimedia cartography. List any four media elements used in it.**

**Model Answer:**
Multimedia cartography is the discipline of producing and disseminating cartographic products that integrate multiple media types—text, images, video, audio, and animation—within an interactive geospatial framework, enabling enhanced spatial communication.

The four media elements are:
1. **Text** — labels, legends, descriptive captions
2. **Images** — satellite imagery, orthophotos, scanned maps
3. **Video/Animation** — fly-throughs, time-lapse visualizations
4. **Audio** — narration, ambient sounds, voice prompts

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What is georelief? Mention any three methods of representing relief on a map.**

**Model Answer:**
Georelief is the cartographic depiction of the three-dimensional shape of the Earth's surface—its elevation, slope, and landforms—on a two-dimensional map medium.

Three methods of relief representation are:
1. **Contour lines** — isolines connecting points of equal elevation
2. **Hill shading** — simulated shadows cast by a hypothetical light source
3. **Hypsometric tints** — color-coded elevation zones (green for lowlands, brown for highlands)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the concept of multimedia cartography. Discuss its key components, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the various methods of representing relief on maps with neat diagrams. Compare their relative merits. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Multimedia Cartography (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Multimedia cartography is the design and production of maps that combine multiple media forms (text, image, audio, video, animation) within an interactive digital environment to convey spatial information effectively.

**Key Components (3 marks):**
- **Data Layer:** Vector/raster/attribute data, real-time sensor feeds.
- **Processing Layer:** Geoprocessing, 3D rendering, animation timelines.
- **Presentation Layer:** Color theory, symbology, typography, responsive layout.
- **Interaction Layer:** Pan/zoom, hyperlinks, time-slider, voice/gesture input.
- **Distribution Layer:** Web servers (WMS/WFS), cloud platforms, mobile apps, AR/VR.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Enhances spatial understanding through multiple sensory channels.
2. Supports interactivity — the user controls the exploration.
3. Enables time-based visualizations (e.g., urban growth animations).
4. Reaches a wider audience (illiterate users benefit from audio).
5. Facilitates real-time decision support (e.g., disaster dashboards).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. High development cost and technical skill requirement.
2. Bandwidth/storage-intensive (especially video and LIDAR data).
3. Risk of cognitive overload — too many media can distract.
4. Accessibility barriers for users with sensory disabilities if not designed inclusively.

---

**Part (b) — Methods of Relief Representation (7 Marks)**

| Method | Principle | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hachures** | Lines along slope direction | Pictorial, intuitive | Time-consuming, qualitative only |
| **Hill Shading** | Simulated shadows (NW light) | Realistic 3D impression | Requires DEM, qualitative |
| **Hypsometric Tints** | Color-coded elevation zones | Easy elevation reading | Subjective color choice |
| **Contour Lines** | Isolines of equal elevation | Quantitative, precise | Cluttered in flat terrain |
| **Spot Heights** | Point elevation markers | Highly accurate | Sparse, no spatial continuity |
| **DEM (Raster)** | Grid of elevation values | Digital, supports analysis | Requires processing |
| **TIN** | Irregular triangular network | Adaptive resolution | Complex to generate |

**Comparison (2 marks):**
- For **engineering precision** → Contours + DEM
- For **public atlases** → Hypsometric tints
- For **web visualization** → Hillshade + tints
- For **modern VR/AR** → TIN with animation

**Diagrams (essential for full marks — 1 mark):** Students should sketch:
- A contour map showing concentric closed contours for a hill
- A hillshade rendering showing NW-lit terrain
- A hypsometric color ramp

---

#### ⭐ Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a multimedia cartographic system. Discuss how multimedia elements enhance a web-based GIS application. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Define Digital Elevation Model (DEM). Explain the structure of a TIN with its mathematical formulation. Compute the storage capacity of a small reservoir from a given DEM. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Architecture of Multimedia Cartographic System (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The architecture has five layers (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):
1. **Data Layer** — sources of spatial data
2. **Processing Layer** — geoprocessing and rendering
3. **Presentation Layer** — visual and auditory display
4. **Interaction Layer** — user input mechanisms
5. **Distribution Layer** — delivery channels

**Web GIS Enhancement (4 marks):**

Multimedia elements enhance a web-based GIS application as follows:
- **Text + Images:** Combine attribute data with on-the-fly popups containing photos of features (e.g., a heritage building's façade).
- **Video/Animation:** Show traffic flow over 24 hours using a time-slider with color-coded line symbology.
- **Audio:** Provide accessibility for visually impaired users — voice reads out feature names on hover.
- **3D Visualization:** CesiumJS / Google Earth Web enable 3D terrain rendering with hillshade and contours overlaid.
- **Real-time Feeds:** Combine IoT sensor data (e.g., water level) with animated charts and audio alerts.
- **User Experience:** Responsive design ensures the same product works on a phone, tablet, and desktop.

**Example:** The Bhuvan portal (ISRO) integrates satellite imagery, 3D terrain, and attribute queries — a complete multimedia cartographic experience.

---

**Part (b) — DEM, TIN, and Reservoir Capacity (7 Marks)**

**DEM Definition (1 mark):**
A Digital Elevation Model is a raster representation of the Earth's surface elevation, stored as a regular grid of $(x, y, z)$ values.

**TIN Structure (3 marks):**
- A TIN is a vector-based representation composed of irregularly distributed nodes connected by edges to form non-overlapping triangles.
- It uses the **Delaunay Triangulation** principle: the circumcircle of any triangle contains no other node.
- For each triangle with vertices $(x_1, y_1, z_1)$, $(x_2, y_2, z_2)$, $(x_3, y_3, z_3)$, the elevation at any interior point is given by a planar interpolation:

$$
z(x, y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y
$$

where the coefficients are obtained by solving the 3×3 linear system formed by the three vertices.

**Reservoir Capacity Calculation (3 marks):**
Using the prismoidal (trapezoidal) rule:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(A_i + A_{i+1})}{2} \times (z_{i+1} - z_i)
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (must show steps):**

Given a DEM with 4 contour levels:

| Contour (m) | Area (m²) |
|---|---|
| 100 | 80,000 |
| 110 | 50,000 |
| 120 | 25,000 |
| 130 | 8,000 |

$$
V_1 = \frac{(80{,}000 + 50{,}000)}{2} \times 10 = 650{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{(50{,}000 + 25{,}000)}{2} \times 10 = 375{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_3 = \frac{(25{,}000 + 8{,}000)}{2} \times 10 = 165{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{total}} = 650{,}000 + 375{,}000 + 165{,}000 = 1{,}190{,}000 \text{ m}^3}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Definition Incomplete:** Writing "multimedia cartography = digital map" loses 2 marks. Must include *integration of multiple media types*.
> 2. **Confusing Georelief with Geology:** Georelief = surface shape; Geology = rock composition. Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Forgetting to Label Contour Diagrams:** Any sketch of contours must show: (i) contour values, (ii) contour interval, (iii) index contour, (iv) hachures for depressions.
> 4. **Hillshade Formula Sign Error:** Forgetting the negative sign in $\cos(\beta - A)$ when the slope faces away from the sun → 1 mark lost.
> 5. **TIN Triangle Count Error:** TIN triangle count is always $2N - 2 - k$ (Euler's formula) where $N$ = nodes and $k$ = boundary nodes. Some students use wrong formula.
> 6. **Numerical Rounding:** Reservoir volume answers must retain at least 3 significant figures.
> 7. **No Diagram in 7-Mark Sub-Questions:** KTU examiners **mandate** a diagram for any 7-mark descriptive question. Skipping it → 1 to 2 marks deducted.
> 8. **Mixing Up Slope vs. Gradient:** Slope is a ratio; gradient is often expressed as an angle. Examiners expect both forms.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Multimedia Cartography & Georelief**

**✅ Core Definitions (Must Memorize):**
- Multimedia Cartography = integration of ≥3 media types in an interactive map
- Georelief = depiction of 3D terrain on a 2D map
- DEM = raster grid of elevation values
- TIN = vector-based triangulated terrain model
- Contour = isoline of equal elevation
- Hillshade = simulated terrain shadow (NW light convention)
- Hachure = slope-direction line (historical)

**✅ Key Mnemonics:**
- **"TIVA"** → Text, Images, Video, Audio (multimedia elements)
- **"NW Light, SE Shadow"** → Hillshade convention
- **"Close = Steep, Wide = Gentle"** → Contour spacing
- **"V's Point Upstream"** → Contour behavior in valleys

**✅ Must-Know Formulas:**
- Hillshade: $H = 255 [\cos Z \cos S + \sin Z \sin S \cos(\beta - A)]$
- Slope: $\theta = \arctan(\Delta h / \Delta d)$
- Volume (Prismoidal): $V = \sum \frac{A_i + A_{i+1}}{2} \Delta z_i$

**✅ Comparison Tables (Likely 7-Mark Questions):**
- Hachures vs. Hillshade vs. Hypsometric vs. Contours vs. DEM
- Multimedia vs. Traditional Cartography
- DEM vs. TIN

**✅ Engineering Applications (Always mention in answers):**
- Civil: cut-and-fill, route alignment
- Defense: viewshed, terrain visualization
- Disaster: flood mapping, animated simulations
- Tourism: virtual tours, AR wayfinding

**✅ KTU-Specific Exam Traps:**
- Always label diagrams (contour values, interval, index contour)
- Always show step-by-step numerical calculations
- Never use the word "geology" when "georelief" is meant
- Always cite at least one real-world example (e.g., Bhuvan, Google Earth)

**✅ Modern Trends (Bonus Marks):**
- VR/AR-based cartography
- Real-time IoT-integrated maps
- AI-driven map generalization
- Crowdsourced geospatial data (OpenStreetMap)
- 3D city models (CityGML / Cesium)

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Multimedia Cartography and Georelief*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET414 - CARTOGRAPHY AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mobile Cartography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobile Cartography** is the specialized branch of cartography concerned with the **design, production, presentation, and interactive use of geospatial information on mobile, handheld, and wearable computing devices** (such as smartphones, tablets, smartwatches, and Head-Mounted Displays / HMDs). It integrates principles of traditional map-making with the technical constraints of mobile hardware, real-time positioning, wireless network communication, and human-computer interaction (HCI).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, Mobile Cartography is treated as a sub-discipline of **Ubiquitous Cartography** — maps that move *with* the user, dynamically adapting to the user's **position, orientation, task, and device capability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Synonyms Used in KTU Module 2:**
> - Ubiquitous Cartography
> - Pervasive Mapping
> - Handheld Cartography
> - Wearable Cartography (subset)

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Mobile Cartography

Mobile Cartography rests on three intersecting technological pillars:

| Pillar | Core Function | Enabling Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | Display, compute, sense | Smartphones, GPS chips, IMU sensors, OLED/LCD screens |
| **Software** | Render, generalize, interact | Mobile GIS apps, Map SDKs (Mapbox, Google Maps SDK) |
| **Network** | Data delivery, cloud sync | 4G/5G, Wi-Fi, MQTT, REST APIs, tile servers |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Tour Guide" 🧭

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a tourist visiting a new city.** A **traditional paper map** is static, large, and cannot tell where you are. A **car GPS** tells you where you are but is fixed inside a vehicle. A **mobile cartography app** (like Google Maps on your phone) is like a *smart tour guide who walks with you* — it knows your real-time location, the direction you are facing, how fast you are walking, the time of day, and the ambient light around you. It then *dynamically redraws itself* — showing less detail when you are stationary and zooming into street-level detail as you turn a corner.

This analogy captures the **three defining traits** of mobile cartography:
1. **Mobility** — the map moves with the user.
2. **Context-awareness** — the map reacts to the user's environment.
3. **Personalization & Real-time adaptation** — the map changes based on user behavior.

### 1.4 Evolution of Mobile Cartography

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Timeline (for KTU context):**
> - **1990s:** Early PDAs (Palm, Psion) with monochrome maps and limited GPS.
> - **2000s:** Introduction of smartphones (iPhone 2007) — the cartographic revolution.
> - **2010s:** Location-Based Services (LBS) explosion, OpenStreetMap mobile editors, vector tile maps.
> - **2020s:** Augmented Reality (AR) maps, Wearable Cartography (smartwatches, AR glasses), AI-driven map personalization.

### 1.5 The Mobile Cartography Ecosystem — A First Glance

The following diagram (developed further in Section 4) shows how a user, a mobile device, a positioning system, a network, a server, and geospatial data all interact:

```
User → Mobile Device → [GPS/Sensors + Mobile App]
        ↓
   Wireless Network (4G/5G/Wi-Fi)
        ↓
   Map Server / Tile Server
        ↓
   Spatial Database (PostGIS, etc.)
        ↓
   Real-time Map Tiles / Vectors → back to Device
```

This continuous **request–response cycle** is what makes mobile cartography "live" — a stark contrast to static paper cartography.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Four-Layer Mobile Cartography Model

Mobile Cartography can be analyzed as a stack of four logical layers. Each layer has specific responsibilities and constraints. KTU examiners frequently ask students to identify or explain these layers.

### Layer 1 — Perception Layer (User–Map Interface)
- **Concerns**: Visual legibility on small screens, touch interaction, color contrast under sunlight/dark.
- **Constraints**: Screen size typically **3.5 to 6.9 inches** diagonal, resolution **326 ppi (Retina)** to **500+ ppi**, brightness ranging from **2 to 1000+ nits**.
- **Design Rules**:
  - Minimum legible text size: **≥ 12 px** at typical viewing distance of **30–40 cm**.
  - Symbol size must scale with the **Smallest Meaningful Display (SMD)** principle.
  - High contrast ratios (**≥ 4.5:1** for WCAG AA compliance).

### Layer 2 — Cartographic Adaptation Layer
- **Concerns**: Generalization, symbolization, and labelling adapted for mobile.
- **Key Concepts**:
  - **Dynamic Generalization** — real-time simplification of geometry as zoom level changes.
  - **On-demand Theming** — switching basemaps (e.g., day/night, traffic, terrain) based on context.
  - **Adaptive Labelling** — labels appear only when their feature's pixel size exceeds a threshold.

### Layer 3 — Spatial Computation Layer
- **Concerns**: Coordinate transformations, projection handling, real-time geolocation, route computation.
- **Operations**:
  - Web Mercator projection (**EPSG:3857**) is the de facto standard for mobile slippy maps.
  - Real-time positioning via **A-GPS (Assisted GPS)**, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou.
  - **Geofencing** — triggering events when the user enters a defined polygon.

### Layer 4 — Network & Data Layer
- **Concerns**: Tiled delivery, caching, offline support, vector vs raster decisions.
- **Tile Pyramids** — pre-rendered raster tiles organized by **z (zoom)**, **x (column)**, **y (row)** following the **XYZ tile scheme** or **TMS (Tile Map Service)** scheme.
- **Vector Tiles** — encoded as **Mapbox Vector Tile (MVT)** or **3D Tiles** for high-fidelity rendering on-device.

## 2.2 Core Mobile Cartography Equations & Relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following relationships are **frequently tested** in KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorize the variables, the equation form, and the boundary conditions.

### 2.2.1 Screen Pixel Density (PPI)
$$ \text{PPI} = \frac{\sqrt{W_p^{\,2} + H_p^{\,2}}}{D_{\text{diag}}} $$
where $W_p$ is the screen width in pixels, $H_p$ is the screen height in pixels, and $D_{\text{diag}}$ is the diagonal screen size in **inches**.

### 2.2.2 Map Scale at Given Zoom Level (Web Mercator Slippy Map)
$$ \text{Scale} \approx \frac{C}{2^z \cdot \text{PPI}} $$
where:
- $C$ = circumference of the Earth at the equator ≈ **40,075,016.686 m**,
- $z$ = slippy map zoom level (**0 to 22** for most providers),
- PPI = device pixel density.

> [!NOTE]
> At zoom $z$, the world is divided into $2^z \times 2^z$ tiles. Each tile is **256 × 256 px** (standard) or **512 × 512 px** (high-resolution).

### 2.2.3 Ground Resolution Per Pixel
$$ R_{\text{ground}} = \frac{C \cdot \cos(\phi)}{2^{z+8}} $$
where $\phi$ is the latitude (in radians) and $C$ is the equatorial circumference. The factor $2^8 = 256$ comes from the standard tile size of 256 px.

### 2.2.4 Effective Field of View on a Mobile Screen
The **Field of View (FoV)** in degrees for a mobile map at a given zoom level is:
$$ \text{FoV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{S_h}{2 \cdot D_v}\right) $$
where $S_h$ is the **screen height** in metres at the user's viewing distance, and $D_v$ is the viewing distance (typically **0.30 m to 0.40 m** for handheld use).

### 2.2.5 Generalized Stroke Width
For mobile map line features, the on-screen stroke width $w_s$ (in pixels) is:
$$ w_s = w_r \cdot k_{\text{scale}} \cdot k_{\text{device}} $$
where $w_r$ is the reference stroke width at the design scale, $k_{\text{scale}}$ is the scale-change factor, and $k_{\text{device}}$ is the device-specific factor (≈ **1.5× to 2×** of desktop web for legibility).

### 2.2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Key Variables | Typical Units | Boundary / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pixel Density | $\text{PPI} = \sqrt{W_p^2 + H_p^2} / D_{\text{diag}}$ | $W_p, H_p, D_{\text{diag}}$ | pixels / inch | $D_{\text{diag}} \in [1, 7]$ in |
| 2 | Slippy Zoom → Scale | $\text{Scale} \approx C / (2^z \cdot \text{PPI})$ | $C, z, \text{PPI}$ | unitless ratio | $z \in [0, 22]$ |
| 3 | Ground Resolution | $R_{\text{ground}} = C \cos(\phi) / 2^{z+8}$ | $C, \phi, z$ | m / pixel | 256-px tile assumed |
| 4 | Tiles per Zoom | $N_{\text{tiles}} = 2^z \times 2^z$ | $z$ | count | doubles per zoom step |
| 5 | Field of View | $\text{FoV} = 2 \arctan(S_h / (2 D_v))$ | $S_h, D_v$ | degrees | $D_v \approx 0.35$ m |
| 6 | Stroke Width | $w_s = w_r \cdot k_{\text{scale}} \cdot k_{\text{device}}$ | $w_r, k_{\text{scale}}, k_{\text{device}}$ | pixels | $w_s \ge 1$ px |
| 7 | Tile Size | $T_s \in \{256, 512\}$ | — | px | standard / retina |
| 8 | Geofence Trigger Radius | $r_{\text{trigger}} = f(\text{GPS accuracy})$ | — | metres | typical $\ge 10$ m |

### 2.3 Why Mobile Cartography Demands a New Cartographic Theory

Traditional cartography assumed a *fixed viewing distance*, *static user position*, *constant scale*, and *single display medium*. Mobile cartography breaks all four assumptions:

1. **Variable viewing distance** — phone in hand vs. mounted on a bike vs. on a watch.
2. **Dynamic user position** — the map must recentre continuously.
3. **Continuously changing scale** — pinch-to-zoom is routine; design must hold across all zoom levels.
4. **Heterogeneous displays** — from 1.4-inch smartwatch to 12.9-inch tablet.

This has led to the development of **Adaptive Cartography** — a core research theme in modern KTU-aligned curriculum.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where Mobile Cartography is applied in industry (and may appear in KTU viva/project viva):**
> - **Ride-hailing** (Ola, Uber, Rapido) — driver & rider maps with real-time ETAs.
> - **Logistics & Field Force Automation** — delivery tracking, last-mile route optimization.
> - **Tourism & AR Wayfinding** — Pokémon GO, Google Live View, AR navigation.
> - **Disaster Management** — field crews using offline mobile maps in low-connectivity zones.
> - **Defence & Tactical Operations** — soldier-worn digital maps.
> - **Public Health** — disease surveillance and contact-tracing maps.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Mobile Cartography is a concept-heavy subject. This section converts the most-tested equations from Section 2 into **fully worked-out derivations** with explanatory text on every transition. KTU board examiners reward students who can **show the chain of logic**, not just the final answer.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ground Resolution Per Pixel ($R_{\text{ground}}$)

**Goal:** Show that the ground distance represented by a single pixel at zoom $z$ and latitude $\phi$ is
$$ R_{\text{ground}} = \frac{C \cdot \cos(\phi)}{2^{z+8}} \quad \text{(metres / pixel)}. $$

### Step 1 — Define the world at zoom zero
At the lowest zoom level $z = 0$, the entire Earth fits into a single **256 × 256 px** tile. So the world is mapped onto a square of $256 \times 256$ pixels.

> The full equatorial circumference of the Earth is $C \approx 40{,}075{,}016.686$ m.

### Step 2 — Compute metres per pixel at $z = 0$
The horizontal extent of one pixel at the equator (where $\phi = 0$, so $\cos \phi = 1$) is:
$$ R_0 \;=\; \frac{C}{256} \;=\; \frac{40{,}075{,}016.686}{256} \;\approx\; 156{,}543.034 \text{ m / px}. $$

### Step 3 — Apply latitude correction
At any latitude $\phi$, a degree of longitude (and hence a horizontal pixel) is shorter by a factor of $\cos \phi$ than at the equator. So:
$$ R(\phi) \;=\; \frac{C \cdot \cos \phi}{256} \quad \text{(m / pixel at z = 0)}. $$

### Step 4 — Account for zoom halving
At each zoom step $z \rightarrow z+1$, the linear resolution of the map **doubles**. That means each pixel now represents **half** the ground distance of the previous zoom level.

> In general, halving occurs $z$ times starting from $z=0$.

Therefore:
$$ R_{\text{ground}}(z, \phi) \;=\; \frac{C \cdot \cos \phi}{256 \cdot 2^z}. $$

### Step 5 — Combine constants
Since $256 = 2^8$, we can rewrite:
$$ R_{\text{ground}}(z, \phi) \;=\; \frac{C \cdot \cos \phi}{2^{z+8}}. $$

This is the standard **ground resolution per pixel** equation used by Google Maps, OpenStreetMap, and Mapbox. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Map Scale from Zoom Level

**Goal:** Derive the relationship
$$ \text{Scale} \approx \frac{C}{2^z \cdot \text{PPI}} $$
for a mobile slippy map.

### Step 1 — Recall map scale definition
Map scale is the ratio of a distance **on the map** to the corresponding distance **on the ground**:
$$ \text{Scale} \;=\; \frac{d_{\text{map}}}{d_{\text{ground}}}. $$

### Step 2 — Express the world in pixels
At zoom $z$, the entire Earth (equatorial circumference $C$) is represented by $2^z \cdot T_s$ pixels horizontally, where $T_s$ is the standard tile size (256 px).

So the world spans:
$$ W_{\text{px}} \;=\; T_s \cdot 2^z \;=\; 256 \cdot 2^z. $$

### Step 3 — Convert pixels to physical inches
The screen has PPI pixels per inch, so the world spans:
$$ W_{\text{inches}} \;=\; \frac{256 \cdot 2^z}{\text{PPI}} \text{ inches.} $$

### Step 4 — Form the scale ratio
$$ \text{Scale} \;=\; \frac{W_{\text{inches}} \text{ (in)}}{C \text{ (m)}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Scale} \;\approx\; \frac{256 \cdot 2^z}{\text{PPI} \cdot C}. $$

### Step 5 — Simplify (using $256 = 2^8$)
$$ \text{Scale} \;\approx\; \frac{2^{z+8}}{\text{PPI} \cdot C}. $$

This gives the **representative fraction** of the slippy map at zoom $z$ on a device with PPI pixels per inch.

> [!TIP]
> KTU **numerical example**: For a 6.1-inch phone with PPI ≈ 460 at zoom $z = 15$:
> $$\text{Scale} \;\approx\; \frac{2^{23}}{460 \cdot 4.0075 \times 10^7} \;\approx\; \frac{8.39 \times 10^6}{1.84 \times 10^{10}} \;\approx\; 4.55 \times 10^{-4}.$$
> That is, **1 unit on screen ≈ 2.2 km on ground** — typical city-block zoom.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Field of View (FoV) on a Mobile Screen

**Goal:** Show
$$ \text{FoV} \;=\; 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{S_h}{2 D_v}\right). $$

### Step 1 — Geometry of viewing
Consider the user holding the phone at distance $D_v$ from their eyes. The screen has a vertical extent of $S_h$. Half of the screen subtends an angle $\theta$ at the eye:
$$ \tan \theta \;=\; \frac{S_h / 2}{D_v}. $$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\theta$
$$ \theta \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\frac{S_h}{2 D_v}\right). $$

### Step 3 — Double for full FoV
The full vertical field of view is:
$$ \text{FoV} \;=\; 2\theta \;=\; 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{S_h}{2 D_v}\right). $$

### Step 4 — Numerical check
A 6.1-inch phone held at $D_v = 0.35$ m has a vertical screen extent of $S_h \approx 0.135$ m. So:
$$ \text{FoV} \;=\; 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.135}{0.70}\right) \;\approx\; 2 \cdot 10.93^\circ \;\approx\; 21.86^\circ. $$

This matches published ergonomic data for comfortable handheld viewing. ✅

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis (Frequently Asked in KTU)

The following matrix compares **Desktop Web Cartography vs. Mobile Cartography**. KTU Part B questions often ask students to write such a comparison as a 7-mark sub-question.

| Dimension | Desktop Web Cartography | Mobile Cartography |
|---|---|---|
| **Screen size** | 13″–32″ | 1.4″–6.9″ (wearable to phablet) |
| **Viewing distance** | 50–70 cm | 25–40 cm |
| **Input modality** | Mouse + keyboard | Touch + gestures + voice |
| **User mobility** | Stationary | Highly mobile (walking, driving) |
| **Network** | Broadband / wired | Cellular (4G/5G), Wi-Fi, intermittent |
| **Map context** | Often task-focused, sitting | **Continuous real-time** (turn-by-turn, AR) |
| **Generalization strategy** | Pre-generalized static tiles | **Dynamic on-device** generalization |
| **Symbol size** | Smaller (more detail) | **Enlarged 1.5×–2×** for legibility |
| **Typography** | Small fonts acceptable | Minimum **12 px** recommended |
| **Positioning** | Optional / coarse | **Mandatory** (GPS, fused sensors) |
| **Offline support** | Rare | **Critical** (field work, disaster response) |
| **Power constraints** | Mains powered | **Battery-limited** ⇒ cache aggressively |
| **Cartographic attention** | Long-duration study | **Glanceable** (< 5 s per look) |

---

## 3.5 Mobile Cartography Workflow — Step-by-Step Engineering Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the canonical KTU **"Explain the workflow of mobile cartography"** answer outline. Use it as a 14-mark answer skeleton.

1. **Data acquisition** — Vector/raster data ingested into a Spatial DBMS (PostGIS, SpatiaLite).
2. **Tile generation** — Server pre-renders raster tiles or encodes vector tiles (MVT).
3. **Tile storage** — Tiles cached on CDN (Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront).
4. **Client request** — Mobile app requests tiles $z/x/y$ based on viewport.
5. **Network transfer** — Tiles delivered over HTTP/HTTPS, often with **HTTP/2 push** or **Protocol Buffers** for vectors.
6. **On-device rendering** — Mobile Map SDK (Mapbox GL, MapLibre, Google Maps) renders tiles using GPU.
7. **Sensor fusion** — GPS, IMU, barometer, magnetometer data combined for orientation and location.
8. **User interaction** — Touch gestures (pan, pinch-zoom, rotate, two-finger tilt) update the viewport.
9. **Adaptive generalization** — At low zoom, geometry simplified on the fly; at high zoom, full detail rendered.
10. **Contextual layer** — Real-time overlays (traffic, weather, geofences) streamed via **WebSocket / MQTT**.
11. **Display output** — Final composite rendered to OLED/LCD with appropriate projection (Web Mercator).
12. **Analytics & feedback** — Anonymous usage telemetry informs future cartographic design.

This 12-step pipeline is the **gold standard** for a 14-mark descriptive KTU answer.
