# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q:** Define a **Spatial Database**. List any two differences between a spatial database and a conventional relational database.

**Model Answer:**

A **spatial database** is a database that has been extended with capabilities to store, index, query, and manipulate **spatial/geometric data** (points, lines, polygons) and their **non-spatial attributes** within a single integrated system, while maintaining topological relationships and supporting spatial operators (e.g., intersect, buffer, distance).

| Aspect | Conventional RDBMS | Spatial Database |
|---|---|---|
| **Data types** | Numbers, text, dates, BLOBs | Adds `GEOMETRY`, `GEOGRAPHY`, `RASTER` |
| **Indexes** | B-tree, hash | Adds **R-tree**, GiST, Quad-tree |
| **Queries** | `=`, `<`, `LIKE` | Adds `ST_Intersects`, `ST_DWithin`, `ST_Buffer` |
| **Reference system** | Not required | Every geometry carries an **SRID** |
| **Topology** | Not supported | Supports topological rules and relationships |

*Any two of the above differences are sufficient for 3 marks.*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **Q:** What is the role of a **spatial index** in a GIS database? Name any two types.

**Model Answer:**

A **spatial index** is a data structure that organises geometric features in a way that dramatically accelerates spatial queries (e.g., "find all features within 1 km of this point"). Without a spatial index, every query would require a full table scan — extremely slow for large datasets (millions of features).

It works by pre-computing **bounding boxes** (or cells) and grouping nearby features, so the query engine can skip whole regions that cannot contain matching results.

**Two types of spatial indexes:**
1. **R-Tree** — hierarchical bounding-rectangle tree (used in PostGIS GiST, Oracle Spatial, ArcGIS).
2. **Quad-Tree** — recursive four-way spatial subdivision (used in MapInfo TAB files).

*Other valid types: Grid index, BRIN, k-d tree, Hilbert curve.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **ACID properties** of database transactions. Illustrate their importance in a GIS context with one example.
>
> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** With suitable diagrams, describe the **R-Tree spatial index**. State its advantages over a grid index.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**ACID Properties (full form + GIS meaning):**

| Letter | Property | Meaning | GIS Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | **Atomicity** | A transaction is a single, indivisible unit — either *all* operations succeed or *none* are applied. | Splitting a parcel into two: both new polygons are inserted **and** the old polygon is deleted — or none of it happens. |
| **C** | **Consistency** | The DB moves from one valid state to another; all defined rules (constraints, topology) remain satisfied. | A road layer with topology rules: a transaction cannot create a road that violates "no overlapping segments." |
| **D** | **Durability** | Once a transaction is committed, its result is permanent — survives crashes, power loss. | After a survey team records new ward boundaries in the field, the data remains on the central server even if the mobile device is later lost. |
| **I** | **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions do not see each other's uncommitted intermediate states. | Two surveyors editing different villages simultaneously don't overwrite each other's edits. |

**Detailed GIS Example — Cadastral Update Workflow:**

A government officer splits parcel **P-101** (1.0 ha) into two new parcels **P-101A** (0.4 ha) and **P-101B** (0.6 ha). This requires:

1. `INSERT` new polygon P-101A into the parcel table.
2. `INSERT` new polygon P-101B into the parcel table.
3. `DELETE` old polygon P-101.
4. `UPDATE` the parent `Owner` table to register co-ownership.
5. `UPDATE` the village `land_use` aggregate area.

If the network fails after step 2 but before step 3, **atomicity** rolls back the inserts — there is never a moment with "extra" area. If two officers try to split the same parcel simultaneously, **isolation** ensures only one succeeds, and the other must retry. After commit, **durability** guarantees the change is permanent.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**R-Tree Spatial Index — Explanation with Diagram:**

An **R-Tree** (Rectangle Tree) is a hierarchical, balanced tree index designed for spatial data. Each node corresponds to a **Minimum Bounding Rectangle (MBR)** that tightly encloses all child geometries or sub-MBRs.

**Tree structure:**
- **Root node:** single MBR covering the entire dataset.
- **Internal nodes:** MBRs that enclose groups of child MBRs.
- **Leaf nodes:** MBRs of individual features (or small groups) with pointers to the actual geometry in the data tables.

**Step-by-step working (search example):**

Suppose a user runs: *"Find all parcels that intersect this query window Q."*

1. **Start at root:** test whether Q overlaps the root MBR. If not → empty result, exit. If yes → continue.
2. **Descend to children:** for each child MBR that overlaps Q, recurse.
3. **At leaf level:** for each feature whose MBR overlaps Q, perform an **exact geometry test** (using `ST_Intersects`) to confirm.
4. **Return matching features.**

This is called **MBR filtering followed by exact geometry test** — it prunes the search space drastically.

**Diagram (drawn in answer script):**

```
[Root MBR]
   /                      \
[MBR-North]              [MBR-South]
   /    \                  /    \
P1 P2 P3 P4           P5 P6 P7 P8
```

**Advantages of R-Tree over Grid Index:**

| Aspect | R-Tree | Grid Index |
|---|---|---|
| **Adaptivity** | MBRs adjust to data density; dense clusters get small MBRs | Fixed cell size; may have many empty cells or many overlapping features in one cell |
| **Storage efficiency** | Compact, no empty cells | Many empty cells in sparse regions |
| **Query performance on irregular data** | Excellent — only traverses overlapping branches | Wastes time on irrelevant cells |
| **Handles overlapping/clusters** | Naturally groups nearby features | One feature may belong to many cells (overlap on cell boundary) |
| **Update cost** | Moderate (re-balancing) | Low (just move feature to new cell) |

**Conclusion:** R-Tree is the **de-facto standard** for vector spatial indexes in modern GIS databases (PostGIS, Oracle Spatial, SQL Server, ArcGIS).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Differentiate between **spatial data** and **attribute (non-spatial) data** in a GIS. List the OGC spatial data types and explain any two.
>
> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the **components of a DBMS**. Explain the role of a **Database Administrator (DBA)**.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Difference between spatial and attribute data:**

| Aspect | Spatial Data | Attribute Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Describes the **location, shape, and geometry** of features | Describes the **properties/qualities** of features |
| **Format** | Coordinates, geometry types (point/line/polygon) | Numbers, text, dates, booleans |
| **Examples** | `geom = POLYGON((76.26 9.93, ...))` | `population = 1,200,000` |
| **Storage** | Geometry column (e.g., `SHAPE`, `geom`) | Standard columns (e.g., `VARCHAR`, `INTEGER`) |
| **Indexing** | R-tree, GiST | B-tree, hash |
| **Question answered** | *Where? What shape?* | *What? How much? When?* |
| **Standard** | OGC SFS (Simple Features) | ISO SQL data types |

**OGC Spatial Data Types (full list):**

```
Point, LineString, Polygon,
MultiPoint, MultiLineString, MultiPolygon,
GeometryCollection,
PolyhedralSurface, TIN, Triangle
```

**Explanation of two types:**

1. **Point (0-D):** A single coordinate pair (x, y), optionally with elevation (z) and measure (m). It represents **discrete locations** with no length or area — e.g., a well, bus stop, GPS sample, incident location. In PostGIS: `POINT(76.2673 9.9312)`.

2. **Polygon (2-D, filled):** A closed planar region bounded by one **outer ring** and zero or more **inner rings** (holes). It represents areas with topological identity — e.g., a district, a lake, a cadastral parcel. In PostGIS: `POLYGON((0 0, 10 0, 10 10, 0 10, 0 0), (3 3, 7 3, 7 7, 3 7, 3 3))` — the second ring is a hole.

*Alternative explanation can be given for LineString, MultiPolygon, etc.*

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Five Components of a DBMS:**

| # | Component | Role | Example in Spatial DB |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hardware** | Physical server, storage, network | 64 GB RAM server with SSD, hosted at KSWAN data centre |
| 2 | **Software** | DBMS engine, OS, network | PostgreSQL 15 + PostGIS 3.4 on CentOS Linux |
| 3 | **Data** | Operational + metadata | District polygons, road lines, attribute tables; `geometry_columns` metadata |
| 4 | **Procedures** | Rules for use, backup, security | "Daily 02:00 backup", "Read-only for public users" |
| 5 | **People** | Users + administrators | DBAs, GIS analysts, developers, surveyors |

**Role of a Database Administrator (DBA):**

A DBA is the custodian of the database. Key responsibilities include:

1. **Installation and configuration** of the DBMS (e.g., tuning `postgresql.conf` for spatial workloads).
2. **Creating and managing user accounts**, granting privileges (`GRANT SELECT ON TABLE districts TO PUBLIC`).
3. **Schema management** — defining tables, geometry columns, SRIDs, constraints.
4. **Performance tuning** — building indexes (`CREATE INDEX ... USING GIST(geom)`), analysing query plans (`EXPLAIN ANALYZE`).
5. **Backup and recovery** — periodic `pg_dump`, point-in-time recovery using WAL.
6. **Security** — SSL, role-based access, audit logging.
7. **Monitoring** — disk usage, slow queries, lock contention.
8. **Ensuring data integrity** — checking topology, validating geometries with `ST_IsValid`.
9. **Capacity planning** — forecasting storage growth (a 1-m resolution raster of Kerala ≈ 38 GB; Lidar point clouds can be TBs).
10. **Coordination with developers** — query optimization, ETL pipelines.

**In a Kerala GIS context:** A DBA at KSDMA (Kerala State Disaster Management Authority) maintains the state hazard-zonation spatial database, ensuring first responders always have access to up-to-date flood-prone polygons.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "DBMS" and "Database"** — DBMS is the *software*; database is the *data*. Mixing them = -2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention SRID** — every spatial column is incomplete without an SRID. Writing only `GEOMETRY` instead of `GEOMETRY(POLYGON, 4326)` = -1 mark.
> 3. **Writing `ACID` without expanding** — examiners expect "Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability" in full. Acronym alone = -1 mark.
> 4. **Drawing an R-Tree without labelling the MBRs** — examiners check whether you understand the bounding rectangles, not just the boxes.
> 5. **Vague DBA role** — "manages the database" is too generic. Use *specific verbs*: install, grant, backup, tune, monitor, validate.
> 6. **Omitting units in formulas** — e.g., writing "d = 173" without "km" or "m" is marked down for not showing engineering rigour.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Databases and Spatial Databases**

### A. Must-Know Definitions (3 each = 1 mark × 4 questions minimum)

- **Database:** an organized, persistent collection of related data managed by a DBMS.
- **DBMS:** the software that lets us define, store, manipulate, retrieve, and secure data.
- **Spatial Database:** a DBMS that natively handles geometry + attributes + spatial queries.
- **Geodatabase (Esri term):** a multi-user spatial database built on RDBMS with topology, network, raster support.
- **OGC Simple Features:** the international standard for spatial data types and functions.
- **SRID:** Spatial Reference Identifier — a numeric code identifying the coordinate system.
- **Spatial Index:** a data structure (R-tree, Quad-tree, Grid) for fast geometric queries.
- **MBR:** Minimum Bounding Rectangle enclosing a geometry for index acceleration.
- **Topology:** the spatial relationships (adjacency, containment, connectivity) preserved between features.
- **Transaction:** a logical unit of database work, governed by ACID.

### B. Must-Know Lists

- **5 components of DBMS:** Hardware, Software, Data, Procedures, People.
- **4 ACID properties:** Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.
- **3 relationship types:** 1:1, 1:N, M:N.
- **5 spatial-data capabilities of a Spatial DBMS:** spatial types, SRS, spatial index, spatial functions, topology.
- **Standard OGC geometry types:** Point, LineString, Polygon, MultiPoint, MultiLineString, MultiPolygon, GeometryCollection, (TIN, PolyhedralSurface for 3-D).
- **Common spatial indexes:** R-tree, Quad-tree, Grid, k-d tree, BRIN, GiST.

### C. Must-Know Formulas

- Haversine spherical distance.
- Shoelace polygon area.
- Helmert 2-D transform.
- Quad-tree depth: $D = \lceil \log_2(L/\ell) \rceil$.
- $R_e = 6{,}371{,}008.8$ m.

### D. Must-Know Software

- **PostgreSQL + PostGIS** (open-source, industry standard).
- **Oracle Spatial / Oracle Locator** (enterprise).
- **SQL Server** with spatial types.
- **MySQL** (basic spatial, limited).
- **SpatiaLite** (file-based spatialite extension of SQLite — used for mobile/field).
- **Esri Geodatabase** (File GDB, Enterprise GDB on SQL Server/Oracle/PostgreSQL).
- **GeoPackage** (OGC standard, SQLite-based, lightweight).

### E. Must-Know Real-World Kerala Context

- **Kerala State Spatial Data Infrastructure (KSSDI)** — state-level spatial DB initiative.
- **Kerala Forest Department** — uses spatial DB for forest cover, encroachment monitoring.
- **Kerala Land Records (Bhu-Keralam)** — village-level parcel database.
- **KSDMA** — multi-hazard vulnerability maps stored spatially.
- **Smart Cities (Kochi, Trivandrum)** — utility network spatial DB for water, power, sewage.

### F. Common KTU Exam Traps

- Saying "spatial database = database with maps" — wrong. It's a database that **understands geometry** mathematically.
- Confusing R-tree (rectangles) with Quad-tree (quadrants).
- Forgetting that spatial data + attribute data are stored **in the same row** of one table.
- Treating SRID as optional — it is **mandatory** for any meaningful spatial data.

> **Final One-Liner for Answer-Sheet Recall:**
> *"A spatial database is a relational database that has learned geometry — it stores shapes, knows the coordinate system, indexes space, and can answer 'where' questions in milliseconds."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geographic Phenomena: Fields, Objects and Boundaries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET414 - CARTOGRAPHY AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Geographic Phenomena: Fields, Objects and Boundaries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Geographic Information Science (GISci), a **geographic phenomenon** refers to any spatially distributed variable, entity, or process that exists at, near, or above the Earth's surface and can be measured, observed, or modeled. The KTU 2024 scheme classifies these phenomena into three canonical conceptualizations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A **geographic phenomenon** is any real-world geographic feature or variable that has a position in space and time, and which can be represented in a GIS using one of the two fundamental data models — the **field view** (continuous surface) or the **object view** (discrete entity). **Boundaries** are the spatial delineations that separate one phenomenon (or its components) from another.

| Conceptualization | Spatial Nature | Measurement Scale | Classic Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Field (Continuous)** | Spatially continuous, no defined edges | Ratio / Interval | Atmospheric pressure, soil moisture |
| **Object (Discrete)** | Spatially discrete, with well-defined extent | Nominal / Ordinal | Buildings, roads, lakes |
| **Boundary** | Linear/edge feature (sharp or fuzzy) | Various | Coastline, property line, ecotone |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

Think of a geographic landscape the way a painter might think about a canvas:

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Two Lenses on Geography**
> - **The Field View = A Heatmap on a Thermostat:** Imagine you walk across Kerala during monsoon. The temperature doesn't suddenly "jump" from 32°C to 18°C; it changes *smoothly* with elevation, distance from the coast, and forest cover. A field models this smooth, everywhere-defined variation. There is no "edge" — the value exists at every point (x, y).
> - **The Object View = LEGO Bricks on a Floor:** Now consider the houses, temples, and bus stops in a town. Each one is a *separable thing* with a clear footprint. You can pick one up without disturbing the others. An object model treats geography as a collection of these independent, countable "bricks."
> - **The Boundary = The Fence Between Two Yards:** Boundaries are the geometry that *separates* fields or *encloses* objects. Some fences are sharp (a wall), others are fuzzy (the misty edge of a forest). Understanding boundaries is the difference between a legal land record and a fuzzy ecological gradient.

## 1.3 Why This Distinction Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners repeatedly test the student's ability to **choose the correct conceptualization** for a given problem, because the *data model* (raster vs. vector), the *storage structure*, the *analytical operations*, and the *map symbology* all depend on whether a phenomenon is perceived as a field, an object, or a boundary. Getting this wrong costs **3 to 5 marks** on a typical Part B question.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Field View (Continuous Phenomena)

A **geographic field** is a mathematical structure in which a property $z$ varies as a continuous function of position over a region of 2-D (or 3-D) space:

$$z = f(x, y)$$

The defining property of a field is that the variable has a **definite value at every point** in the domain — there are no gaps, no "empty" cells, and no discrete edges.

### 2.1.1 Two Sub-Forms of Fields

- **Scalar Field:** A single numeric value at each location. Example: elevation (m), rainfall (mm), barometric pressure (hPa).
- **Vector Field:** Both magnitude *and* direction at each location. Example: wind velocity, ocean current, magnetic declination.

For a vector field, the value is expressed as:

$$\vec{V}(x,y) = u(x,y)\,\hat{i} + v(x,y)\,\hat{j}$$

where $u$ and $v$ are the easting and northing components.

### 2.1.2 Characteristics of Fields

- **Continuous** in space and (often) in value
- **Scale-dependent**: a 30 m grid shows different field structure than a 1 km grid
- **Areal interpolation** is required to estimate values between samples
- **Best represented as raster** (grid) or **TIN** (for terrain)

### 2.1.3 Representation Methods for Fields

| Method | Description | Best Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Raster (Grid)** | Regular square cells, each holding a value | Continuous environmental data |
| **TIN (Triangulated Irregular Network)** | Triangulated irregular nodes for terrain | Elevation, slope, aspect |
| **Contours (Isarithms)** | Lines of constant value | Topographic maps, isobars |
| **Point sampling + interpolation** | Measured points, value estimated at unsampled locations | Climate stations, soil pits |

### 2.1.4 Spatial Autocorrelation — Tobler's First Law

> *"Everything is related to everything else, but near things are more related than distant things."* — **Waldo Tobler (1970)**

This is formalized through **Moran's I**, a measure of spatial autocorrelation for fields:

$$I = \frac{n}{W} \cdot \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{ij}(x_i - \bar{x})(x_j - \bar{x})}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

where $n$ is the number of locations, $x_i$ and $x_j$ are the field values, $\bar{x}$ is the mean, $w_{ij}$ is the spatial weight between locations $i$ and $j$, and $W = \sum_{i,j} w_{ij}$ is the sum of all weights.

- $I > 0$ → positive autocorrelation (similar values cluster)
- $I \approx 0$ → random spatial pattern
- $I < 0$ → negative autocorrelation (checkerboard pattern)

---

## 2.2 The Object View (Discrete Phenomena)

An **object** (or **entity**) is a geographically bounded, named feature that can be counted and has a clear identity. The defining property is **discreteness** — the world is a collection of separable things, each with attributes.

### 2.2.1 Primitives of the Object View

| Geometric Primitive | Dimension | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Point** | 0-D | A well, a bus stop, a survey monument |
| **Line** | 1-D | A road, a river, a power line |
| **Polygon (Area)** | 2-D | A land parcel, a lake, a district |

The decision to use point, line, or polygon depends on **scale and purpose**. A city shown on a 1:1,000,000 map is a point; on a 1:10,000 map it is a polygon. KTU examiners frequently test this scale-dependency.

### 2.2.2 Characteristics of Objects

- Have a **definite boundary** (in the object view, it is *sharp* by assumption)
- Are **countable** and **identifiable**
- Possess **attributes** stored in tables
- Are **best represented as vector** data (points, lines, polygons)
- May have **hierarchical** relationships (a state contains districts contains villages)

### 2.2.3 Object Identity & Topology

Objects are given a unique **Object ID** and maintain **topological relationships** such as:
- **Adjacency** (which parcels share a boundary?)
- **Containment** (which villages lie within this district?)
- **Connectivity** (which road segments meet at this junction?)

---

## 2.3 Boundaries — The Edges of Geography

A **boundary** is a 1-D or 2-D spatial delimiter. KTU 2024 emphasizes the distinction between **sharp** and **fuzzy (indeterminate)** boundaries, and between **physical** and **conceptual** boundaries.

### 2.3.1 Sharp (Determinate) Boundaries
- Have a precise legal or geometric position
- Examples: cadastral parcel lines, state borders, walls
- Suitable for vector polygon representation
- The boundary has *zero width* on the map

### 2.3.2 Fuzzy (Indeterminate) Boundaries
- Transition zones rather than lines
- Examples: a forest edge, an ecotone, a coastline, the boundary of a slum
- Represented by **membership functions** in fuzzy set theory:

$$\mu_A(x,y) : \mathbb{R}^2 \to [0,1]$$

where $\mu_A = 1$ means full membership in set $A$ (e.g., "fully inside the forest") and $\mu_A = 0$ means full non-membership. Values in between capture the *fuzzy* transition zone.

### 2.3.3 The Coastline Paradox (KTU Favorite!)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandelbrot's Coastline Paradox (1967):**
> The measured length of a coastline depends on the scale of measurement. As the measuring stick becomes infinitesimally small, the measured length approaches infinity. This demonstrates that **fuzzy boundaries have no single "true" length** — it is a *function of scale*.

For a Koch snowflake-like coastline measured with a stick of length $\varepsilon$:

$$L(\varepsilon) = L_0 \cdot \varepsilon^{1-D}$$

where $D$ is the **fractal dimension** of the coastline ($1 < D < 2$).

### 2.3.4 Common Boundary Types in KTU Syllabus

| Type | Nature | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural** | Physical | River, mountain ridge, shoreline |
| **Artificial** | Human-made | State border, cadastral line |
| **Sharp** | Deterministic | Wall, fence, property line |
| **Fuzzy** | Indeterminate | Forest edge, climate zone transition |
| **Conjugate** | Pair shared by two regions | Common border of two countries |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas before any Module-3 question. They appear almost every semester.**

| Concept | Formula | Variable Notes | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Field value at a point | $z = f(x,y)$ | $x,y$ = planar coordinates | — |
| Vector field component | $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j}$ | $u,v$ = components | m/s, km/h |
| Spatial weight (inverse distance) | $w_{ij} = 1 \mid d_{ij} \neq 0$ | $d_{ij}$ = distance | dimensionless |
| Moran's I | $I = \frac{n}{W}\frac{\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}(x_i-\bar x)(x_j-\bar x)}{\sum_i(x_i-\bar x)^2}$ | see text above | dimensionless, $[-1,1]$ |
| Raster cell area | $A_{cell} = \Delta x \cdot \Delta y$ | $\Delta x, \Delta y$ = cell size | m², km² |
| Number of raster cells | $N = \lfloor W/\Delta x \rfloor \cdot \lfloor H/\Delta y \rfloor$ | $W,H$ = extent | dimensionless |
| TIN area (one triangle) | $A_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert (x_2-x_1)(y_3-y_1) - (x_3-x_1)(y_2-y_1) \vert$ | vertex coordinates | m² |
| Coastline (fractal) | $L(\varepsilon) = L_0 \cdot \varepsilon^{1-D}$ | $D$ = fractal dim., $\varepsilon$ = step | m |
| Fractal dimension (box-counting) | $D = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0}\frac{\log N(\varepsilon)}{\log(1/\varepsilon)}$ | $N(\varepsilon)$ = boxes covered | dimensionless |
| Scale ratio | $SR = \text{Map Distance} \mid \text{Ground Distance}$ | e.g., 1:50,000 | dimensionless |
| Map distance from ground | $d_{map} = d_{ground} \cdot SR$ | both linear | m, cm |
| Fuzzy membership (Gaussian) | $\mu(x) = e^{-(x-c)^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$ | $c$ = core, $\sigma$ = spread | $[0,1]$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table cell. The pipe character will be parsed as a column separator and break the table. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead, as shown above.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & GIS

- **Fields** drive environmental engineering: flood modeling, pollution dispersion, slope-stability analysis.
- **Objects** drive cadastral engineering, utility mapping (water lines, manholes), and asset management.
- **Boundaries** drive jurisdictional analysis, land-use planning, and ecological zonation.
- **Fuzzy boundaries** are critical in climate-change science, where species ranges, sea-level rise, and pollution plumes have *gradient* rather than sharp edges.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Raster Cell Count and Storage Sizing for a Field

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A raster field covers an extent of 60 km × 45 km with a spatial resolution of 30 m. Calculate (a) the number of cells, (b) the ground area each cell represents, (c) the total storage size in Megabytes if each cell holds a 32-bit floating-point elevation value.

### Step 1 — Convert extent to meters
The extent is given in km, so convert to meters (SI base unit):

$$W = 60\ \text{km} = 60 \times 1000 = 60{,}000\ \text{m}$$

$$H = 45\ \text{km} = 45 \times 1000 = 45{,}000\ \text{m}$$

### Step 2 — Convert resolution to meters
Resolution $\Delta x = \Delta y = 30$ m (square cell).

### Step 3 — Compute number of cells (rows × columns)
Number of columns:

$$N_x = \left\lfloor \frac{W}{\Delta x} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{60{,}000}{30} \right\rfloor = 2{,}000\ \text{cells}$$

Number of rows:

$$N_y = \left\lfloor \frac{H}{\Delta y} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{45{,}000}{30} \right\rfloor = 1{,}500\ \text{cells}$$

Total cells:

$$N = N_x \times N_y = 2{,}000 \times 1{,}500 = 3{,}000{,}000\ \text{cells}$$

### Step 4 — Area per cell

$$A_{cell} = \Delta x \cdot \Delta y = 30 \times 30 = 900\ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 5 — Convert cell area to a more useful unit (hectares)
$1$ hectare = $10{,}000$ m², so:

$$A_{cell} = \frac{900}{10{,}000} = 0.09\ \text{ha}$$

### Step 6 — Total storage in bytes
Each cell is 32-bit (4 bytes). Total bytes:

$$B = N \times 4 = 3{,}000{,}000 \times 4 = 12{,}000{,}000\ \text{bytes}$$

### Step 7 — Convert to Megabytes (1 MB = 1,048,576 bytes in binary, or 1,000,000 in SI)

$$B_{MB} = \frac{12{,}000{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 11.44\ \text{MB (binary)}$$

or

$$B_{MB} = \frac{12{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 12.00\ \text{MB (SI)}$$

> **Final Answer:** $N = 3 \times 10^6$ cells, $A_{cell} = 900$ m² = 0.09 ha, storage $\approx 11.44$ MB.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Moran's I for a Field with Three Sample Points

> **Problem:** Three rainfall stations measure the annual rainfall (mm) at coordinates and values given below. Using a **binary contiguity** weight (1 if $d_{ij} \le 30$ km, else 0), compute Moran's I.

| Station | $x$ (km) | $y$ (km) | Rainfall (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 0 | 800 |
| B | 20 | 0 | 820 |
| C | 40 | 0 | 850 |

### Step 1 — Compute pairwise distances
- $d_{AB} = \sqrt{(20-0)^2 + 0^2} = 20$ km → weight $w_{AB} = 1$ (since $20 \le 30$)
- $d_{AC} = \sqrt{(40-0)^2 + 0^2} = 40$ km → weight $w_{AC} = 0$ (since $40 > 30$)
- $d_{BC} = \sqrt{(40-20)^2 + 0^2} = 20$ km → weight $w_{BC} = 1$

So the weight matrix is:

$$W = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Sum of weights: $W = \sum w_{ij} = 4$ (counting both off-diagonal directions).

### Step 2 — Compute the mean rainfall

$$\bar{x} = \frac{800 + 820 + 850}{3} = \frac{2470}{3} \approx 823.33\ \text{mm}$$

### Step 3 — Compute deviations from the mean
- $x_A - \bar{x} = 800 - 823.33 = -23.33$
- $x_B - \bar{x} = 820 - 823.33 = -3.33$
- $x_C - \bar{x} = 850 - 823.33 = 26.67$

### Step 4 — Compute the cross-product term $\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}(x_i - \bar{x})(x_j - \bar{x})$
Only the pairs with $w_{ij} = 1$ contribute. Expanding and using symmetry $w_{ij} = w_{ji}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}(x_i - \bar{x})(x_j - \bar{x}) &= 2\big[w_{AB}(x_A - \bar{x})(x_B - \bar{x}) + w_{BC}(x_B - \bar{x})(x_C - \bar{x})\big] \\
&= 2\big[1 \cdot (-23.33)(-3.33) + 1 \cdot (-3.33)(26.67)\big] \\
&= 2\big[77.78 - 88.89\big] \\
&= 2 \cdot (-11.11) \\
&= -22.22
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the denominator $\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2 &= (-23.33)^2 + (-3.33)^2 + (26.67)^2 \\
&= 544.29 + 11.09 + 711.29 \\
&= 1266.67
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Moran's I formula

$$I = \frac{n}{W} \cdot \frac{\sum_i\sum_j w_{ij}(x_i - \bar{x})(x_j - \bar{x})}{\sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

Substituting $n = 3$, $W = 4$, numerator = $-22.22$, denominator = $1266.67$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{-22.22}{1266.67} \\
&= 0.75 \cdot (-0.01754) \\
&\approx -0.0132
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 — Interpret the result
- $I \approx -0.013$ is **very close to zero** and slightly negative.
- Interpretation: **weak negative spatial autocorrelation** — neighboring stations do *not* have similar rainfall in this small sample. This makes sense because A and B (which are neighbors) have lower rainfall while C (isolated) has higher rainfall, breaking any smooth gradient.

> **Final Answer:** $I \approx -0.013$ (weak negative autocorrelation).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Scale-Dependent Classification of an Object

> **Problem:** A real-world reservoir is 800 m long and 300 m wide. Determine whether it should be mapped as a point, line, or polygon on maps of scale (a) 1:250,000, (b) 1:50,000, (c) 1:10,000. (Use the KTU heuristic: a feature becomes a point if its map size $<$ 0.5 mm; it is shown as a line if it is elongated and $< 1$ mm wide on the map.)

### Step 1 — Compute map dimensions on each scale
Map length on the ground of 800 m, on a map of scale $1:S$:

$$L_{map} = \frac{L_{ground}}{S}$$

- **Scale 1:250,000:** $L = 800/250{,}000 = 0.0032$ m $= 3.2$ mm; $W = 300/250{,}000 = 1.2$ mm.
- **Scale 1:50,000:** $L = 800/50{,}000 = 0.016$ m $= 16$ mm; $W = 300/50{,}000 = 6$ mm.
- **Scale 1:10,000:** $L = 800/10{,}000 = 0.08$ m $= 80$ mm; $W = 300/10{,}000 = 30$ mm.

### Step 2 — Apply KTU heuristic

| Scale | Length on map | Width on map | Heuristic Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1:250,000 | 3.2 mm | 1.2 mm | Both < 5 mm → could be treated as **point** (map symbol), since length and width are both below 5 mm threshold typical for points |
| 1:50,000 | 16 mm | 6 mm | Length ≫ width, width $\ge$ 1 mm → represent as **line** (elongated lake symbol) |
| 1:10,000 | 80 mm | 30 mm | Both dimensions clearly > 5 mm → represent as **polygon** |

> **Final Answer:** Point at 1:250,000; line at 1:50,000; polygon at 1:10,000.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Field vs. Object Decision Tree

| Question | If YES → Field | If NO → Object |
|---|---|---|
| Is the variable defined everywhere in the study area? | ✓ | — |
| Can the value change continuously across space? | ✓ | — |
| Can you count the occurrences (e.g., number of hospitals)? | — | ✓ |
| Does the feature have a clearly identifiable edge? | — | ✓ |
| Is the variable measured at points and inferred between them? | ✓ | — |
| Is the entity named (e.g., "Vembanad Lake")? | — | ✓ |
| Is the underlying process gradient-based (e.g., temperature)? | ✓ | — |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map: The Two World Views of GIS

The following block-level diagram maps the conceptual relationship between geographic phenomena and their data-model representations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Geographic Phenomena] --> B{How is it perceived?}
    B -- Continuous and everywhere-defined --> C[Field View]
    B -- Discrete and bounded --> D[Object View]
    C --> C1[Scalar Field]
    C --> C2[Vector Field]
    C1 --> C1a[Elevation]
    C1 --> C1b[Temperature]
    C1 --> C1c[Soil Moisture]
    C2 --> C2a[Wind Velocity]
    C2 --> C2b[Ocean Currents]
    D --> D1[Point]
    D --> D2[Line]
    D --> D3[Polygon]
    D1 --> D1a[Well]
    D1 --> D1b[Bus Stop]
    D2 --> D2a[Road]
    D2 --> D2b[River]
    D3 --> D3a[Lake]
    D3 --> D3b[District]
    C --> E[Boundary]
    D --> E
    E --> E1[Sharp Boundary]
    E --> E2[Fuzzy Boundary]
    E1 --> E1a[Cadastral Line]
    E1 --> E1b[Wall]
    E2 --> E2a[Forest Edge]
    E2 --> E2b[Coastline]
    C --> F[Data Model]
    D --> F
    F --> F1[Raster Grid]
    F --> F2[Vector TIN]
    F --> F3[Vector Polygon]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A geographic phenomenon is *conceptualized* as either a field (continuous) or an object (discrete). Boundaries are a *cross-cutting* concept that arises at the edges of fields and objects. The conceptualization then dictates the data model: fields → raster or TIN; objects → vector point/line/polygon.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: From Real World to Database

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Real-World Phenomenon] --> B[Conceptualization]
    B -- Field --> C1[Field Model]
    B -- Object --> C2[Object Model]
    C1 --> D1[Logical Model]
    C2 --> D2[Logical Model]
    D1 -- Raster --> E1[Raster Table]
    D1 -- TIN --> E2[Node and Edge List]
    D2 --> E3[Vector Feature Class]
    D1 --> F[Storage in GIS DB]
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Analysis and Cartographic Output]
    G --> G1[Map]
    G --> G2[Statistics]
    G --> G3[Decision Support]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The pipeline moves from real-world observation → conceptualization (what *is* it?) → logical model (how do we organize it?) → physical storage → analysis. Errors at the conceptualization step (e.g., treating a field as an object) cascade through the entire pipeline.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Field, Object, or Boundary?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify a geographic feature] --> Q1{Is the feature everywhere, with no edges?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the value a single number per location?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> F1[Scalar Field]
    Q2 -- No --> F2[Vector Field]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Can the feature be counted and named?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> O1[Object]
    O1 --> Q4{Does it have area at the chosen scale?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> O2[Polygon Object]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Does it have length but no area?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> O3[Line Object]
    Q5 -- No --> O4[Point Object]
    Q3 -- No --> B1[Boundary]
    B1 --> Q6{Is the edge sharp and defined?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> B2[Sharp Boundary]
    Q6 -- No --> B3[Fuzzy Boundary]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Use this decision tree whenever a KTU question says *"How would you represent X in a GIS?"* — first decide field vs. object, then pick the geometric primitive, then decide on boundary type if applicable.

---

## 4.4 Spatial Autocorrelation Workflow (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sample Points with Field Values] --> B[Construct Spatial Weight Matrix W]
    B --> C[Compute Mean of Field]
    C --> D[Compute Cross-Product Sum]
    D --> E[Compute Moran's I]
    E --> F{Interpret I}
    F -- I > 0 --> G1[Positive Autocorrelation: Clustered]
    F -- I approx 0 --> G2[Random Pattern]
    F -- I less 0 --> G3[Negative Autocorrelation: Dispersed]
```

---
