<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Tessellations and Cell Geometry

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**

> Define **tessellation** in the context of GIS data representation. Compare **square grid** and **hexagonal grid** tessellations with respect to (i) equal-area property, (ii) neighbor connectivity, and (iii) suitability for density analysis.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Tessellation is the partitioning of a 2-D plane into a regular, non-overlapping, gap-free mosaic of polygonal cells, where each cell carries a single attribute value. It is the geometric foundation of the **raster data model**.
>
> **Comparison (2 marks):**
>
> | Property | Square Grid | Hexagonal Grid |
> |----------|-------------|----------------|
> | Equal-area | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
> | Neighbor count | 4 (orthogonal) or 8 (with diagonals) | 6 (uniform) |
> | Suitability for density | Anisotropic — diagonal neighbors are $\sqrt{2}$ farther → biased smoothing | Isotropic — uniform distance to all 6 neighbors → unbiased density estimation |
>
> **Conclusion:** Hexagonal grids are theoretically superior for density analysis; square grids dominate due to computational simplicity and sensor-native output.

---

### Question 2: Scale vs. Resolution

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**

> Distinguish between **map scale** and **spatial resolution**. If a topographic map has a scale of $1:50{,}000$, compute the smallest ground feature (in meters) that can be resolved on the map, assuming a minimum resolvable map distance of $1\,\text{mm}$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Distinction (1 mark):** *Map scale* is the ratio of map distance to ground distance (a property of the map's projection). *Spatial resolution* is the size of a single raster cell on the ground (a property of the data).
>
> **Computation (2 marks):**
>
> Minimum resolvable map distance $= 1\,\text{mm} = 0.001\,\text{m}$.
>
> Ground distance corresponding to this:
>
> $$
> d_g = 0.001 \times 50{,}000 = 50\,\text{m}
> $$
>
> **Answer:** The smallest resolvable ground feature is $\mathbf{50\,\text{m}}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Topology and the 9-Intersection Model

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Define **topology** in GIS. Explain the four fundamental topological invariants — **connectivity, adjacency, containment, and order** — with one real-world example for each.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Describe the **9-Intersection Model (9-IM)** of Egenhofer and Franzosa. Using a $3 \times 3$ matrix, show how the topological relationship "A **touches** B" would be represented. Mention the role of **tolerance** in Clementini's refinement.

**Model Answer:**

#### (a) Topology and the Four Invariants (7 marks)

> **Definition (2 marks):** Topology is the branch of geometry that studies properties preserved under continuous deformation (stretching, bending) but not tearing or gluing. In GIS, it describes the **logical relationships** between spatial features: how they connect, share boundaries, and contain one another. Topological relationships are invariant under scaling, rotation, and translation — they depend only on the *order* of features, not their exact geometry.
>
> **The Four Invariants (4 marks, 1 each):**
>
> 1. **Connectivity** — Defines which arcs meet at which nodes. *Example:* In a road network, two road segments share a node at an intersection, enabling a path-finding algorithm to traverse from one to the other.
>
> 2. **Adjacency (Contiguity)** — Defines which polygons share a common boundary. *Example:* Two administrative districts that share a river boundary are "left-of" and "right-of" that river, an adjacency relationship used in redistricting.
>
> 3. **Containment** — Defines which polygon is enclosed within another. *Example:* An island is contained within a lake, which is contained within a country — a three-level nesting hierarchy.
>
> 4. **Order** — Defines the left and right polygon relative to a *directed* arc. *Example:* A one-way street has a "left" side (the legal driving side) and a "right" side (the sidewalk), critical for navigation.
>
> **Why Topology Matters (1 mark):** Topological data structures eliminate **redundant storage** (a shared boundary is stored once, not twice) and enable **error-free spatial queries** (e.g., "which parcels border this river?").
>
> > **Examiner tip:** Award 1 mark for each invariant explained with example, 0.5 for example alone.

#### (b) 9-Intersection Model (7 marks)

> **The Framework (3 marks):** For two spatial features $A$ and $B$, each is decomposed into three topologically distinct parts:
>
> - $A^\circ$ — the **interior** of $A$ (excluding boundary).
> - $\partial A$ — the **boundary** of $A$.
> - $A^-$ — the **exterior** of $A$ (everything outside $A$).
>
> The 9-Intersection Model evaluates the dimension of all 9 pairwise intersections:
>
> $$
> I(A, B) = \begin{bmatrix} \dim(A^\circ \cap B^\circ) & \dim(A^\circ \cap \partial B) & \dim(A^\circ \cap B^-) \\ \dim(\partial A \cap B^\circ) & \dim(\partial A \cap \partial B) & \dim(\partial A \cap B^-) \\ \dim(A^- \cap B^\circ) & \dim(A^- \cap \partial B) & \dim(A^- \cap B^-) \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> The dimension $\dim(\cdot)$ takes values in $\{-1, 0, 1, 2\}$, representing $\emptyset$, point, line, and area respectively.
>
> **Touches Example (2 marks):** Two polygons $A$ and $B$ "touch" (share a boundary but interiors do not overlap):
>
> - $A^\circ \cap B^\circ = \emptyset$ → $\dim = -1$ (interiors disjoint)
> - $\partial A \cap \partial B \neq \emptyset$ → $\dim = 1$ (a line segment shared)
> - $A^- \cap B^- \neq \emptyset$ → $\dim = 2$ (common exterior exists)
>
> The 9-IM matrix for "Touches" reduces to:
>
> $$
> I_{touch} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1 \text{ or } 1 & -1 \text{ or } 2 \\ -1 \text{ or } 1 & 1 & -1 \text{ or } 2 \\ -1 \text{ or } 2 & -1 \text{ or } 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> **Clementini's Tolerance (2 marks):** Real-world data has floating-point and digitization errors. Clementini introduced a **small buffer of tolerance $\varepsilon$** around each feature. If two points are within $\varepsilon$, they are considered the same. This refines the 9-IM to the **DE-9IM** (Dimensionally Extended), which can distinguish:
>
> - $T$ (true — intersection is non-empty),
> - $F$ (false — intersection is empty),
> - $0, 1, 2$ (specific dimensions),
> - $-1$ (empty).
>
> This makes topological predicates **robust against measurement noise**, which is critical for GPS and survey-grade data.

---

### Question B: Vector vs. Raster — Comprehensive Comparison

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Compare the **vector** and **raster (tessellation)** data models across the following dimensions: (i) data structure, (ii) coordinate system, (iii) storage efficiency, (iv) topological capability, (v) analytical strengths, (vi) typical use cases, and (vii) visualization behavior on zoom.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A GIS database has two layers: (i) a **road network** as vector polylines with topology, and (ii) a **digital elevation model (DEM)** as a raster grid with $30\,\text{m}$ resolution. For each layer, recommend the **most suitable representation** and justify it. Then, describe how the temporal dimension can be added to track **road-construction events** and **monthly elevation changes due to mining**.

**Model Answer:**

#### (a) Comprehensive Vector vs. Raster Comparison (7 marks)

> Award **1 mark per dimension** with clear, specific examples.
>
> | Dimension | Vector Model | Raster (Tessellation) Model |
> |-----------|---------------|------------------------------|
> | (i) Data structure | Coordinate lists $(x_i, y_i)$; primitives (point, line, polygon) | 2-D matrix of cell values |
> | (ii) Coordinate system | Can be geographic or projected; precise per-vertex | Grid coordinates (row, col) tied to a CRS via origin and cell size |
> | (iii) Storage efficiency | Compact for sparse features; grows with vertex count | Fixed $m \times n$ matrix; can be large even for sparse data |
> | (iv) Topological capability | ✅ Native topology (nodes, arcs, polygons) | ❌ No native topology; must be derived |
> | (v) Analytical strengths | Network analysis, buffer, overlay, proximity | Surface analysis, interpolation, spatial statistics |
> | (vi) Typical use cases | Cadastral parcels, utilities, road networks | Satellite imagery, DEMs, land-use maps, weather |
> | (vii) Zoom behavior | Infinite detail (no pixelation) | Pixelation on zoom (limited by cell size) |

#### (b) Representation Choice + Temporal Extension (7 marks)

> **Road Network — Vector with Topology (2 marks):** The road network demands **connectivity, directionality (one-way streets), and turn restrictions**. A vector polyline with topology stores these explicitly. A raster representation would lose the line-network structure and require a graph-extraction step. *Justification:* Topology is the **defining** requirement for routing algorithms (Dijkstra, A\*).
>
> **DEM — Raster (Tessellation) (2 marks):** Elevation is a **continuous field** varying smoothly in space. A raster grid with $30\,\text{m}$ resolution is sensor-native (e.g., SRTM, ASTER GDEM) and supports efficient slope, aspect, hillshade, and viewshed computations. A vector TIN would also work but is harder to update and less efficient for large areas at uniform resolution. *Justification:* Field-nature + sensor-native + surface-analysis-friendly.
>
> **Temporal Extension (3 marks):**
>
> - **Road-construction events:** Use an **event-based temporal model**. Each new road is stored as a new feature with a `valid_from` timestamp. The database records only the **change events** (e.g., "Road X added on 2020-03-15") rather than full snapshots. This is efficient because most of the network is static.
>
> - **Monthly elevation changes due to mining:** Use a **raster stack** (also called a **raster time series** or **data cube**). Each month, a new raster band is appended. Analytical queries (e.g., "find areas where elevation dropped by $>5\,\text{m}$ between January and December") become simple band-arithmetic operations:
>
> $$
> \Delta H(i,j,t) = H(i,j,t_{Dec}) - H(i,j,t_{Jan})
> $$
>
> The **temporal resolution** is 1 month. The **change detection** is a spatio-temporal query: "where is $\vert \Delta H \vert > 5\,\text{m}$?"
>
> **Final Recommendation:** Vector-event model for the network; raster-stack model for the DEM. Both models coexist in modern spatio-temporal GIS (e.g., PostGIS/PostGIS Raster, ArcGIS Pro time-enabled layers).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Confusing Scale with Resolution:** Many students write "scale is the resolution". They are related but **not identical**. Scale is a *map* property (ratio); resolution is a *data* property (cell size or smallest distinguishable feature). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting to State Units:** "The answer is 50" loses 0.5 marks. Always write "$50\,\text{m}$" or "$50\,\text{km}$". The KTU valuation key is strict on units.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Mixing Field and Object Vocabulary:** Writing "the temperature polygon" or "the river field" is a conceptual error. *Temperature is a field; a river is an object*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for such mix-ups.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Omitting the Tolerance in Topology:** When explaining the 9-IM, students often forget Clementini's tolerance refinement. The full 14-mark answer in Module 3 typically expects **mention of tolerance (DE-9IM)** for the last 2 marks.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Hand-waving Temporal Models:** Saying "we add a time column" is insufficient. Examiners expect a **named model** (Snapshot / Event-based / Moving Object) with **justification**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Tick each box before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Tessellation** = partitioning a plane into regular, gap-free cells (raster foundation).
- [ ] **Square grid** = 4 (or 8) neighbors, anisotropic; **Hexagonal** = 6 neighbors, isotropic, best for density.
- [ ] **TIN** = variable triangles for terrain; not equal-area.
- [ ] **Vector primitives** = Point (1 coord), Line ($n$ coords), Polygon (closed loop).
- [ ] **Spaghetti model** has no topology; **topological model** has nodes, arcs, polygons with rules.
- [ ] **Topology invariants** = Connectivity, Adjacency, Containment, Order.
- [ ] **9-IM** = $3 \times 3$ matrix of interior/boundary/exterior intersections; dimensions $\in \{-1, 0, 1, 2\}$.
- [ ] **Clementini's tolerance** refines 9-IM to DE-9IM (T/F/0/1/2/-1 symbols).
- [ ] **Map scale** = $MD / GD$ (RF); $1\,\text{mm} = 0.001 \times S$ ground meters.
- [ ] **Spatial resolution** = ground size of one cell; **temporal resolution** = time interval between observations.
- [ ] **Field view** = continuous surface (temperature, elevation); **Object view** = discrete entities (buildings, roads).
- [ ] **Temporal models:** Snapshot (full state), Event-based (changes only), Moving Object (trajectories).
- [ ] **Valid Time** = real-world occurrence; **Transaction Time** = DB recording; **World Time** = absolute or relative.
- [ ] **Hexagon area** = $\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} s^2$.
- [ ] **RLE compression** stores runs of identical values; **Quad-tree** recursively subdivides into 4.
- [ ] **Quad-tree depth $k$** → $4^k$ cells; **Euler's formula** for TIN: $V - E + F = 1$.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Data Representation in GIS.**

*Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme guidelines. Aligned with Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate). For PECET414 — Cartography and GIS.*
