# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams conform to the strict alpha-node and label-formatting rules of the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `n1`, `flowA`). All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text.

## 4.1 Architecture of a Spatial Database System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n1["USER LAYER - GIS CLIENT OR QGIS"]
    n2["APPLICATION LAYER - SPATIAL QUERIES SQL WITH OGC OPERATORS"]
    n3["SDBMS LAYER - POSTGIS OR ORACLE SPATIAL"]
    n4["SPATIAL INDEX LAYER - R TREE OR QUAD TREE"]
    n5["STORAGE LAYER - DISK WITH SHAPEFILE GEOTIFF RASTER TABLES"]
    n1 -->|SPATIAL REQUEST| n2
    n2 -->|OGC SQL ST INTERSECTS ST BUFFER| n3
    n3 -->|INDEX LOOKUP| n4
    n4 -->|PHYSICAL READ| n5
    n5 -->|RAW DATA| n4
    n4 -->|INDEXED RESULT| n3
    n3 -->|GEOMETRY ATTRIBUTES| n2
    n2 -->|MAP OR REPORT| n1
```

```mermaid
    flowA --> flowB
    flowB --> flowC
    flowC --> flowD
    flowD --> flowE
    flowE --> flowF
    flowF --> flowG
    flowG --> flowH
```

```mermaid
    rtR --> rtL2
```

```mermaid
    dsA --> dsQ2
    dsQ1 --> dsV["VECTOR STORAGE - POSTGIS GEOMETRY GEOGRAPHY"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a spatial database. List any four spatial data types supported by it.**

**Model Answer (KTU-Standard 3-Mark Format):**

A **spatial database** is a database optimized to store, retrieve, and query data representing objects defined in a geometric space, by extending the relational model with spatial data types, indexes, and operators.

Four spatial data types supported:

1. **Point** — 0-dimensional, single coordinate pair.
2. **Line / Polyline** — 1-dimensional, sequence of connected points.
3. **Polygon** — 2-dimensional, closed sequence of points enclosing an area.
4. **Geometry Collection** — heterogeneous set of points, lines, and polygons.

> [!NOTE]
> 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the four types (0.5 each). KTU expects at least *three* types for full credit.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between R-Tree and Quad-Tree spatial indexes (any three points).**

**Model Answer (KTU-Standard 3-Mark Format):**

| # | R-Tree | Quad-Tree |
|---|--------|-----------|
| 1 | Uses variable-size **Minimum Bounding Rectangles (MBRs)** to group nearby features. | Divides space into **four equal, fixed quadrants** recursively. |
| 2 | Supports points, lines, and polygons efficiently. | Best suited for **point data**; less efficient for line/polygon. |
| 3 | MBRs of sibling nodes **may overlap**, requiring multiple tree paths during search. | Quadrants are **disjoint**; a search follows a single path. |
| 4 | Used in **PostGIS, Oracle Spatial, MySQL**. | Used in older GIS and in-memory engines. |

> [!NOTE]
> 3 marks = 3 × 1-point contrasts. KTU valuation expects a **comparison table** or **bulleted pairs**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, Module 4 carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice**. Both options A and B are provided below — students answer **one** in the exam hall.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **components of a spatial database** in detail. Discuss the role of **OGC Simple Features for SQL** in standardizing spatial data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **R-Tree spatial index**. Construct a **level-1 R-Tree** for the following 6 point features with **M = 3**: A(2,3), B(5,4), C(9,6), D(4,7), E(8,1), F(7,2). Show all MBRs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Components of a Spatial Database (7 marks):**

A spatial database has **three core components**:

1. **Spatial Data Model** — Defines the abstraction of geographic reality. The two principal models are:
   - **Vector model** — features as points, lines, polygons.
   - **Raster model** — space as a grid of cells with attribute values.

2. **Spatial Data Structure** — The encoding used to store the model in files or tables. Examples:
   - **WKT (Well-Known Text)** — human-readable, e.g., `POLYGON((0 0, 10 0, 10 10, 0 10, 0 0))`.
   - **WKB (Well-Known Binary)** — compact binary form.
   - **Shapefile (.shp)** — ESRI's industry-standard vector format.
   - **GeoJSON** — web-friendly JSON-based format.

3. **Spatial DBMS (SDBMS)** — Manages storage, indexing, and querying. Examples: **PostgreSQL/PostGIS, Oracle Spatial, SQL Server, MySQL, SpatiaLite**. It must support:
   - Spatial data types and functions.
   - Spatial indexing (R-tree, GiST).
   - CRS transformations (ST_Transform).
   - ACID transactions.

**Role of OGC Simple Features for SQL (3 marks within part a):**

The **Open Geospatial Consortium (OGC)** developed the **Simple Features for SQL (SFS)** specification to provide a standard SQL interface for spatial data. Its roles include:

- **Standardization of geometry types** — point, line, polygon, multi-point, multi-line, multi-polygon, geometry collection.
- **Standard SQL functions** — ST_Intersects, ST_Contains, ST_Buffer, ST_Distance, ST_Area, ST_Length.
- **Metadata tables** — `GEOMETRY_COLUMNS` and `SPATIAL_REF_SYS` define geometry columns and CRS identifiers (EPSG codes).
- **Portability** — spatial queries written for one SFS-compliant DBMS can be migrated to another with minimal changes.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU valuation scheme for part (a): 4 marks for components, 3 marks for OGC role. Use **bold subheadings** for clarity.

**(b) R-Tree Spatial Index and Construction (7 marks):**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

An **R-Tree** is a hierarchical spatial index proposed by Guttman (1984) that organizes spatial objects using **Minimum Bounding Rectangles (MBRs)**. Properties:
- Each **leaf node** stores actual feature MBRs (and optionally, the geometry).
- Each **internal node** stores an MBR that encloses all of its children's MBRs.
- The **root MBR** encloses the entire indexed dataset.
- Search follows tree branches whose MBRs intersect the query region.

**Construction (4 marks):**

Group the 6 points into 2 leaves of size ≤ 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L1 &= \{A(2,3),\, B(5,4),\, D(4,7)\} \\
L2 &= \{C(9,6),\, E(8,1),\, F(7,2)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute MBRs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MBR}(L1) &= [2,\, 5,\, 3,\, 7] \\
\text{MBR}(L2) &= [7,\, 9,\, 1,\, 6] \\
\text{MBR}(R) &= [2,\, 9,\, 1,\, 7]
\end{aligned}
$$

Draw the tree (refer to SECTION 4.3 for the Mermaid diagram):

```
        Root  [x:2–9, y:1–7]
       /                       \
   L1 [x:2–5, y:3–7]       L2 [x:7–9, y:1–6]
   /    |    \              /    |    \
 A(2,3) B(5,4) D(4,7)    C(9,6) E(8,1) F(7,2)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to draw **overlapping** MBRs in the wrong leaves (e.g., grouping all x < 5 into L1 and x ≥ 5 into L2, ignoring y). KTU expects the MBRs to be **tight (minimum area)**. Always re-check the min/max — partial marks are awarded for correct min/max even if the tree structure is slightly off.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is **DE-9IM**? Explain the **8 OGC topological predicates** with one example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two cities are at latitudes φ₁ = 9.9312° N, λ₁ = 76.2673° E (Kochi) and φ₂ = 11.0168° N, λ₂ = 76.9558° E (Coimbatore). Compute the great-circle (Haversine) distance between them. Take **R = 6,371,000 m**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) DE-9IM and 8 OGC Topological Predicates (7 marks):**

The **Dimensionally Extended 9-Intersection Model (DE-9IM)** is a mathematical framework that describes the topological relationship between two spatial features A and B. It examines the **dimension** (0 = point, 1 = line, 2 = area, F = empty) of the intersection of the **interior (I)**, **boundary (∂)**, and **exterior (E)** of A and B, forming a 3×3 matrix. The **OGC** derives **8 named predicates** from specific patterns of this matrix:

1. **Equals** — A and B are topologically and geometrically identical. *Example: Two cadastral records of the same plot.*
2. **Disjoint** — A and B share no point. *Example: A school building and an unrelated lake.*
3. **Intersects** — A and B share at least one point. *Example: A road crossing a district polygon.*
4. **Touches** — A and B meet only at their boundaries. *Example: Two adjacent land parcels sharing a fence line.*
5. **Crosses** — A and B cross, with dimension dropping. *Example: A river (line) crossing a state (polygon).*
6. **Within** — A is completely inside B. *Example: A village polygon inside a district polygon.*
7. **Contains** — Reverse of Within. *Example: A district polygon contains village polygons.*
8. **Overlaps** — A and B share interior points but neither contains the other. *Example: Two overlapping land-use zones.*

> [!NOTE]
> KTU awards 0.5 marks per correctly named predicate + 0.5 for the correct example. 3 marks reserved for explaining DE-9IM matrix and dimension codes (0, 1, 2, F).

**(b) Haversine Distance Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert to radians.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_1 &= 9.9312 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17335 \text{ rad} \\
\phi_2 &= 11.0168 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.19229 \text{ rad} \\
\Delta\phi &= 0.01894 \text{ rad} \\
\Delta\lambda &= (76.9558 - 76.2673) \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.6885 \times 0.0174533 = 0.01202 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Haversine intermediate value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos(\phi_1)\cos(\phi_2)\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) \\
a &= \sin^2(0.00947) + \cos(0.17335)\cos(0.19229)\sin^2(0.00601) \\
a &= (0.00947)^2 + (0.98503)(0.98155)(0.00601)^2 \\
a &= 0.0000897 + (0.96692)(0.0000361) \\
a &= 0.0000897 + 0.0000349 \\
a &= 0.0001246
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Central angle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= 2 \arcsin(\sqrt{a}) = 2 \arcsin(0.01116) = 2 \times 0.01116 = 0.02232 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Distance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= R \times c = 6{,}371{,}000 \times 0.02232 = 142{,}200 \text{ m} \approx 142.2 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Boxed Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{d \approx 142.2 \text{ km}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Two common mistakes in the Haversine question:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** — results in a numerically absurd answer (distance in the millions of km). KTU deducts **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Swapping Δφ and Δλ** — wrong latitude/longitude difference. Always subtract the *smaller* from the *larger* and label correctly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Box — Read Twice Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- **Spatial Database** = RDBMS + spatial types + spatial indexes + OGC functions.
- **Three pillars** of a spatial DB: data model, data structure, SDBMS.
- **OGC Simple Features for SQL** is the standard; supports point, line, polygon, multi-versions, geometry collection.
- **Two main spatial data models** = Vector (discrete) and Raster (continuous).
- **Common spatial query types** = Range, Nearest-Neighbor, Spatial Join, Buffer, Overlay.
- **Spatial Indexes**:
  - **R-Tree** — variable MBRs, supports all geometry types, used in PostGIS/Oracle.
  - **Quad-Tree** — four equal quadrants, best for points, simpler but less efficient for lines/polygons.
- **DE-9IM** = 3×3 matrix of dimension codes (0, 1, 2, F) of Interior, Boundary, Exterior intersections.
- **8 OGC Topological Predicates** = Equals, Disjoint, Intersects, Touches, Crosses, Within, Contains, Overlaps. *Memorize all 8 — appears almost every semester.*
- **Euclidean distance formula** (2D) = $\sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$.
- **Haversine distance** = $2R \arcsin(\sqrt{a})$, where $R = 6{,}371{,}000$ m. *Always convert to radians first.*
- **Shoelace area formula** = $\frac{1}{2}\vert\sum(x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i)\vert$ — write every cross-product row.
- **R-Tree** properties: hierarchical, MBR-based, allows overlap, O(log N) search.
- **Quad-Tree** properties: recursive 4-way split, disjoint regions, ideal for point queries.
- **Popular Spatial DBMS software**: PostgreSQL/PostGIS, Oracle Spatial, SQL Server Spatial, MySQL Spatial, SpatiaLite, MongoDB (with GeoJSON).
- **Buffer area** of a circle = $\pi r^2$.
- **CRS / EPSG codes** are mandatory for any spatial database column.
- **WKT and WKB** are OGC-standard encodings for geometry interchange.
- **Spatial metadata tables** in OGC SFS: `GEOMETRY_COLUMNS` and `SPATIAL_REF_SYS`.
- **Spatial join** = join two tables based on a spatial predicate, not on a foreign key.
- **R-tree fan-out rule**: each node contains between $m$ and $M$ entries; $m \le M/2$ is a common choice.
- **Quad-tree depth for N points** ≈ $\lceil \log_4 N \rceil$.
- **DE-9IM dimension codes**: 0 (point), 1 (curve/line), 2 (surface/area), F (empty).
- **Always show step-by-step substitutions** — KTU awards **1 mark per logical step**, not just the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET414 - CARTOGRAPHY AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Input: Spatial Data Input

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌍 SPATIAL DATA INPUT — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Spatial Data Input** is the systematic process of capturing, encoding, transferring, and converting real-world geographical phenomena into a digital, computer-readable format that can be stored, analyzed, manipulated, and displayed within a Geographic Information System (GIS) environment. It encompasses all procedures required to transform analog maps, field measurements, remotely sensed imagery, survey records, and existing digital datasets into the **vector** (point, line, polygon) and **raster** (grid cell/pixel) data models that form the foundation of geospatial analysis.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme framework, spatial data input includes:
- **Primary Data Acquisition** (direct capture from the field)
- **Secondary Data Acquisition** (digitization and conversion of existing materials)
- **Data Pre-processing** (format conversion, projection assignment, topology building)
- **Attribute Data Entry** (linking descriptive information to spatial features)
- **Quality Assurance and Validation** (error detection and rectification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Spatial data input is the **first and most critical step** of the GIS data pipeline. It is the bridge between the *physical world* and the *digital analytical world*. Errors introduced at this stage propagate through all subsequent analyses — the famous GIS principle: **"Garbage In = Garbage Out (GIGO)."**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making Sense of It

Imagine you are an architect tasked with designing a new city park. Before you can draw blueprints, you must first **gather information**: measure the land (surveying), photograph the existing trees (remote sensing), interview local residents (attribute data), and record soil conditions (field sampling). You then **translate** all this raw information into standardized construction drawings (digitization). The drawings are useless unless they faithfully represent the real world — a measurement error at this stage will haunt the entire project.

**Spatial Data Input is exactly this translation process for GIS:**
- The **field/real world** = the *actual geography* (rivers, roads, buildings)
- The **measurement/capture devices** = GPS, total stations, satellites, scanners
- The **blueprint** = the digital vector/raster dataset inside the GIS

### 1.3 The Two Fundamental Spatial Data Models Recap

| Data Model | Structure | Best Represents | Example |
|------------|-----------|-----------------|---------|
| **Vector** | Points, Lines, Polygons (with X, Y, Z coordinates) | Discrete features with sharp boundaries | Roads, buildings, parcels |
| **Raster** | Grid of cells (pixels) with a value per cell | Continuous phenomena | Elevation, temperature, satellite imagery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Coordinate:** A coordinate is a set of numerical values (e.g., latitude, longitude) that uniquely define a position on the Earth's surface relative to a known reference system.

## 1.4 Why Is Spatial Data Input So Important?

- It determines the **accuracy** and **precision** of the entire GIS project.
- It dictates the **cost** — often 60–80% of total project budget is spent on data acquisition and input.
- It defines the **scale**, **resolution**, and **temporal currency** of all downstream products.
- It establishes the **metadata foundation** required for legal, scientific, and administrative use.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Board Pitfall: Many students confuse **data input** with **data editing**. Data input is the **initial creation/capture** of features; data editing is the **subsequent correction and refinement** of already-existing features. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Spatial Data Input Methods

Spatial data input methods can be broadly classified into **two master categories** based on the **origin** of the data:

### A) Primary Data Input Methods (Direct Capture from Reality)

These methods involve collecting data **directly** from the real world through field instruments or sensors.

#### 1. **Keyboard Entry / Coordinate Entry**
- The simplest, most primitive method.
- User manually types X, Y (and optionally Z) coordinates into attribute tables.
- Used only for very small datasets (a few hundred points).
- Highly error-prone; reserved for low-accuracy applications.

#### 2. **GPS (Global Positioning System) Surveying**
- Satellite-based positioning using a constellation of **24+ satellites** (NAVSTAR).
- Accuracy ranges from **±5–10 m** (handheld GPS) to **±mm-level** (RTK / DGPS).
- Outputs coordinates in WGS-84 datum, which must be transformed to local datum (e.g., Everest 1956 for India, WGS-84 / UTM for global projects).
- Captures **point features** (e.g., benchmarks, wells) primarily; can capture lines/polygons via kinematic (walking/driving) mode.

#### 3. **Total Station / Electronic Distance Measurement (EDM) Surveying**
- Ground-based, line-of-sight instrument measuring angles and distances.
- Sub-centimeter accuracy.
- Used for cadastral mapping, engineering surveys, and high-precision cadastral boundary delineation.

#### 4. **Photogrammetry**
- Extraction of geometric information from **stereoscopic aerial photographs** or satellite stereo pairs.
- Uses **parallax differences** between overlapping images to compute elevation.
- Produces both **vector** (planimetric features) and **raster** (DEMs/orthophotos) data.
- Highly efficient for large-area topographic mapping.

#### 5. **Remote Sensing**
- Acquisition of data from **airborne or spaceborne sensors** (Landsat, Sentinel, MODIS, LISS-III).
- Produces primarily **raster** data (multispectral / hyperspectral imagery).
- Spatial resolution ranges from **0.3 m** (WorldView) to **>1 km** (MODIS).

#### 6. **LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)**
- Active sensor emitting laser pulses; measures return time to compute distance.
- Produces dense **point clouds** (millions of points/km²) for high-resolution DEM generation.
- Can penetrate vegetation canopy to produce **bare-earth DEMs**.

### B) Secondary Data Input Methods (Conversion of Existing Materials)

These methods transform **existing analog or digital materials** into GIS-ready formats.

#### 1. **Manual Digitization (using Digitizing Table)**
- A **digitizing table** is an electronic board with an embedded wire mesh grid.
- The operator traces features on a **paper map fixed to the table** using a **cursor (puck)** with crosshairs and function keys.
- Resolution: typically **0.025 mm** (≈ 1000 lines/inch).
- **Modes:**
  - *Point Mode* — operator clicks each vertex.
  - *Stream Mode* — points are recorded automatically at fixed time/distance intervals.

#### 2. **Heads-up (On-screen) Digitization**
- A scanned raster image (TIFF/GeoTIFF) is displayed on the computer monitor.
- Operator traces features using a **mouse** over the displayed raster backdrop.
- **Much faster and more accurate** than table digitization; the modern industry standard.

#### 3. **Scanning (Raster Scanning)**
- A **scanner** converts paper maps into raster image files.
- **Two main types:**
  - *Drum Scanner* — paper mounted on rotating drum; very high geometric fidelity.
  - *Flatbed Scanner* — stationary paper, moving sensor head; convenient but prone to distortion.
- The output requires **geometric rectification** (rubber-sheeting) to remove scanner-induced distortions.

#### 4. **Vector-to-Raster / Raster-to-Vector Conversion**
- **Rasterization:** Converting vector polygons/lines into grid cells (e.g., for overlay analysis).
- **Vectorization (Automatic):** Software detects line intersections and traces them from raster (e.g., ESRI ArcScan, QGIS Trace).
- Often requires manual **cleaning** because automatic vectorization produces spaghetti, overshoots, and dangling arcs.

#### 5. **Importing Existing Digital Data**
- Acquiring data from government agencies (e.g., Survey of India, ISRO Bhuvan, OpenStreetMap).
- Requires format conversion (e.g., DWG → Shapefile, KML → GeoJSON, GeoTIFF → IMG) and **datum/projection transformation**.

## 2.2 The Seven Core Stages of the Spatial Data Input Pipeline

1. **Source Identification** — locating suitable analog/digital materials.
2. **Pre-processing** — cleaning, scanning, georeferencing of source documents.
3. **Coordinate Registration** — assigning a real-world coordinate system to the raw data.
4. **Feature Capture** — manual/automated digitization or sensor data ingestion.
5. **Topology Construction** — building spatial relationships (connectivity, adjacency, containment).
6. **Attribute Linking** — connecting descriptive tables to spatial features via unique IDs.
7. **Validation & Quality Control** — checking for errors, slivers, overshoots, undershoots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Topology Matters:** Topology describes the **logical relationships** between spatial features (e.g., two polygons share a common boundary, lines connect at nodes, polygons are closed). Without topology, a GIS cannot perform network analysis, adjacency queries, or polygon overlay.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Coordinate Transformation (2D Similarity)** | $X' = aX - bY + c$, $Y' = bX + aY + d$ | Where $a = S\cos\theta$, $b = S\sin\theta$; $S$ = scale, $\theta$ = rotation |
| **Scale Factor** | $S = \frac{\text{Paper Distance}}{\text{Ground Distance}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Digitizing Table Resolution** | $R = \frac{1}{\text{Lines per inch}}$ | Typically **0.025 mm** |
| **Ground Sampling Distance (GSD)** | $GSD = H \cdot \tan(\text{IFOV})$ | $H$ = sensor height, IFOV = instantaneous field of view; unit = meters |
| **GPS Dilution of Precision (DOP)** | $DOP^2 = \text{EDOP}^2 + \text{NDOP}^2 + \text{VDOP}^2 + \text{TDOP}^2$ | < 4 = good, > 6 = poor |
| **Sliver Polygon Size Tolerance** | $A_{sliver} < T$ (threshold) | E.g., $T = 1\,\text{m}^2$ for cadastral maps |
| **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** | $RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i,obs} - x_{i,true})^2}$ | Standard accuracy measure in georeferencing |
| **Parallax Equation (Photogrammetry)** | $P = x_1 - x_2 = \frac{B \cdot f}{H}$ | $B$ = base, $f$ = focal length, $H$ = flying height |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks students to write the **equation for digitizing table resolution** or the **similarity transformation** for coordinate registration. Memorize the formula and the meaning of each term.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Urban Planning:** Digitizing cadastral maps for property tax administration.
- **Disaster Management:** Ingesting satellite imagery for flood inundation mapping.
- **Transportation Engineering:** Capturing road centerlines via GPS for pavement management systems.
- **Environmental Engineering:** LiDAR-derived DEMs for watershed delineation and flood modeling.
- **Cadastral & Land Records:** Total station surveys for legally binding boundary demarcation.
- **Forestry:** Drone photogrammetry for canopy height modeling.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Two-Dimensional Similarity (Conformal) Transformation

When a scanned map or digitizing table is registered to real-world coordinates, four parameters must be solved: **two translations (c, d), one rotation (θ), and one scale factor (S)**. The classical similarity transformation is derived from the requirement that **shape must be preserved** (no shearing or non-uniform scaling).

### Step 1 — Begin with the General Rotation Equations

For a point $(X, Y)$ rotated counter-clockwise by an angle $\theta$ about the origin:

$$
X' = X\cos\theta - Y\sin\theta
$$

$$
Y' = X\sin\theta + Y\cos\theta
$$

### Step 2 — Introduce Uniform Scale Factor S

When the rotated point is also uniformly scaled by factor $S$:

$$
X' = S \cdot X\cos\theta - S \cdot Y\sin\theta
$$

$$
Y' = S \cdot X\sin\theta + S \cdot Y\cos\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Define Simplified Coefficients for Convenience

Let us substitute:

$$
a = S\cos\theta
$$

$$
b = S\sin\theta
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back into the Equations

$$
X' = aX - bY
$$

$$
Y' = bX + aY
$$

### Step 5 — Add Translations (Origin Shift)

Real-world maps rarely have their origin at $(0,0)$. We add translation parameters $c$ and $d$:

$$
X' = aX - bY + c
$$

$$
Y' = bX + aY + d
$$

### Step 6 — Determine Parameters from Known Control Points

To solve for the four unknowns $(a, b, c, d)$, we need **at least two control points (GCPs)**. Each control point provides two equations (one for $X'$, one for $Y'$).

For control points $(X_1, Y_1) \to (X'_1, Y'_1)$ and $(X_2, Y_2) \to (X'_2, Y'_2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X'_1 &= aX_1 - bY_1 + c \\
Y'_1 &= bX_1 + aY_1 + d \\
X'_2 &= aX_2 - bY_2 + c \\
Y'_2 &= bX_2 + aY_2 + d
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Solve the Linear System

Subtracting equation 1 from equation 3, and equation 2 from equation 4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X'_2 - X'_1 &= a(X_2 - X_1) - b(Y_2 - Y_1) \\
Y'_2 - Y'_1 &= b(X_2 - X_1) + a(Y_2 - Y_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $\Delta X = X_2 - X_1$, $\Delta Y = Y_2 - Y_1$, $\Delta X' = X'_2 - X'_1$, $\Delta Y' = Y'_2 - Y'_1$. The system becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta X' &= a \cdot \Delta X - b \cdot \Delta Y \\
\Delta Y' &= b \cdot \Delta X + a \cdot \Delta Y
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a $2 \times 2$ linear system in $a$ and $b$. Solving using Cramer's rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= a^2 + b^2 = S^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = S^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{\Delta X' \cdot \Delta X + \Delta Y' \cdot \Delta Y}{(\Delta X)^2 + (\Delta Y)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{\Delta Y' \cdot \Delta X - \Delta X' \cdot \Delta Y}{(\Delta X)^2 + (\Delta Y)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The denominator $(\Delta X)^2 + (\Delta Y)^2$ is the squared distance between the two control points. If the control points are **too close together**, the denominator becomes very small and small measurement errors produce huge errors in $a$ and $b$ — a phenomenon called **numerical instability**. Always choose **well-distributed, well-separated GCPs**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) for Georeferencing

When a map is georeferenced using $n$ ground control points, the **residual error** at each point is the difference between the observed (digitized) coordinate and the true (known) coordinate.

### Step 1 — Define Residual

For the $i$-th control point, the residual vector is:

$$
r_{xi} = X_{i,obs} - X_{i,true}
$$

$$
r_{yi} = Y_{i,obs} - Y_{i,true}
$$

### Step 2 — Sum of Squared Residuals

$$
SS_x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{xi}^2
$$

$$
SS_y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{yi}^2
$$

### Step 3 — RMSE Formulas

$$
RMSE_x = \sqrt{\frac{SS_x}{n}}
$$

$$
RMSE_y = \sqrt{\frac{SS_y}{n}}
$$

### Step 4 — Total (2D) RMSE

$$
RMSE_{xy} = \sqrt{RMSE_x^2 + RMSE_y^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Rule:** A georeferencing result is considered acceptable for most engineering applications when $RMSE_{xy} \leq 0.5 \times \text{pixel size}$ of the source raster image.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Input Methods

| Criterion | Manual Digitization | Heads-up Digitization | GPS Surveying | Photogrammetry | Remote Sensing | LiDAR |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|---------------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| **Accuracy** | High (vector) | High (vector) | Medium–High | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | Low | Medium | High | Medium–High | Very High |
| **Speed** | Slow | Fast | Fast | Fast | Very Fast | Very Fast |
| **Data Type Produced** | Vector | Vector | Vector (points) | Vector + Raster | Raster | Point Cloud → Raster |
| **Skill Required** | Moderate | Low | High | Very High | High | Very High |
| **Best Use Case** | Small-scale cadastre | Updating existing maps | Field inventory | Topographic mapping | Land cover classification | High-resolution DEM |
| **Error Sensitivity** | Operator fatigue | Snapping errors | Signal multipath | Atmospheric distortion | Sensor calibration | Vegetation penetration |
