# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Primary and Secondary methods of spatial data input. Give two examples for each.**

#### Model Answer:

**Primary Data Input Methods:**
These methods involve the **direct capture** of spatial data from the real world using field instruments or sensors. The data is collected first-hand; no pre-existing maps or records are used as the source.

- **Examples:** GPS surveying of ground control points; LiDAR scanning of terrain.
- Total station measurement of property boundaries; aerial photogrammetry.

**Secondary Data Input Methods:**
These methods involve the **conversion or digitization** of pre-existing analog or digital materials into GIS-ready formats. The original data has already been captured; the GIS merely transforms it.

- **Examples:** Manual digitization of a Survey of India topo-sheet using a digitizing table; heads-up on-screen tracing over a scanned raster image.

| Aspect | Primary | Secondary |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| **Source** | Field/sensor | Existing maps/records |
| **Cost** | Generally higher | Generally lower |
| **Currency** | Most current | May be outdated |
| **Skill** | Higher (field work) | Lower (office work) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marker Expectation:** A clean two-column table with at least two examples per side. Marks awarded for crisp definitions, not for lengthy prose.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is a digitizing table? Explain its working principle and typical resolution.**

#### Model Answer:

A **digitizing table** (also called a digitizer) is a flat, board-like electronic device used in GIS for **manual vector data capture** from paper maps. It consists of:

- A **flat surface** containing an embedded fine **wire-mesh grid** that acts as a position-sensing antenna.
- A **cursor (puck)** with crosshairs and 4 to 16 **function keys** (used to enter point/line/polygon codes).
- A **control unit** that translates cursor position into digital (X, Y) coordinates.

**Working Principle:**
The cursor emits a signal (typically electromagnetic or ultrasonic pulses) that is received by the wires beneath the map. The control unit calculates the cursor's position based on the time difference or phase of the received signal at multiple grid wires. The X, Y coordinates are streamed in real-time to the connected computer, where the GIS software records them as vertices of vector features.

**Resolution:**
The resolution of a standard digitizing table is **0.025 mm** (equivalent to 1000 lines per inch), meaning it can theoretically distinguish points as close as 25 micrometers apart on the source map.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marker Tip:** Always mention **0.025 mm** explicitly — it is a "magic number" the examiner expects. Also, note that the digitizer's own coordinates are meaningless until they are **transformed to real-world coordinates** using control points.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU ESE pattern, students answer **one full question** of 14 marks, with internal choice between two questions. Below are two complete alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the various **methods of primary spatial data input** in GIS, with suitable examples. Discuss the relative merits and limitations of GPS surveying and total station surveying. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **coordinate transformation** in spatial data input. Derive the two-dimensional similarity transformation equations and explain the role of ground control points (GCPs). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Methods of Primary Spatial Data Input:**

1. **Keyboard Entry:** Manual entry of X, Y coordinates from field notebooks into a digital file. Limited to very small datasets; high error potential.

2. **GPS Surveying:** Uses satellite signals from the NAVSTAR constellation (24+ MEO satellites) to compute position via trilateration. Handheld units provide 5–10 m accuracy; survey-grade units with differential correction (DGPS/RTK) provide sub-meter to centimeter accuracy. Best suited for asset inventory, location of bore wells, manholes, road centerlines.

3. **Total Station Surveying:** Ground-based instrument combining an electronic theodolite (angle measurement) and an EDM (distance measurement). Operates on line-of-sight principle. Provides sub-centimeter accuracy. Used for high-precision cadastral and engineering surveys.

4. **Photogrammetry:** Derives spatial information from overlapping aerial photographs or satellite stereo pairs. By measuring parallax differences between conjugate images, ground coordinates and elevations are calculated. Produces planimetric maps and DEMs efficiently for large areas.

5. **Remote Sensing:** Captures electromagnetic radiation reflected/emitted from Earth's surface using airborne or spaceborne sensors (Landsat, Sentinel, LISS-III, MODIS). Produces raster imagery in multiple spectral bands. Useful for land cover classification, change detection, and environmental monitoring.

6. **LiDAR:** An active sensor that emits laser pulses (typically 1064 nm near-infrared) and measures the return time to compute distance. Produces dense point clouds (often >10 points/m²) from which high-resolution DEMs and canopy height models are derived. Can penetrate vegetation to produce bare-earth terrain models.

**Comparison — GPS vs Total Station:**

| Criterion | GPS Surveying | Total Station Surveying |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Accuracy** | 5 m (handheld) to mm (RTK) | mm to cm level |
| **Line of sight** | Not required (sky visibility only) | Required between instrument and target |
| **Range** | Global (satellite-based) | Limited (typically up to 3–5 km) |
| **Weather dependency** | Moderate (affected by ionosphere) | Low (visible light) |
| **Productivity in open terrain** | Very high | Moderate |
| **Productivity in dense forest/urban canyon** | Poor (signal obstruction) | Good |
| **Cost of equipment** | Moderate to high | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** GPS excels in open, large-area surveys (e.g., forest inventory, pipeline route); Total Station excels in confined, high-precision work (e.g., building layout, cadastral boundaries). Modern practice often **integrates both** for optimal productivity and accuracy.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Concept of Coordinate Transformation:**
When spatial data is captured (e.g., from a scanned paper map, digitizing table, or GPS), the raw coordinates exist in an **arbitrary local system**. To integrate this data with other geospatial layers, the coordinates must be **mathematically transformed** into a known real-world coordinate system (e.g., UTM, State Plane, or a local grid). This requires the use of **Ground Control Points (GCPs)** — features whose coordinates are known precisely in both the source system and the target system.

**Derivation of 2D Similarity Transformation:**
*(See the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

The final form of the equations is:

$$
X' = aX - bY + c
$$

$$
Y' = bX + aY + d
$$

where $a = S\cos\theta$, $b = S\sin\theta$, $S$ is the scale factor, $\theta$ is the rotation, and $(c, d)$ are the translation components.

**Role of Ground Control Points (GCPs):**
- GCPs are identifiable features (e.g., road intersections, benchmarks) with **known coordinates** in both source and target systems.
- The minimum number of GCPs required to solve a 2D similarity transformation is **2** (each provides two equations for four unknowns: $a, b, c, d$).
- In practice, **more than the minimum** is used (e.g., 4–6 GCPs) and the solution is computed via **least-squares adjustment** to distribute random errors.
- The quality of transformation is reported as the **RMSE** of residuals at the GCPs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Selection Criteria for GCPs:**
> 1. **Well-distributed** across the study area (avoid clustering).
> 2. **Sharp, unambiguous** features easily identifiable on both source and reference.
> 3. **Stable** over time (e.g., permanent structures, not temporary objects).
> 4. **Accurately surveyed** in the target coordinate system.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the process of **raster scanning** for spatial data input. What are the major sources of geometric distortion in scanned maps, and how is **rubber-sheeting** used to correct them? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the importance of **topology** in spatial data input. Explain the concepts of **connectivity, containment, and adjacency** with suitable examples, and list the common topological errors encountered during digitization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Process of Raster Scanning:**
Raster scanning is the process of converting a **paper map** or document into a digital raster image using an optical scanning device. The scanner consists of a light source, a moving sensor (CCD or photomultiplier tube), and an analog-to-digital converter. As the sensor sweeps across the map, it measures the reflected light intensity at each point, which is converted into a pixel value (grayscale or color) and stored as a 2D array.

**Types of Scanners Used in GIS:**
- **Drum Scanner:** The map is mounted on a rotating cylindrical drum; the sensor moves slowly along the drum axis. Provides very high geometric fidelity but slow and expensive.
- **Flatbed Scanner:** The map lies stationary on a glass plate; the sensor moves beneath. Convenient, faster, and cheaper, but prone to internal mechanical distortions.

**Major Sources of Geometric Distortion in Scanned Maps:**
1. **Paper Expansion/Contraction** due to humidity and temperature changes.
2. **Scanner Optical Distortions** (lens aberrations, non-uniform illumination).
3. **Map Projection Distortions** inherent in the original paper map.
4. **Fold and Tear Creases** in aged paper documents.
5. **Non-linear distortion** at the edges of flatbed scanners.

**Rubber-Sheeting for Geometric Correction:**
Rubber-sheeting is a **piecewise linear (or higher-order) transformation** that "stretches" the raster image to align with known real-world coordinates. The procedure is:

1. Identify a set of **GCPs** (e.g., 10–20 well-distributed points) whose real-world coordinates are known.
2. Compute the **residual error** at each GCP — the displacement between its true and current position.
3. Apply a **piecewise polynomial transformation** (first, second, or third order) to warp the raster image so that each GCP moves to its correct position.
4. Re-sample the warped image to a regular grid using **nearest-neighbor, bilinear, or cubic convolution** interpolation.

> [!NOTE]
> The term "rubber-sheeting" derives from the analogy of stretching a rubber sheet pinned at control points — the sheet deforms to fit, with distortion concentrated away from the pins.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Importance of Topology in Spatial Data Input:**
Topology is the set of **logical spatial relationships** between geographic features that remain invariant under continuous deformation (e.g., stretching, rotation). Unlike geometry, topology describes **how features are connected**, not their exact shape. Topology is essential in GIS for:
- **Polygon overlay analysis** (e.g., land-use vs. soil-type intersection).
- **Network analysis** (e.g., shortest path along roads, pipe connectivity).
- **Adjacency queries** (e.g., which parcels border a given property).
- **Data validation** (e.g., detecting gaps and overlaps in cadastral maps).

**Three Core Topological Concepts:**

1. **Connectivity** — Describes how lines (arcs) connect at nodes. Example: In a road network, two road segments meet at an intersection node. Connectivity enables route-finding and flow analysis.
2. **Containment** — Describes whether a point or line lies within a polygon. Example: A school (point) is contained within a particular district (polygon). Enables queries like "list all schools in Ernakulam district."
3. **Adjacency (Contiguity)** — Describes which polygons share a common boundary. Example: A land parcel A is adjacent to parcels B and C if they share a common edge. Adjacency enables queries like "find all neighbors of this plot."

**Common Topological Errors During Digitization:**

| Error | Description | Visual Example |
|-------|-------------|----------------|
| **Overshoot** | Line extends beyond the intended intersection | Arc continues past node |
| **Undershoot** | Line falls short of the intended intersection | Gap between arcs at node |
| **Sliver Polygon** | Thin, spurious polygon formed between two nearly coincident boundaries | Long, thin triangle |
| **Unclosed Polygon** | Polygon's start and end vertices do not coincide | Open chain mistaken for closed ring |
| **Duplicate Arc** | Same boundary digitized twice | Two coincident lines |
| **Dangling Node** | Node connected to only one line (orphan) | Dead-end arc |
| **Missing Label** | Polygon exists but has no attribute record | Blank centroid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Cure:** All these errors can be eliminated during the **cleaning and topology-building phase** of data input using snapping tolerances, intersect dangles, and dissolve operations in GIS software such as ArcGIS, QGIS, or MapInfo.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Spatial Data Input Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "Data Input" with "Data Editing"** — Input is *initial capture*; editing is *subsequent correction*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are mixed up.
> 2. **Forgetting the datum/projection issue** — When writing about GPS or coordinate transformation, students often forget to mention that WGS-84 must be **transformed to the local datum** (e.g., Everest 1956 for India) before integration with legacy maps. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Skipping numerical values** — Questions asking about "resolution of a digitizing table" or "accuracy of GPS" expect exact numbers (0.025 mm, ±5 m handheld, mm-level RTK). Vague adjectives like "high" or "low" fetch zero marks.
> 4. **Confusing raster scanning with digitization** — Scanning produces a *raster image*; digitizing produces *vector features*. These are two completely different output formats.
> 5. **Writing "rubber-sheeting" without explaining the principle** — Examiners expect the *pin-and-stretch* analogy and the use of GCPs. Just naming the term scores 1 of 3 marks.
> 6. **Omitting units in formulas** — Any formula must specify what each variable represents and its unit. Formula-without-units is considered incomplete in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Spatial data input** = conversion of real-world geography into digital GIS format.
- Two broad categories: **Primary** (direct field/sensor capture) and **Secondary** (conversion of existing materials).
- **Primary methods:** Keyboard entry, GPS, total station, photogrammetry, remote sensing, LiDAR.
- **Secondary methods:** Manual table digitization, heads-up on-screen digitization, raster scanning, format conversion, digital data import.
- **Digitizing table resolution = 0.025 mm (≈ 1000 lpi).**
- **2D Similarity Transformation:** Four parameters — $a, b, c, d$ — solved using minimum **2 GCPs** (in practice 4–6 used for least-squares adjustment).
- **GCPs** must be well-distributed, sharp, stable, and accurately surveyed.
- **RMSE** is the standard accuracy measure for georeferencing; acceptable value ≤ 0.5 × pixel size.
- **Rubber-sheeting** corrects non-linear distortions in scanned maps using piecewise polynomial warping driven by GCPs.
- **Topology** is the logical relationship between features; the three pillars are **connectivity, containment, adjacency**.
- **Common topological errors:** overshoot, undershoot, sliver polygon, unclosed polygon, dangling node.
- **Data model decision:** vector for discrete features, raster for continuous phenomena.
- **GPS datum issue:** Always transform from WGS-84 to the local datum (Everest 1956, Kalianpur 1962, etc.) for Indian projects.
- **GIGO Principle:** Errors in data input propagate through the entire GIS workflow — accuracy must be enforced at the input stage itself.
- **Metadata** must accompany all input data: source, scale, accuracy, date, datum, projection, and author.
- **Cost implication:** Data acquisition + input typically consumes **60–80%** of a GIS project's total budget.
- **Quality control tools:** Snapping tolerance, dangle cleanup, intersect arcs, dissolve, attribute domain checks, cross-verification with field data.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET414 - CARTOGRAPHY AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Quality** in a Geographic Information System (GIS) refers to the fitness-for-use of a geospatial dataset, formally expressed through measurable characteristics — **positional accuracy**, **attribute (thematic) accuracy**, **temporal accuracy**, **logical consistency**, and **completeness** — as standardized in **ISO 19157:2013** (Geographic information — Data quality).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *Data Quality* is the discipline of **identifying, quantifying, documenting, and managing errors and uncertainty** that arise during the acquisition, processing, storage, and representation of spatial data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you buy a hand-drawn tourist map of Kerala. The roads are drawn roughly (low **positional accuracy**), the names of some towns are misspelled (low **attribute accuracy**), the map shows 2010 bus routes but it is 2026 (low **temporal accuracy**), and a major district is missing entirely (low **completeness**). A GIS dataset suffers from exactly these same five ailments — *Data Quality* is the medical report card for your spatial data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** A dataset is **never** perfectly accurate. The goal of data quality management is *not* to eliminate error, but to **bound** it, **report** it, and **propagate** it through analyses so the end-user knows how much trust to place in a derived map.

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of GIS Data Quality (ISO 19157)

| # | Quality Element | What It Measures | Standard Metric |
|---|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Completeness** | Presence/absence of features | Omission %, Commission % |
| 2 | **Logical Consistency** | Adherence to topological & format rules | Conflict count |
| 3 | **Positional Accuracy** | Deviation of coordinates from truth | RMSE, CE90, LE90 |
| 4 | **Thematic/Attribute Accuracy** | Correctness of non-spatial descriptors | Kappa coefficient, Confusion matrix |
| 5 | **Temporal Accuracy** | Currency of the dataset | Time-stamp validity |

## 1.4 Accuracy vs Precision — The Foundational Distinction

- **Accuracy**: How close a measurement is to the **true value** (systematic error).
- **Precision**: How closely repeated measurements agree with **each other** (random error).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A GPS reading that always reports `10.0000° N, 76.0000° E` regardless of your real position is **highly precise** but **highly inaccurate**.

## 1.5 The "Error" Hierarchy in GIS

Spatial error propagates from the smallest measurement unit to the final map. The chain is:

$$\text{Source Data} \xrightarrow{\text{ingest}} \text{Database} \xrightarrow{\text{processing}} \text{Overlay/Analysis} \xrightarrow{\text{cartographic output}} \text{Final Map}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Watch:** Any question on "why metadata matters" is a direct hook to data quality — the two are inseparable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Data Quality Element

Every quality element in ISO 19157 has **three sub-components**:

1. **Measure** — the statistical test applied (e.g., RMSE).
2. **Data Quality Measure** — the numeric outcome.
3. **Evaluation Procedure** — how the measure was operationalized (sample size, sampling method, reference data source).

## 2.2 Positional Accuracy — The Heart of GIS Quality

### 2.2.1 Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)
The single most cited metric in KTU board examinations for positional accuracy:

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(x_i - x_{t,i})^{2} + (y_i - y_{t,i})^{2}\right]}$$

where $(x_i, y_i)$ is the measured coordinate and $(x_{t,i}, y_{t,i})$ is the true/reference coordinate at checkpoint $i$.

### 2.2.2 Circular Error 90 (CE90) & Linear Error 90 (LE90)
Used by **NSSDA (National Standard for Spatial Data Accuracy)**:

$$\text{CE90} \approx 1.5177 \times \text{RMSE}_r, \quad \text{LE90} \approx 1.6449 \times \text{RMSE}_x \text{ or } \text{RMSE}_y$$

The **multiplier 1.5177** comes from the Rayleigh distribution — a 90% confidence circle for a 2-D bivariate normal error distribution.

### 2.2.3 Positional Accuracy Components (Bullet Logic)
- **Absolute accuracy** — closeness to a recognized geodetic datum (e.g., WGS84).
- **Relative accuracy** — closeness of features to one another.
- **Gridded accuracy** — how well a raster cell value matches ground truth.
- **Bias** — systematic shift; corrected by georeferencing/transformation.
- **Random error** — uncorrectable noise; modeled probabilistically.

## 2.3 Thematic (Attribute) Accuracy

### 2.3.1 Confusion Matrix & Kappa
A **confusion matrix** cross-tabulates classified vs. reference labels:

|                  | Ref: Class A | Ref: Class B | Ref: Class C |
|------------------|:------------:|:------------:|:------------:|
| **Classified A** | $n_{11}$     | $n_{12}$     | $n_{13}$     |
| **Classified B** | $n_{21}$     | $n_{22}$     | $n_{23}$     |
| **Classified C** | $n_{31}$     | $n_{32}$     | $n_{33}$     |

The **Kappa coefficient** ($\hat{\kappa}$) corrects for chance agreement:

$$\hat{\kappa} = \frac{p_o - p_c}{1 - p_c}$$

where $p_o = \dfrac{\sum_{i} n_{ii}}{N}$ is observed agreement and $p_c = \sum_{i}\left(\frac{n_{i+}\,n_{+i}}{N^{2}}\right)$ is expected-by-chance agreement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation scale:** $\kappa \ge 0.81$ = *almost perfect*; $0.61 - 0.80$ = *substantial*; $0.41 - 0.60$ = *moderate*; below 0.40 is *poor* (Landis & Koch, 1977).

## 2.4 Completeness

Two ratios are evaluated:

$$\text{Commission Rate} = \frac{\text{Extra features present}}{\text{Reference features expected}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Omission Rate} = \frac{\text{Missing features}}{\text{Reference features expected}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.5 Temporal Accuracy

Measured as the **time lag** between ground reality and dataset vintage. A crucial metric for flood-mapping, land-use change, and disaster response layers.

## 2.6 Logical Consistency

Tested by **topological rules** in GIS software:

- Must-not-overlap (polygons)
- Must-not-have-gaps (polygons)
- Must-be-covered-by (lines within polygon)
- Endpoint-must-be-covered-by (connectivity)

## 2.7 Error Propagation — The Multiplier Effect

When two layers are overlaid, errors compound **non-linearly**. For independent layers with variances $\sigma_{1}^{2}$ and $\sigma_{2}^{2}$:

$$\sigma_{\text{result}}^{2} = \sigma_{1}^{2} + \sigma_{2}^{2} \quad \text{(variance adds)}$$

For products, variances add through partial derivatives (Taylor expansion):

$$\sigma_{f}^{2} \approx \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right)^{2}\sigma_{x}^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right)^{2}\sigma_{y}^{2}$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Use Case | Key Constant | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum d_i^{2}}{n}}$ | Positional accuracy | n = checkpoints | metres |
| $\text{CE90} = 1.5177 \times \text{RMSE}_r$ | 90% confidence circle | **1.5177** | metres |
| $\text{LE90} = 1.6449 \times \text{RMSE}_x$ | 90% confidence linear | **1.6449** | metres |
| $\hat{\kappa} = \dfrac{p_o - p_c}{1 - p_c}$ | Attribute agreement | None | dimensionless |
| $\sigma_{\text{prop}}^{2} = \sigma_{1}^{2} + \sigma_{2}^{2}$ | Error addition | Independence | $(\text{unit})^{2}$ |
| $\text{Omission \%} = \dfrac{\text{missing}}{\text{expected}} \times 100$ | Completeness | None | percent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **civil engineering** (road alignment, corridor mapping), positional accuracy of $\le$ **0.5 m RMSE** is the KTU/BIS benchmark. In **environmental engineering** (wetland delineation), attribute accuracy of $\kappa \ge 0.80$ is the working threshold.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The RMSE Formula from First Principles

**Starting premise:** A single positional discrepancy $d_i$ is the Euclidean distance between measured $(x_i, y_i)$ and true $(x_{t,i}, y_{t,i})$ positions.

**Step 1 — Express the squared discrepancy in 2-D:**

$$d_i^{2} = (x_i - x_{t,i})^{2} + (y_i - y_{t,i})^{2}$$

This follows directly from the Pythagorean theorem applied to the horizontal ground plane.

**Step 2 — Sum over $n$ independent checkpoints:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(x_i - x_{t,i})^{2} + (y_i - y_{t,i})^{2}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Take the arithmetic mean (expected squared error):**

$$\overline{d^{2}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(x_i - x_{t,i})^{2} + (y_i - y_{t,i})^{2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root to recover a length scale (meters):**

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(x_i - x_{t,i})^{2} + (y_i - y_{t,i})^{2}\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why square, then root?** Because raw errors can be positive or negative and would cancel. Squaring eliminates sign; the root restores units. The RMSE has the same units as the input data — typically **metres** in GIS.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The CE90 Multiplier (1.5177)

**Step 1 — Model the 2-D positional error as a bivariate normal:**

$$f(r) = \frac{r}{\sigma^{2}} e^{-r^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})}, \quad r \ge 0$$

This is the **Rayleigh distribution**, governing the radial distance $r$ from the true position when X and Y errors are independent, identically distributed Gaussians with $\sigma = \sigma_x = \sigma_y$.

**Step 2 — Set the cumulative probability at 0.90 and solve for $r$:**

$$P(R \le r) = 1 - e^{-r^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})} = 0.90$$

**Step 3 — Invert:**

$$e^{-r^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})} = 0.10 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{r^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} = \ln 10$$

$$r^{2} = 2\sigma^{2} \ln 10 \;\Rightarrow\; r = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln 10}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerically:**

$$r = \sigma \times \sqrt{2 \times 2.302585} = \sigma \times \sqrt{4.60517} = 2.1460\,\sigma$$

**Step 5 — Relate $\sigma$ to RMSE$_r$ (the radial RMSE).** For the Rayleigh model, $\text{RMSE}_r = \sigma\sqrt{2}$. Therefore:

$$\text{CE90} = 2.1460 \times \frac{\text{RMSE}_r}{\sqrt{2}} = 2.1460 / 1.4142 \times \text{RMSE}_r$$

$$\text{CE90} \approx 1.5177 \times \text{RMSE}_r$$

This explains the **1.5177** constant used by the NSSDA and the FGDC.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kappa Coefficient

**Step 1 — Observed agreement proportion:**

$$p_o = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_{ii}}{N}$$

This is the diagonal sum (correct classifications) divided by the total sample size $N$.

**Step 2 — Expected-by-chance agreement:** For row $i$ (classified as class $i$) and column $i$ (reference class $i$):

$$p_{c,i} = \frac{n_{i+} \cdot n_{+i}}{N^{2}}$$

where $n_{i+}$ is the row total and $n_{+i}$ is the column total.

**Step 3 — Sum over all $k$ classes:**

$$p_c = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_{i+} \cdot n_{+i}}{N^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by the maximum possible agreement (which is 1):**

$$\hat{\kappa} = \frac{p_o - p_c}{1 - p_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the denominator. Kappa = 0 means *chance-level* agreement, **not** zero agreement. A naïve "percent correct" can be 80% on a 5-class problem purely by chance; Kappa exposes this.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Error Propagation in Map Overlay

Suppose two raster layers $A$ and $B$ are multiplied to form $f = A \times B$. Using the **first-order Taylor expansion** of $f$ about the means:

$$f \approx f(\bar{A}, \bar{B}) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial A}(A - \bar{A}) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial B}(B - \bar{B})$$

Squaring and taking expectations, assuming $\text{Cov}(A,B) = 0$:

$$\sigma_{f}^{2} = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial A}\right)^{2}\sigma_{A}^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial B}\right)^{2}\sigma_{B}^{2}$$

For $f = A \times B$, $\partial f / \partial A = B$ and $\partial f / \partial B = A$:

$$\sigma_{f}^{2} = B^{2}\sigma_{A}^{2} + A^{2}\sigma_{B}^{2}$$

Dividing by $f^{2}$ to get the **relative variance**:

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_{f}}{f}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{\sigma_{A}}{A}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\sigma_{B}}{B}\right)^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Relative variances **add** for products, just like they do for sums. This is why cascading GIS analyses must be planned with care — a "harmless" classification accuracy of 85% applied four times in series yields a compound accuracy of $0.85^{4} \approx 0.52$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

A topographic layer is to be tested against 8 ground-control points. Discrepancies (in metres) in $X$ and $Y$ are tabulated:

| Point | $\Delta x$ | $\Delta y$ |
|:-----:|:----------:|:----------:|
| 1 | 0.4 | -0.3 |
| 2 | -0.6 | 0.5 |
| 3 | 0.2 | 0.7 |
| 4 | -0.3 | -0.2 |
| 5 | 0.5 | 0.4 |
| 6 | -0.4 | 0.6 |
| 7 | 0.3 | -0.5 |
| 8 | -0.2 | 0.3 |

**Step 1 — Compute squared discrepancies for each point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{1}^{2} &= 0.4^{2} + (-0.3)^{2} = 0.16 + 0.09 = 0.25 \\
d_{2}^{2} &= (-0.6)^{2} + 0.5^{2} = 0.36 + 0.25 = 0.61 \\
d_{3}^{2} &= 0.2^{2} + 0.7^{2} = 0.04 + 0.49 = 0.53 \\
d_{4}^{2} &= (-0.3)^{2} + (-0.2)^{2} = 0.09 + 0.04 = 0.13 \\
d_{5}^{2} &= 0.5^{2} + 0.4^{2} = 0.25 + 0.16 = 0.41 \\
d_{6}^{2} &= (-0.4)^{2} + 0.6^{2} = 0.16 + 0.36 = 0.52 \\
d_{7}^{2} = 0.3^{2} + (-0.5)^{2} &= 0.09 + 0.25 = 0.34 \\
d_{8}^{2} = (-0.2)^{2} + 0.3^{2} &= 0.04 + 0.09 = 0.13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum and divide by $n = 8$:**

$$\sum d_i^{2} = 0.25 + 0.61 + 0.53 + 0.13 + 0.41 + 0.52 + 0.34 + 0.13 = 2.92$$

$$\frac{\sum d_i^{2}}{n} = \frac{2.92}{8} = 0.365$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root:**

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{0.365} \approx 0.604 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to CE90 (NSSDA standard):**

$$\text{CE90} = 1.5177 \times 0.604 \approx 0.917 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** With 90% confidence, every point in this layer is within **≈ 0.92 m** of its true ground location. For 1:10 000 scale mapping, this satisfies the typical KTU/BIS threshold of $\le$ 1.0 m.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five Elements of Data Quality — Architecture

The following block diagram traces the **input-to-output** quality pipeline in a GIS workflow, showing how each ISO 19157 quality element is sampled, measured, and reported.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spatial Data Source] --> B[Ingestion Module]
    B --> C{Quality Test Suite}
    C --> D[Completeness Check]
    C --> E[Logical Consistency Check]
    C --> F[Positional Accuracy Check]
    C --> G[Thematic Accuracy Check]
    C --> H[Temporal Accuracy Check]
    D --> I[Quality Report Generator]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Metadata ISO 19115]
    I --> K[QA QC Dashboard]
    J --> L[End User Map Product]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Fitness for Use Decision]
```

## 4.2 Error Propagation Through a GIS Workflow

The diagram below shows how a single positional error **cascades** through successive overlay operations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Layer A] -->|sigma A| C[Overlay Operation]
    B[Source Layer B] -->|sigma B| C
    C -->|sigma C squared equals sigma A squared plus sigma B squared| D[Derived Layer C]
    D -->|sigma D squared equals f partial squared sigma C squared| E[Final Map Product]
    E --> F[Cartographic Generalisation]
    F --> G[Published Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the edge labels** as the error-propagation equation. Each stage adds a transformation that potentially enlarges the variance of the output layer.

## 4.3 The Metadata-Data Quality Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Dataset] --> B[Lineage]
    A --> C[Positional Accuracy]
    A --> D[Attribute Accuracy]
    A --> E[Logical Consistency]
    A --> F[Temporal Validity]
    A --> G[Completeness]
    B --> H[ISO 19115 Metadata Record]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Clearinghouse Discovery]
    I --> J[End User]
```

## 4.4 Sampling Strategy for QA/QC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Universe of Features] --> B{Choose Sampling Method}
    B -->|Random| C[Simple Random Sampling]
    B -->|Stratified| D[Stratified by Land Cover]
    B -->|Systematic| E[Every Nth Feature]
    C --> F[Collect Independent Reference Data]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute RMSE, Kappa, Omission]
    G --> H{Acceptance Threshold Met?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Pass: Issue Metadata]
    H -->|No| J[Fail: Reject or Remediate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "design a QA/QC protocol", draw a flowchart of this style. Examiners reward visual structure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse *accuracy* (closeness to truth) with *precision* (closeness of repeats). Marks are routinely lost on this 2-marker.
> 2. The Kappa coefficient needs the denominator $(1 - p_c)$. Computing $p_o$ alone and calling it "Kappa" is the most common mistake.
> 3. For CE90, the **multiplier is 1.5177**, not 1.96 (which is the Gaussian z-score for 95% — a different statistic).
> 4. ISO 19157 is the **data quality** standard; ISO 19115 is the **metadata** standard. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Distinguish between positional accuracy and attribute accuracy with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Positional accuracy** measures how closely a feature's coordinates match its true ground location. Example: The recorded location of a river bend is within 1.0 m (RMSE) of its GPS-surveyed position.
- **Attribute accuracy** (or thematic accuracy) measures how correctly the non-spatial descriptors of a feature are recorded. Example: A land-use polygon labelled "paddy" is actually "paddy" in 90 of 100 ground-truth checks.
- Positional accuracy deals with **geometry**; attribute accuracy deals with **semantics**. The two are independent — a feature can be geometrically perfect yet mislabelled.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the five data quality elements specified in ISO 19157.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Completeness** — presence or absence of features, attributes, or relationships.
2. **Logical consistency** — degree of adherence to logical rules of structure, attribution, and relationships.
3. **Positional accuracy** — closeness of position information to true position.
4. **Thematic accuracy** — closeness of quantitative or non-quantitative attributes to true values.
5. **Temporal accuracy** — closeness of temporal references and validity periods to ground truth.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (ESE Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern:** Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Define **RMSE** and **CE90** as positional-accuracy metrics. For a survey dataset, the squared discrepancies at 10 checkpoints are: `0.25, 0.49, 0.16, 0.64, 0.36, 0.81, 0.09, 0.25, 0.16, 0.04` m². Compute the RMSE and CE90, and comment on its fitness for a 1:10,000-scale map.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Kappa coefficient** for thematic accuracy. A classified land-cover map yields the following confusion matrix (rows = classified, columns = reference; total $N = 200$). Compute Kappa and interpret.

|                  | Forest | Water | Urban |
|------------------|:------:|:-----:|:-----:|
| **Forest**       | 80     | 8     | 4     |
| **Water**        | 6      | 45    | 3     |
| **Urban**        | 4      | 7     | 43    |

---

### ⭐ Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) Solution [7 marks]

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **RMSE (Root Mean Square Error):** the square root of the mean of squared positional discrepancies between measured and true coordinates. It is the standard 2-D positional accuracy metric.
- **CE90 (Circular Error 90%):** the radius of a circle, centred on the true position, within which 90% of measurements fall. Computed as $\text{CE90} = 1.5177 \times \text{RMSE}$.

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

$$\sum d_i^{2} = 0.25 + 0.49 + 0.16 + 0.64 + 0.36 + 0.81 + 0.09 + 0.25 + 0.16 + 0.04 = 3.25 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{3.25}{10}} = \sqrt{0.325} \approx 0.5702 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{CE90} = 1.5177 \times 0.5702 \approx 0.866 \text{ m}$$

**Fitness Comment (1 mark):**
The 1:10,000-scale standard (NSSDA / BIS) requires positional accuracy of $\le$ **1.0 m CE90**. The computed **0.866 m CE90** falls within tolerance, so the dataset **passes** the threshold and is fit for 1:10,000 mapping.

#### (b) Solution [7 marks]

**Kappa Explanation (2 marks):**
Kappa is a chance-corrected measure of agreement between classified and reference datasets. Values range from $-1$ to $+1$; zero indicates chance-level agreement; positive values indicate agreement better than chance.

**Compute row/column totals (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{1+} &= 80 + 8 + 4 = 92, \quad n_{2+} = 6 + 45 + 3 = 54, \quad n_{3+} = 4 + 7 + 43 = 54 \\
n_{+1} &= 80 + 6 + 4 = 90, \quad n_{+2} = 8 + 45 + 7 = 60, \quad n_{+3} = 4 + 3 + 43 = 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Observed agreement (1 mark):**

$$p_o = \frac{80 + 45 + 43}{200} = \frac{168}{200} = 0.840$$

**Expected agreement (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_c &= \frac{92 \times 90}{200^{2}} + \frac{54 \times 60}{200^{2}} + \frac{54 \times 50}{200^{2}} \\
&= \frac{8280 + 3240 + 2700}{40000} \\
&= \frac{14220}{40000} = 0.3555
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute Kappa (0.5 mark):**

$$\hat{\kappa} = \frac{0.840 - 0.3555}{1 - 0.3555} = \frac{0.4845}{0.6445} \approx 0.752$$

**Interpretation (0.5 mark):**
By Landis & Koch (1977), $\kappa = 0.752$ indicates **substantial agreement** — fit for most operational land-cover mapping tasks.

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the concepts of **accuracy, precision, bias, and error propagation** in GIS data quality. How do these concepts interact during a multi-layer overlay analysis?

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the structure of a **data quality report** as mandated by ISO 19157. Differentiate between **ISO 19157** (data quality) and **ISO 19115** (metadata) standards.

---

### ⭐ Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) Solution [7 marks]

**Concept Definitions (3 marks):**
- **Accuracy** — closeness to the true value; reduced by systematic **bias**.
- **Precision** — repeatability of measurements; reduced by **random error**.
- **Bias** — systematic offset; corrected by calibration, georeferencing, and datum transformations.
- **Error propagation** — the manner in which input uncertainties cascade through analytical operations to influence the uncertainty of the output.

**Interaction in Overlay Analysis (3 marks):**
When two layers $A$ and $B$ are overlaid, the output variance follows:

$$\sigma_{f}^{2} = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_A}\right)^{2}\sigma_A^{2} + \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_B}\right)^{2}\sigma_B^{2} + 2\,\text{Cov}(A,B)\,\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_A}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_B}$$

For independent layers, $\text{Cov}(A,B) = 0$, and variances **add**. Bias in either layer produces a biased output; precision in each layer contributes to the output's precision.

**Practical Illustration (1 mark):**
Suppose Layer A has a 5 m horizontal bias and Layer B has 0.5 m RMSE. The output map inherits a 5 m shift in all features lying on A, plus a positional noise of $\sqrt{0.5^{2} + \sigma_B^{2}}$. Therefore, **a single biased layer contaminates the entire product**, no matter how precise the other layers are.

#### (b) Solution [7 marks]

**ISO 19157 Data Quality Report Structure (3 marks):**
A report contains:
- **Scope** — extent of the dataset being evaluated.
- **Data quality measures** — RMSE, CE90, Kappa, completeness ratios.
- **Evaluation method** — sampling design, reference data source, sample size $n$.
- **Data quality results** — the numeric values of the measures.
- **Data quality conformance** — pass/fail vs. an acceptability threshold.

**ISO 19157 vs ISO 19115 (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ISO 19157 | ISO 19115 |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Data quality measures | Metadata structure |
| **Outputs** | Quantitative accuracy stats | Descriptive records |
| **Mandatory?** | Quality elements + measures | Core metadata elements |
| **Sampling design** | Required | Not required |
| **Use case** | QA/QC certification | Discovery & documentation |

**Practical Linkage (1 mark):**
In a published dataset, the ISO 19115 metadata record *cites* the ISO 19157 data quality report. They are two halves of the same deliverable: **what** the data is (19115) and **how trustworthy** it is (19157).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Data quality is **not perfection** — it is **bounded uncertainty**.
- The five ISO 19157 quality elements: **Completeness, Logical Consistency, Positional Accuracy, Thematic Accuracy, Temporal Accuracy**.
- **Accuracy ≠ Precision**: accuracy is closeness to truth; precision is repeatability.
- **RMSE** = $\sqrt{\sum d_i^{2}/n}$ is the workhorse positional-accuracy metric.
- **CE90 = 1.5177 × RMSE$_r$** (Rayleigh distribution, 90% confidence).
- **LE90 = 1.6449 × RMSE$_x$ or RMSE$_y$** (one-dimensional equivalent).
- **Kappa** corrects the confusion matrix for chance agreement; $> 0.80$ is "almost perfect".
- **Error propagation**: variances **add** for sums; relative variances **add** for products.
- **Bias** is systematic and fixable; **random error** is not.
- **ISO 19157** = data quality; **ISO 19115** = metadata — don't mix them in exams.
- **NSSDA** (USA) and **BIS IS 16439** (India) are the operational standards used for positional-accuracy certification.
- **Sampling strategy** must be declared: simple random, stratified, or systematic — not arbitrary.
- **Metadata is the carrier** of data quality information; without metadata, quality is unverified.
- For **1:10,000 scale** maps, target $\text{CE90} \le 1.0$ m and $\kappa \ge 0.80$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Preparation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET414 - CARTOGRAPHY AND GIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Preparation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Preparation** in a Geographic Information System (GIS) is the comprehensive, multi-stage process of collecting, capturing, cleaning, transforming, integrating, and structuring spatial (geometric) and non-spatial (attribute) data from heterogeneous sources into a unified, topologically clean, and georeferenced digital database that is fit for analytical and cartographic use.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Data Preparation forms the **most time-consuming phase of any GIS project** — industry studies and KTU reference material consistently indicate that **60% to 80%** of the total project effort is spent on data preparation, even though it is rarely visible in the final map product.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Kitchen Analogy" for GIS Data Preparation 🍳**
>
> Imagine you are a chef preparing a multi-course meal. Before you can cook:
> 1. You must **source** raw ingredients (vegetables, spices, meat) — this is **Data Acquisition**.
> 2. You must **wash, peel, and chop** them — this is **Data Cleaning & Preprocessing**.
> 3. You must **measure** them into proper quantities — this is **Georeferencing & Projection**.
> 4. You must **marinate** some ingredients together to blend flavors — this is **Data Integration**.
> 5. Finally, you arrange everything neatly on a shelf in labeled containers — this is **Database Creation & Topology Building**.
>
> Skipping or rushing any of these steps guarantees a bad dish — just as skipping data preparation guarantees a flawed GIS analysis.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Data Preparation

The following **bolded metrics** are universally used in the GIS industry and are examinable:

- **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** — measures positional accuracy after georeferencing
- **Map Scale (1 : N)** — defines the level of spatial detail
- **Spatial Resolution** — pixel size for raster data (e.g., **30 m** for Landsat, **0.3 m** for WorldView-3)
- **National Map Accuracy Standard (NMAS)** — **90%** of well-defined points must fall within a tolerance
- **DPI (Dots Per Inch)** — for scanned map resolution (typically **300–600 DPI** for digitization)
- **Topological Tolerance (Fuzzy Tolerance)** — usually expressed in **map units** (e.g., 0.001 m)
- **Decimal Degrees** (e.g., 76.27°E) vs **DMS** (76°16'12"E) for coordinate representation

## 1.4 Primary vs Secondary Data Sources

| Source Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Collected directly in the field by the user | GPS survey, Total Station, Drone imagery |
| **Secondary** | Acquired from existing maps, agencies, or archives | Survey of India (SoI) toposheets, Census data, Bhuvan, OpenStreetMap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between *primary* and *secondary* sources and the **legal/ethical implications** of using copyrighted secondary datasets without proper licensing.

## 1.5 Data Types in GIS

| Data Type | Geometric Model | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Vector** | Points, Lines, Polygons | Wells, Roads, Parcels |
| **Raster** | Grid of cells (pixels) | Satellite imagery, DEM, scanned maps |
| **Attribute** | Tabular records linked to geometry | Population, soil type, road name |
| **Metadata** | "Data about data" | Source, date, scale, accuracy, projection |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Sequential Stages of Data Preparation

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme groups Data Preparation into **five sequential stages**. Memorize this flow — it is a guaranteed 7-mark question.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition
- Identify the project requirement (scale, extent, themes needed).
- Locate **primary sources** (field GPS, Total Station, drone) and **secondary sources** (SoI, Bhuvan, USGS Earth Explorer, OpenStreetMap).
- Verify licensing and data ownership.

### Stage 2 — Data Input (Digitization)
Three sub-methods:
1. **Manual Digitization** — using a mouse/tablet on-screen over a scanned raster.
2. **Heads-up Digitization** — tracing features directly on a georeferenced image.
3. **Automatic Vectorization** — software (e.g., ArcScan) auto-traces raster lines into vectors.

### Stage 3 — Data Preprocessing
- **Georeferencing** — assigning real-world coordinates to a scanned map.
- **Reprojection** — converting coordinates from one datum/projection to another.
- **Reformatting** — converting shapefile to geodatabase, GeoTIFF to IMG, etc.
- **Edge-matching** — aligning features that cross map sheet boundaries.
- **Mosaicking** — stitching multiple raster tiles into a single seamless image.

### Stage 4 — Data Cleaning & Topology Building
- **Overshoots** — line segments that extend past their intersection.
- **Undershoots** — line segments that fall short of their intersection.
- **Slivers / Gaps** — small spurious polygons between adjacent boundaries.
- **Duplicate geometry** — overlapping or coincident features.
- **Topology rules** in software enforce: *must not overlap, must not have gaps, must not have dangles, must be covered by boundary of*.

### Stage 5 — Data Integration & Database Creation
- Link attribute tables to spatial features via **unique IDs**.
- Build **relational database** structure (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many).
- Document everything in **metadata** (FGDC or ISO 19115 standard).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Every formula here has appeared in KTU previous-year question papers for Cartography and GIS electives.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Map Scale | $S = \dfrac{D_{\text{map}}}{D_{\text{ground}}} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | $N$ = scale denominator (unitless) | Determines minimum legible feature size |
| 2 | Ground Sample Distance (GSD) | $\text{GSD} = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$ | $H$ = flying height (m), $p$ = pixel pitch (μm), $f$ = focal length (mm) | Raster spatial resolution |
| 3 | Affine Transformation (X) | $X' = aX + bY + c$ | $a,b,c$ = coefficients, $X,Y$ = source coords | Georeferencing |
| 4 | Affine Transformation (Y) | $Y' = dX + eY + f$ | $d,e,f$ = coefficients, $X',Y'$ = target coords | Georeferencing |
| 5 | Rotation | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ | $a,b$ = from affine matrix | Map rotation angle |
| 6 | Scale (X-axis) | $S_x = \sqrt{a^{2} + b^{2}}$ | $S_x$ = scale factor along X | Scale change |
| 7 | Scale (Y-axis) | $S_y = \sqrt{d^{2} + e^{2}}$ | $S_y$ = scale factor along Y | Scale change |
| 8 | RMSE per control point | $\text{RMSE}_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x'_i)^{2} + (y_i - y'_i)^{2}}$ | $(x_i,y_i)$ source, $(x'_i,y'_i)$ target, units = map units | Positional accuracy |
| 9 | Total RMSE (N points) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - x'_i)^{2} + (y_i - y'_i)^{2}}{N}}$ | $N$ = number of GCPs, must be $\geq 3$ | Overall fit quality |
| 10 | Skew | $\text{Skew} = \dfrac{\arctan(b/a) - \arctan(d/e)}{\pi/2}$ | $a,b,d,e$ from affine matrix | Shear detection |
| 11 | Decimal Degrees to DMS | $\text{DMS} = D + \dfrac{M}{60} + \dfrac{S}{3600}$ | $D$ = degrees, $M$ = minutes, $S$ = seconds | Coordinate display |
| 12 | UTM Zone | $\text{Zone} = \left\lfloor\dfrac{\lambda + 180}{6}\right\rfloor + 1$ | $\lambda$ = longitude in degrees | UTM zone selection |
| 13 | Minimum Mapping Unit (MMU) | $\text{MMU} = S \cdot 0.5\,\text{mm}$ | $S$ = scale denominator | Smallest mappable polygon |
| 14 | Snapping Tolerance | $T_{s} \approx 0.5 \cdot \text{MMU}$ | $T_s$ = snap distance | Topology building |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planning** — preparing cadastral and land-use layers for master plans.
- **Transportation Engineering** — building clean road network topology for routing algorithms.
- **Environmental Engineering** — preparing DEMs and watershed boundaries for hydrological modeling.
- **Disaster Management** — integrating multi-sensor (drone + satellite + field) data for rapid flood mapping.
- **Utility Management** — building a topologically correct water-pipe network to compute pressure zones.

## 2.4 Coordinate Reference System (CRS) Concepts

> [!NOTE]
> **Three nested components of any CRS:**
> 1. **Datum** — defines the reference ellipsoid (e.g., WGS 1984, Everest 1956) and its position relative to Earth's centre.
> 2. **Projection** — mathematical transformation of the curved surface to a flat plane (e.g., UTM, Lambert Conformal Conic, Mercator).
> 3. **Coordinate System** — units and axis orientation (degrees vs metres, X-Easting vs Y-Northing).

A common KTU pitfall: storing data in **WGS 1984 Geographic (lat/lon, degrees)** but analysing it as if it were in **UTM (metres)** — this causes severe distance/area errors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Affine Transformation for Georeferencing

The **affine transformation** is the cornerstone of georeferencing a scanned paper map. It is an *order-1 polynomial* that preserves straight lines and parallel lines.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Two General Equations

A 2D affine transformation uses **six parameters** ($a, b, c, d, e, f$) to map every source pixel $(X, Y)$ to a target ground coordinate $(X', Y')$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X' &= aX + bY + c \\
Y' &= dX + eY + f
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Geometric Interpretation of Each Parameter

- $a$ — scale along the X-axis (combined with rotation).
- $b$ — rotation + skew contribution to X.
- $c$ — **translation** in X (i.e., where the origin moves to).
- $d$ — rotation + skew contribution to Y.
- $e$ — scale along the Y-axis (combined with rotation).
- $f$ — **translation** in Y.

### 3.1.3 Deriving the Rotation Angle

We extract rotation by examining only the *linear* part of the affine matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ d & e \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **pure rotation** (no scale, no skew), the matrix reduces to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{rot}} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing $A$ with $A_{\text{rot}}$ element-wise, we can solve for $\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta &= \dfrac{b}{a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Deriving the Scale Factor (X-axis)

The scale factor along X is the magnitude of the first column vector of $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_x &= \sqrt{a^{2} + b^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** a scale of $S_x$ along X means a unit vector along the original X-axis (1, 0) becomes a vector of length $S_x$ in image space — the components are $(a, d)$, so its length is $\sqrt{a^{2} + d^{2}}$. (For a non-skewed affine, $b$ and $d$ represent the same rotation, hence the alternate form $\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}$.)

Similarly, the Y-scale:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_y &= \sqrt{d^{2} + e^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Minimum Number of Control Points

Since we have **6 unknowns** ($a, b, c, d, e, f$), we need **at least 6 independent linear equations**. Each Ground Control Point (GCP) provides **2 equations** (one for $X'$, one for $Y'$). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\min} &= \dfrac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ GCPs}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, GIS software recommends **at least 4 GCPs** and ideally **6–10** to detect blunders and to compute RMSE meaningfully.

## 3.2 Derivation: Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) for Positional Accuracy

### 3.2.1 Per-Point Residual

For the $i$-th GCP, the **residual** is the Euclidean distance between the *transformed* point and the *actual* target point:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_i &= \sqrt{(X'_i - X^{\text{true}}_i)^{2} + (Y'_i - Y^{\text{true}}_i)^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Squaring to Remove the Square Root

We square the residual so that errors in different directions combine (instead of cancelling if the direction reverses):

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_i^{2} &= (X'_i - X^{\text{true}}_i)^{2} + (Y'_i - Y^{\text{true}}_i)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Sum of Squared Residuals

Add the squared residuals over all $N$ GCPs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ (X'_i - X^{\text{true}}_i)^{2} + (Y'_i - Y^{\text{true}}_i)^{2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Averaging and Taking the Square Root

Divide by $N$ to get the *mean squared error*, then take the square root to recover units of length (metres, not m²):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RMSE} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\Sigma}{N}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ (X'_i - X^{\text{true}}_i)^{2} + (Y'_i - Y^{\text{true}}_i)^{2} \right]}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** RMSE has the same unit as the input coordinates. An RMSE of **2.0 m** after georeferencing means that, on average, transformed points deviate from true positions by 2 metres.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This exact example type appears regularly in KTU 14-mark questions. Practise it.

**Problem:** Three GCPs are used in an affine georeferencing. Compute the total RMSE.

| GCP | Source $(X, Y)$ | Target $(X', Y')$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (10, 20) | (100, 200) |
| 2 | (50, 80) | (500, 800) |
| 3 | (90, 30) | (900, 300) |

(Assume the affine coefficients transform each source point exactly to its target — i.e., perfect fit for 3 GCPs, residual is **0**.)

### Step 1 — Per-Point Residuals

For GCP 1:
$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \sqrt{(100 - 100)^{2} + (200 - 200)^{2}} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

For GCP 2:
$$
\begin{aligned}
r_2 &= \sqrt{(500 - 500)^{2} + (800 - 800)^{2}} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

For GCP 3:
$$
\begin{aligned}
r_3 &= \sqrt{(900 - 900)^{2} + (300 - 300)^{2}} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Sum of Squared Residuals

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma &= 0^{2} + 0^{2} + 0^{2} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Total RMSE

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RMSE} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\Sigma}{N}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0}{3}} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — A More Realistic Case (Add Residuals)

If a *fourth* independent check point (independent validation point) had source (40, 60) and transformed output (395, 605) versus true (397, 603):

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_4 &= \sqrt{(395 - 397)^{2} + (605 - 603)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{(-2)^{2} + (2)^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{4 + 4} \\
&= \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This residual is reported separately as the **independent check-point error** — a more honest measure than in-sample RMSE.

## 3.4 Derivation: Ground Sample Distance (GSD) for Drone/Imagery Data

When acquiring raster data via drone or aerial photography:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GSD} &= \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $H$ = flying height above ground (m)
- $p$ = pixel pitch of the sensor (μm, convert to m: $p_{\text{m}} = p_{\mu\text{m}} \times 10^{-6}$)
- $f$ = focal length (mm, convert to m: $f_{\text{m}} = f_{\text{mm}} \times 10^{-3}$)

### Worked Example

Drone flying at $H = 120$ m, sensor pixel pitch $p = 2.4$ μm, focal length $f = 8.8$ mm.

Convert units:
$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= 2.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
f &= 8.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute GSD:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GSD} &= \dfrac{120 \cdot 2.4 \times 10^{-6}}{8.8 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \dfrac{2.88 \times 10^{-4}}{8.8 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 0.0327 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 3.27 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** each pixel represents roughly a **3.27 cm × 3.27 cm** area on the ground — extremely high resolution, suitable for cadastral-level mapping.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Preparation Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the full lifecycle of data preparation, showing inputs, processes, intermediate artefacts, and the final output database.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Primary Data Sources\nGPS Survey, Total Station, Drone]
    A2[Secondary Data Sources\nSoI Toposheets, Bhuvan, OSM, Census]
    A3[Existing Digital Layers\nShapefiles, GeoTIFFs, Tabular CSVs]

    B1[Stage 1: Data Acquisition\nIdentify, license, download]
    B2[Stage 2: Data Input\nManual Digitization\nHeads-up Digitization\nAuto-Vectorization\nGPS Import]
    B3[Stage 3: Preprocessing\nGeoreferencing\nReprojection\nEdge Matching\nMosaicking]
    B4[Stage 4: Cleaning and Topology\nFix Overshoots, Undershoots\nResolve Slivers\nApply Snapping Tolerance\nBuild Topology]
    B5[Stage 5: Integration and Database\nLink Attribute Tables\nCreate Relational Schema\nGenerate Metadata]

    C1[Quality Control\nCompute RMSE\nCheck Topology Rules\nValidate Schema]
    D1[Output: Clean GIS Database\nReady for Spatial Analysis\nand Cartographic Output]

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    C1 -.rejects.-> B2
```

## 4.2 Georeferencing Process (Detailed Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Scanned Paper Map\nUnreferenced Raster] --> P2[Add Control Points\nMinimum 3 to 4 GCPs]
    P2 --> P3[Choose Transformation\nAffine 1st Order\n2nd Order Polynomial\n3rd Order Polynomial]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Residuals\nand RMSE per GCP]
    P4 --> P5{RMSE within\nTolerance?}
    P5 -- No --> P6[Reposition or Remove\nBlunder GCPs]
    P6 --> P4
    P5 -- Yes --> P7[Apply Transformation\nRectify Image]
    P7 --> P8[Save as Georeferenced\nGeoTIFF]
    P8 --> P9[Define Coordinate\nReference System]
    P9 --> P10[Layer is ready for\nSpatial Analysis]
```

## 4.3 Data Integration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Vector Layer\nRoads, Rivers]
    L2[Raster Layer\nDEM, Satellite Image]
    L3[Tabular Attribute\nCensus, Land Records]
    L4[GPS Point Cloud\nField Survey]
    L5[Web Service Data\nWMS, WFS, APIs]

    M1[Spatial Join\nMatch by Location]
    M2[Attribute Join\nMatch by Unique ID]
    M3[Overlay Analysis\nIntersect, Union, Erase]
    M4[Format Conversion\nShapefile to GDB\nCSV to Table]
    M5[Coordinate Harmonization\nReproject all to\ncommon CRS]

    N1[Integrated Geo-Database\nFile Geodatabase or\nPostGIS Database]
    N2[Metadata Catalogue\nISO 19115 Standard]

    L1 --> M5
    L2 --> M5
    L3 --> M2
    L4 --> M5
    L5 --> M1
    M1 --> N1
    M2 --> N1
    M3 --> N1
    M4 --> N1
    M5 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
```

## 4.4 Topology Error Types (Visual Catalogue)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Err[Common Topology Errors]
    E1[Overshoot\nLine extends past\nintersection point]
    E2[Undershoot\nLine falls short\nof intersection]
    E3[Sliver Polygon\nSpurious thin polygon\nalong shared boundary]
    E4[Duplicate Geometry\nTwo coincident\nfeatures]
    E5[Gap\nMissing polygon\nbetween neighbours]
    E6[Dangle\nLine ends in\nopen space]
    E7[Self-Intersection\nPolygon crosses itself]
    end

    Fix[Apply: Trim, Extend, Snap,\nMerge, Eliminate, Rebuild]
    E1 --> Fix
    E2 --> Fix
    E3 --> Fix
    E4 --> Fix
    E5 --> Fix
    E6 --> Fix
    E7 --> Fix
```
